For example, in Eastern Japan there are still many Hakusan shrines where the shrine itself is called either ''gongen'' or ''jinja''<ref name="sos3"/>. Because it represents the application of Buddhist terminology to Shinto ''kami'', the use of the term was legally abolished by the Meiji government with the {{nihongo|[[Shinbutsu bunri|Shinto and Buddhism Separation Order]]|神仏判然令|Shin-butsu Hanzenrei}}, and shrines began to be called ''jinja''<ref name="sos3"/>.
=== Gongen of Japan === * {{nihongo|Izuna Gongen|飯網の権現}} — Also called Izuna [[Myōjin]] and enshrined in Izuna Shrine in Nagano, it is similar to a ''tengu''.<ref>Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=193 Izuna Gongen], accessed on October 6, 2008</ref> * {{nihongo|Izusan Gongen|伊豆山権現}} or {{nihongo|Hashiri-yu Gongen|走湯権現}} — It's the spirit of a hot spring on Izusan, a hill in Shizuoka Prefecture<ref>Jaanus, ''Izusan Gongen'', accessed on October 6, 2008.</ref> * {{nihongo|Kumano Gongen|熊野権現}} — Also known as {{nihongo|[[Kumano Sanzan]]|熊野三山}}<ref name="kumano">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1076 Kumano Shinkō], accessed on October 6, 2008</ref><ref>[http://www.sekaiisan-wakayama.jp/english/s_sanzan.htm Kumano Sanzan], World Heritage Registration Association, accessed on October 13, 2008</ref>. The ''kami'' enshrined in the three Kumano Sanzan Grand Shrines are the three Kumano mountains: Hongū, Shingū, and Nachi<ref name="kumano"/>. * {{nihongo|Seiryū Gongen|清滝権現}} — Enshrined in Jingo-ji in Takao as tutelary ''kami'' of the [[Shingon Buddhism|Shingon sect]] by [[Kūkai]].<ref>Jaanus, ''Seiryū Gongen'', accessed on October 6, 2008</ref> * Tōshō Daigongen — One of the most famous examples of ''gongen'' is the already mentioned {{nihongo|Tōshō Daigongen|東照大権現}}, or Tokugawa Ieyasu, posthumously enshrined in so-called [[Tōshō-gū]] shrines present all over Japan. The original one is [[Nikkō Tōshō-gū]] in [[Nikkō]]. * {{nihongo|Zaō Gongen|蔵王権現}} or {{nihongo|Kongō Zaō Bosatsu|金剛蔵王菩薩}} — A deity worshiped only by Japan's [[Shugendō]] sect.<ref>Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=201 Zaō Gongen], accessed on October 6, 2008</ref>
== ''Gongen-zukuri'' == [[Image:Gongen Zukuri.png|thumb|The blueprint of a gongen-zukuri shrine]] {{main|Ishi-no-ma-zukuri}} {{nihongo|''Gongen-zukuri''|権現造}} is the name of a complex [[Shinto shrine]] structure in which the ''[[haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'', or worship hall, and the [[honden]], or main sanctuary, are interconnected under the same roof in the shape of an H<ref name="sos2">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_g.html#gongen-zukuri ''Gongen-zukuri''] accessed on October 5, 2008</ref><ref>For details about these terms, see the article [[Shinto shrine]].</ref>. One of the oldest examples of ''gongen-zukuri'' is Kitano Tenmangū in [[Kyoto]]<ref name="sos2"/>. The name comes from Nikkō Tōshō-gū in Nikkō because, as we have seen, it enshrines the Tōshō Daigongen and adopts this structure<ref name="Jaa">Jaanus, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/g/gongenzukuri.htm Gongen-zukuri], accessed on October 5, 2008</ref>.
== See also == * The [[Glossary of Shinto]] for an explanation of terms concerning Japanese Shinto, Shinto art, and Shinto shrine architecture.
==Notes==
{{Reflist|2}}
== References == *{{cite book |last=Tamura |first=Yoshiro |title=Japanese Buddhism — A Cultural History |publisher=Kosei Publishing Company |location=Tokyo|date=2000 |edition=First Edition |pages=232 pages |isbn=4-333-01684-3 |language= }} * {{cite book | last = John Breen | first = Mark Teuween (editors) | coauthors = | title = Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami | publisher = University of Hawaii Press | date = July 2000 | location = Honolulu | language = | isbn = 978-0824823634 | oclc = 43487317 }} [[Category:Shinto]] [[Category:Japanese religions terms]] [[Category:Buddhism in Japan]]
[[ja:権現]] [[fi:Gongen]]

[[Image:Gate-nikko-japan.jpg|thumb|250px|Nikkō Tōshō-gū enshrines Tokugawa Ieyasu under the posthumous name of Tōshō Daigongen]] {{nihongo|'''Gongen'''|権現}} in Japanese [[Buddhism|Buddhist]] and [[Shinto]] terminology is an [[avatar]], a Japanese ''[[kami]]'' which is really a temporary manifestation of a [[Buddhahood|buddha]] come from India to guide the Japanese to salvation<ref name="SoS">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_g.html#gongen ''Gongen''] accessed on October 5, 2008</ref><ref name="TY87">Tamura (2000:87)</ref>. The words {{nihongo|''gonge''|権化}} and {{nihongo|''kegen''|化現}} are [[synonym]]s<ref name="sos3"/>. {{nihongo|''Gongen shinkō''|権現信仰}} is the belief in the existence of ''gongen''<ref name="sos3"/>.
== History == [[Image:NikkoToriiTablet5127.jpg|left|thumb|Tablet on ''torii'' at Nikkō Tōshō-gū reads "Tōshō Daigongen" (calligraphy by [[Emperor Go-Mizunoo]])]]It is sometimes assumed that the word ''gongen'' derives from [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]]'s posthumous name (Tōshō Daigongen). The term however was created and started being used in the middle of the [[Heian period]] in an effort to harmonize Buddhism and Shinto in what is called [[shinbutsu shūgō]], or "[[syncretism]] of ''kami'' and buddhas"<ref name="TY87">Tamura (2000:87)</ref>. At that time, the assumption that Japanese ''kami'' and buddhas were essentially the same evolved into a theory called {{nihongo|''[[honji suijaku]]''|本地垂迹}} which held that ''kami'' were manifestations (''avatars'') of buddhas, [[bodhisattva]]s and other Buddhist deities<ref name="TY87"/>. As the theory gradually spread around the country, the concept of ''gongen'' evolved. As a result, under the influence of [[Tendai|Tendai Buddhism]] and [[Shugendō]], the ''gongen'' concept was adapted for example to religious beliefs tied to [[Mount Iwaki]], a volcano, so that female ''kami'' Kuniyasutamahime became associated with Jūichimen [[Kannon Bosatsu]] (eleven-faced Kannon), ''kami'' [[Ōkuninushi]] with [[Yakushi Nyorai]], and [[Kunitokotachi]] no Mikoto with [[Amida Nyorai]]<ref>Breen, Teuween (2000:194)</ref>.
The title ''gongen'' started being attached to the names of Shinto ''kami'' and shrines were built within the premises of large Buddhist temples to enshrine their tutelary ''kami''<ref name="TY87">Tamura (2000:87)</ref>. During the Japanese Middle Ages, shrines started being called with the name ''gongen''<ref name="sos3">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=790 Gongen shinkō], accessed on October 5, 2008</ref>. For example, in Eastern Japan there are still many Hakusan shrines where the shrine itself is called either ''gongen'' or ''jinja''<ref name="sos3"/>. Because it represents the application of Buddhist terminology to Shinto ''kami'', the use of the term was legally abolished by the Meiji government with the {{nihongo|[[Shinbutsu bunri|Shinto and Buddhism Separation Order]]|神仏判然令|Shin-butsu Hanzenrei}}, and shrines began to be called ''jinja''<ref name="sos3"/>.
=== Gongen of Japan === * {{nihongo|Izuna Gongen|飯網の権現}} — Also called Izuna [[Myōjin]] and enshrined in Izuna Shrine in Nagano, it is similar to a ''tengu''.<ref>Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=193 Izuna Gongen], accessed on October 6, 2008</ref> * {{nihongo|Izusan Gongen|伊豆山権現}} or {{nihongo|Hashiri-yu Gongen|走湯権現}} — It's the spirit of a hot spring on Izusan, a hill in Shizuoka Prefecture<ref>Jaanus, ''Izusan Gongen'', accessed on October 6, 2008.</ref> * {{nihongo|Kumano Gongen|熊野権現}} — Also known as {{nihongo|[[Kumano Sanzan]]|熊野三山}}<ref name="kumano">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1076 Kumano Shinkō], accessed on October 6, 2008</ref><ref>[http://www.sekaiisan-wakayama.jp/english/s_sanzan.htm Kumano Sanzan], World Heritage Registration Association, accessed on October 13, 2008</ref>. The ''kami'' enshrined in the three Kumano Sanzan Grand Shrines are the three Kumano mountains: Hongū, Shingū, and Nachi<ref name="kumano"/>. * {{nihongo|Seiryū Gongen|清滝権現}} — Enshrined in Jingo-ji in Takao as tutelary ''kami'' of the [[Shingon Buddhism|Shingon sect]] by [[Kūkai]].<ref>Jaanus, ''Seiryū Gongen'', accessed on October 6, 2008</ref> * Tōshō Daigongen — One of the most famous examples of ''gongen'' is the already mentioned {{nihongo|Tōshō Daigongen|東照大権現}}, or Tokugawa Ieyasu, posthumously enshrined in so-called [[Tōshō-gū]] shrines present all over Japan. The original one is [[Nikkō Tōshō-gū]] in [[Nikkō]]. * {{nihongo|Zaō Gongen|蔵王権現}} or {{nihongo|Kongō Zaō Bosatsu|金剛蔵王菩薩}} — A deity worshiped only by Japan's [[Shugendō]] sect.<ref>Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=201 Zaō Gongen], accessed on October 6, 2008</ref>
== ''Gongen-zukuri'' == [[Image:Gongen Zukuri.png|thumb|The blueprint of a gongen-zukuri shrine]] {{main|Ishi-no-ma-zukuri}} {{nihongo|''Gongen-zukuri''|権現造}} is the name of a complex [[Shinto shrine]] structure in which the ''[[haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'', or worship hall, and the [[honden]], or main sanctuary, are interconnected under the same roof in the shape of an H<ref name="sos2">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_g.html#gongen-zukuri ''Gongen-zukuri''] accessed on October 5, 2008</ref><ref>For details about these terms, see the article [[Shinto shrine]].</ref>. One of the oldest examples of ''gongen-zukuri'' is Kitano Tenmangū in [[Kyoto]]<ref name="sos2"/>. The name comes from Nikkō Tōshō-gū in Nikkō because, as we have seen, it enshrines the Tōshō Daigongen and adopts this structure<ref name="Jaa">Jaanus, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/g/gongenzukuri.htm Gongen-zukuri], accessed on October 5, 2008</ref>.
== See also == * The [[Glossary of Shinto]] for an explanation of terms concerning Japanese Shinto, Shinto art, and Shinto shrine architecture.

=== Gongen of Japan === * {{nihongo|Izuna Gongen|飯網の権現}} — Also called Izuna [[Myōjin]] and enshrined in Izuna Shrine in Nagano, it is similar to a ''tengu''.<ref>Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=193 Izuna Gongen], accessed on October 6, 2008</ref> * {{nihongo|Izusan Gongen|伊豆山権現}} or {{nihongo|Hashiri-yu Gongen|走湯権現}} — It's the spirit of a hot spring on Izusan, a hill in Shizuoka Prefecture<ref>Jaanus, ''Izusan Gongen'', accessed on October 6, 2008.</ref> * {{nihongo|Kumano Gongen|熊野権現}} — Also known as {{nihongo|[[Kumano Sanzan]]|熊野三山}}<ref name="kumano">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1076 Kumano Shinkō], accessed on October 6, 2008</ref><ref>[http://www.sekaiisan-wakayama.jp/english/s_sanzan.htm Kumano Sanzan], World Heritage Registration Association, accessed on October 13, 2008</ref>. The ''kami'' enshrined in the three Kumano Sanzan Grand Shrines are the three Kumano mountains: Hongū, Shingū, and Nachi<ref name="kumano"/>. * {{nihongo|Seiryū Gongen|清滝権現}} — Enshrined in Jingo-ji in Takao as tutelary ''kami'' of the [[Shingon Buddhism|Shingon sect]] by [[Kūkai]].<ref>Jaanus, ''Seiryū Gongen'', accessed on October 6, 2008</ref> * Tōshō Daigongen — One of the most famous examples of ''gongen'' is the already mentioned {{nihongo|Tōshō Daigongen|東照大権現}}, or Tokugawa Ieyasu, posthumously enshrined in so-called [[Tōshō-gū]] shrines present all over Japan. The original one is [[Nikkō Tōshō-gū]] in [[Nikkō]]. * {{nihongo|Zaō Gongen|蔵王権現}} or {{nihongo|Kongō Zaō Bosatsu|金剛蔵王菩薩}} — A deity worshiped only by Japan's [[Shugendō]] sect.<ref>Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=201 Zaō Gongen], accessed on October 6, 2008</ref>
== ''Gongen-zukuri'' == [[Image:Gongen Zukuri.png|thumb|The blueprint of a gongen-zukuri shrine]] {{main|Ishi-no-ma-zukuri}} {{nihongo|''Gongen-zukuri''|権現造}} is the name of a complex [[Shinto shrine]] structure in which the ''[[haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'', or worship hall, and the [[honden]], or main sanctuary, are interconnected under the same roof in the shape of an H<ref name="sos2">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_g.html#gongen-zukuri ''Gongen-zukuri''] accessed on October 5, 2008</ref><ref>For details about these terms, see the article [[Shinto shrine]].</ref>. The original one is [[Nikkō Tōshō-gū]] in [[Nikkō]]. * {{nihongo|Zaō Gongen|蔵王権現}} or {{nihongo|Kongō Zaō Bosatsu|金剛蔵王菩薩}} — A deity worshiped only by Japan's [[Shugendō]] sect.<ref>Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=201 Zaō Gongen], accessed on October 6, 2008</ref>
== ''Gongen-zukuri'' == [[Image:Gongen Zukuri.png|thumb|The blueprint of a gongen-zukuri shrine]] {{main|Ishi-no-ma-zukuri}} {{nihongo|''Gongen-zukuri''|権現造}} is the name of a complex [[Shinto shrine]] structure in which the ''[[haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'', or worship hall, and the [[honden]], or main sanctuary, are interconnected under the same roof in the shape of an H<ref name="sos2">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_g.html#gongen-zukuri ''Gongen-zukuri''] accessed on October 5, 2008</ref><ref>For details about these terms, see the article [[Shinto shrine]].</ref>. One of the oldest examples of ''gongen-zukuri'' is Kitano Tenmangū in [[Kyoto]]<ref name="sos2"/>. The name comes from Nikkō Tōshō-gū in Nikkō because, as we have seen, it enshrines the Tōshō Daigongen and adopts this structure<ref name="Jaa">Jaanus, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/g/gongenzukuri.htm Gongen-zukuri], accessed on October 5, 2008</ref>.
== See also == * The [[Glossary of Shinto]] for an explanation of terms concerning Japanese Shinto, Shinto art, and Shinto shrine architecture.
==Notes==
{{Reflist|2}}
== References == *{{cite book |last=Tamura |first=Yoshiro |title=Japanese Buddhism — A Cultural History |publisher=Kosei Publishing Company |location=Tokyo|year=2000 |edition=First Edition |pages=232 pages |isbn=4-333-01684-3 |language= }} * {{cite book | last = John Breen | first = Mark Teuween (editors) | coauthors = | title = Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami | publisher = University of Hawaii Press | date = July 2000 | location = Honolulu | language = | isbn = 978-0824823634 | oclc = 43487317 }}
[[Category:Shinto]] [[Category:Japanese religions terms]] [[Category:Buddhism in Japan]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]]
[[ja:権現]] [[fi:Gongen]]

[[Image:Gate-nikko-japan.jpg|thumb|250px|Nikkō Tōshō-gū enshrines Tokugawa Ieyasu under the posthumous name of Tōshō Daigongen]] {{nihongo|'''Gongen'''|権現}} in Japanese [[Buddhism|Buddhist]] and [[Shinto]] terminology is an [[Avatar (Hinduism)|avatar]], a Japanese ''[[kami]]'' which is really a temporary manifestation of a [[Buddhahood|buddha]] come from India to guide the Japanese to salvation<ref name="SoS">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_g.html#gongen ''Gongen''] accessed on October 5, 2008</ref><ref name="TY87">Tamura (2000:87)</ref>. The words {{nihongo|''gonge''|権化}} and {{nihongo|''kegen''|化現}} are [[synonym]]s<ref name="sos3"/>. {{nihongo|''Gongen shinkō''|権現信仰}} is the belief in the existence of ''gongen''<ref name="sos3"/>.
== History == [[Image:NikkoToriiTablet5127.jpg|left|thumb|Tablet on ''torii'' at Nikkō Tōshō-gū reads "Tōshō Daigongen" (calligraphy by [[Emperor Go-Mizunoo]])]]It is sometimes assumed that the word ''gongen'' derives from [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]]'s posthumous name (Tōshō Daigongen). The term however was created and started being used in the middle of the [[Heian period]] in an effort to harmonize Buddhism and Shinto in what is called [[shinbutsu shūgō]], or "[[syncretism]] of ''kami'' and buddhas"<ref name="TY87">Tamura (2000:87)</ref>. At that time, the assumption that Japanese ''kami'' and buddhas were essentially the same evolved into a theory called {{nihongo|''[[honji suijaku]]''|本地垂迹}} which held that ''kami'' were manifestations (''avatars'') of buddhas, [[bodhisattva]]s and other Buddhist deities<ref name="TY87"/>. As the theory gradually spread around the country, the concept of ''gongen'' evolved. As a result, under the influence of [[Tendai|Tendai Buddhism]] and [[Shugendō]], the ''gongen'' concept was adapted for example to religious beliefs tied to [[Mount Iwaki]], a volcano, so that female ''kami'' Kuniyasutamahime became associated with Jūichimen [[Kannon Bosatsu]] (eleven-faced Kannon), ''kami'' [[Ōkuninushi]] with [[Yakushi Nyorai]], and [[Kunitokotachi]] no Mikoto with [[Amida Nyorai]]<ref>Breen, Teeuwen (2000:194)</ref>.
The title ''gongen'' started being attached to the names of Shinto ''kami'' and shrines were built within the premises of large Buddhist temples to enshrine their tutelary ''kami''<ref name="TY87">Tamura (2000:87)</ref>. During the Japanese Middle Ages, shrines started being called with the name ''gongen''<ref name="sos3">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=790 Gongen shinkō], accessed on October 5, 2008</ref>. For example, in Eastern Japan there are still many Hakusan shrines where the shrine itself is called either ''gongen'' or ''jinja''<ref name="sos3"/>. Because it represents the application of Buddhist terminology to Shinto ''kami'', the use of the term was legally abolished by the Meiji government with the {{nihongo|[[Shinbutsu bunri|Shinto and Buddhism Separation Order]]|神仏判然令|Shin-butsu Hanzenrei}}, and shrines began to be called ''jinja''<ref name="sos3"/>.
=== Gongen of Japan === * {{nihongo|Izuna Gongen|飯網の権現}} — Also called Izuna [[Myōjin]] and enshrined in Izuna Shrine in Nagano, it is similar to a ''tengu''.<ref>Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=193 Izuna Gongen], accessed on October 6, 2008</ref> * {{nihongo|Izusan Gongen|伊豆山権現}} or {{nihongo|Hashiri-yu Gongen|走湯権現}} — It's the spirit of a hot spring on Izusan, a hill in Shizuoka Prefecture<ref>Jaanus, ''Izusan Gongen'', accessed on October 6, 2008.</ref> * {{nihongo|Kumano Gongen|熊野権現}} — Also known as {{nihongo|[[Kumano Sanzan]]|熊野三山}}<ref name="kumano">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1076 Kumano Shinkō], accessed on October 6, 2008</ref><ref>[http://www.sekaiisan-wakayama.jp/english/s_sanzan.htm Kumano Sanzan], World Heritage Registration Association, accessed on October 13, 2008</ref>. The ''kami'' enshrined in the three Kumano Sanzan Grand Shrines are the three Kumano mountains: Hongū, Shingū, and Nachi<ref name="kumano"/>. * {{nihongo|Seiryū Gongen|清滝権現}} — Enshrined in Jingo-ji in Takao as tutelary ''kami'' of the [[Shingon Buddhism|Shingon sect]] by [[Kūkai]].<ref>Jaanus, ''Seiryū Gongen'', accessed on October 6, 2008</ref> * Tōshō Daigongen — One of the most famous examples of ''gongen'' is the already mentioned {{nihongo|Tōshō Daigongen|東照大権現}}, or Tokugawa Ieyasu, posthumously enshrined in so-called [[Tōshō-gū]] shrines present all over Japan. The original one is [[Nikkō Tōshō-gū]] in [[Nikkō]]. * {{nihongo|Zaō Gongen|蔵王権現}} or {{nihongo|Kongō Zaō Bosatsu|金剛蔵王菩薩}} — A deity worshiped only by Japan's [[Shugendō]] sect.<ref>Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=201 Zaō Gongen], accessed on October 6, 2008</ref>
== ''Gongen-zukuri'' == [[Image:Gongen Zukuri.png|thumb|The blueprint of a gongen-zukuri shrine]] {{main|Ishi-no-ma-zukuri}} {{nihongo|''Gongen-zukuri''|権現造}} is the name of a complex [[Shinto shrine]] structure in which the ''[[haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'', or worship hall, and the [[honden]], or main sanctuary, are interconnected under the same roof in the shape of an H<ref name="sos2">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_g.html#gongen-zukuri ''Gongen-zukuri''] accessed on October 5, 2008</ref><ref>For details about these terms, see the article [[Shinto shrine]].</ref>. One of the oldest examples of ''gongen-zukuri'' is Kitano Tenmangū in [[Kyoto]]<ref name="sos2"/>. The name comes from Nikkō Tōshō-gū in Nikkō because, as we have seen, it enshrines the Tōshō Daigongen and adopts this structure<ref name="Jaa">Jaanus, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/g/gongenzukuri.htm Gongen-zukuri], accessed on October 5, 2008</ref>.
== See also == * The [[Glossary of Shinto]] for an explanation of terms concerning Japanese Shinto, Shinto art, and Shinto shrine architecture.
==Notes==

The words {{nihongo|''gonge''|権化}} and {{nihongo|''kegen''|化現}} are [[synonym]]s<ref name="sos3"/>. {{nihongo|''Gongen shinkō''|権現信仰}} is the belief in the existence of ''gongen''<ref name="sos3"/>.
== History == [[Image:NikkoToriiTablet5127.jpg|left|thumb|Tablet on ''torii'' at Nikkō Tōshō-gū reads "Tōshō Daigongen" (calligraphy by [[Emperor Go-Mizunoo]])]]It is sometimes assumed that the word ''gongen'' derives from [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]]'s posthumous name (Tōshō Daigongen). The term however was created and started being used in the middle of the [[Heian period]] in an effort to harmonize Buddhism and Shinto in what is called [[shinbutsu shūgō]], or "[[syncretism]] of ''kami'' and buddhas"<ref name="TY87">Tamura (2000:87)</ref>. At that time, the assumption that Japanese ''kami'' and buddhas were essentially the same evolved into a theory called {{nihongo|''[[honji suijaku]]''|本地垂迹}} which held that ''kami'' were manifestations (''avatars'') of buddhas, [[bodhisattva]]s and other Buddhist deities<ref name="TY87"/>. As the theory gradually spread around the country, the concept of ''gongen'' evolved. As a result, under the influence of [[Tendai|Tendai Buddhism]] and [[Shugendō]], the ''gongen'' concept was adapted for example to religious beliefs tied to [[Mount Iwaki]], a volcano, so that female ''kami'' Kuniyasutamahime became associated with Jūichimen [[Kannon Bosatsu]] (eleven-faced Kannon), ''kami'' [[Ōkuninushi]] with [[Yakushi Nyorai]], and [[Kunitokotachi]] no Mikoto with [[Amida Nyorai]]<ref>Breen, Teeuwen (2000:194)</ref>.

During the Japanese Middle Ages, shrines started being called with the name ''gongen''<ref name="sos3">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=790 Gongen shinkō], accessed on October 5, 2008</ref>. For example, in Eastern Japan there are still many Hakusan shrines where the shrine itself is called either ''gongen'' or ''jinja''<ref name="sos3"/>. Because it represents the application of Buddhist terminology to Shinto ''kami'', the use of the term was legally abolished by the Meiji government with the {{nihongo|[[Shinbutsu bunri|Shinto and Buddhism Separation Order]]|神仏判然令|Shin-butsu Hanzenrei}}, and shrines began to be called ''jinja''<ref name="sos3"/>.
=== Gongen of Japan === * {{nihongo|Izuna Gongen|飯網の権現}} — Also called Izuna [[Myōjin]] and enshrined in Izuna Shrine in Nagano, it is similar to a ''tengu''.<ref>Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=193 Izuna Gongen], accessed on October 6, 2008</ref> * {{nihongo|Izusan Gongen|伊豆山権現}} or {{nihongo|Hashiri-yu Gongen|走湯権現}} — It's the spirit of a hot spring on Izusan, a hill in Shizuoka Prefecture<ref>Jaanus, ''Izusan Gongen'', accessed on October 6, 2008.</ref> * {{nihongo|Kumano Gongen|熊野権現}} — Also known as {{nihongo|[[Kumano Sanzan]]|熊野三山}}<ref name="kumano">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1076 Kumano Shinkō], accessed on October 6, 2008</ref><ref>[http://www.sekaiisan-wakayama.jp/english/s_sanzan.htm Kumano Sanzan], World Heritage Registration Association, accessed on October 13, 2008</ref>. The ''kami'' enshrined in the three Kumano Sanzan Grand Shrines are the three Kumano mountains: Hongū, Shingū, and Nachi<ref name="kumano"/>. * {{nihongo|Seiryū Gongen|清滝権現}} — Enshrined in Jingo-ji in Takao as tutelary ''kami'' of the [[Shingon Buddhism|Shingon sect]] by [[Kūkai]].<ref>Jaanus, ''Seiryū Gongen'', accessed on October 6, 2008</ref> * Tōshō Daigongen — One of the most famous examples of ''gongen'' is the already mentioned {{nihongo|Tōshō Daigongen|東照大権現}}, or Tokugawa Ieyasu, posthumously enshrined in so-called [[Tōshō-gū]] shrines present all over Japan. The original one is [[Nikkō Tōshō-gū]] in [[Nikkō]]. * {{nihongo|Zaō Gongen|蔵王権現}} or {{nihongo|Kongō Zaō Bosatsu|金剛蔵王菩薩}} — A deity worshiped only by Japan's [[Shugendō]] sect.<ref>Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=201 Zaō Gongen], accessed on October 6, 2008</ref>
== ''Gongen-zukuri'' == [[Image:Gongen Zukuri.png|thumb|The blueprint of a gongen-zukuri shrine]] {{main|Ishi-no-ma-zukuri}} {{nihongo|''Gongen-zukuri''|権現造}} is the name of a complex [[Shinto shrine]] structure in which the ''[[haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'', or worship hall, and the [[honden]], or main sanctuary, are interconnected under the same roof in the shape of an H<ref name="sos2">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_g.html#gongen-zukuri ''Gongen-zukuri''] accessed on October 5, 2008</ref><ref>For details about these terms, see the article [[Shinto shrine]].</ref>. One of the oldest examples of ''gongen-zukuri'' is Kitano Tenmangū in [[Kyoto]]<ref name="sos2"/>. The name comes from Nikkō Tōshō-gū in Nikkō because, as we have seen, it enshrines the Tōshō Daigongen and adopts this structure<ref name="Jaa">Jaanus, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/g/gongenzukuri.htm Gongen-zukuri], accessed on October 5, 2008</ref>.

revision.comment: Disagree with evaluation. The article is about an obscure subject, but crystal clear. If you have concrete proposals, please use the talk page as the template suggests.

[[ja:権現]] [[fi:Gongen]]

[[Image:Gate-nikko-japan.jpg|thumb|250px|Nikkō Tōshō-gū enshrines Tokugawa Ieyasu under the posthumous name of Tōshō Daigongen]] {main|Honji suijaku}} During the era of ''[[shinbutsu shūgō]]'' (religious syncretism of ''[[kami]]'' and [[Buddhahood|buddhas]]), a ( {{nihongo|'''gongen'''|権現}} in Japan was a ''[[kami]]'' which was really a local manifestation of an Indian [[Buddhahood|buddha]], an entity who had come to Japan to guide the Japanese to salvation.<ref name="SoS">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_g.html#gongen ''Gongen''] accessed on October 5, 2008</ref><ref name="TY87">Tamura (2000:87)</ref> The words {{nihongo|''gonge''|権化}} and {{nihongo|''kegen''|化現}} are [[synonym]]s<ref name="sos3"/>. {{nihongo|''Gongen shinkō''|権現信仰}} is the belief in the existence of ''gongen''<ref name="sos3"/>.
The ''gongen'' concept is the cornerstone of the ''honji suijaku'' theory, according to which Indian Buddhist deities choose to appear to the Japanese as native ''kami'' in order to save them.
== History == [[Image:NikkoToriiTablet5127.jpg|left|thumb|Tablet on ''torii'' at Nikkō Tōshō-gū reads "Tōshō Daigongen" (calligraphy by [[Emperor Go-Mizunoo]])]]It is sometimes assumed that the word ''gongen'' derives from [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]]'s posthumous name (Tōshō Daigongen). The term however was created and started being used in the middle of the [[Heian period]] in an effort to harmonize Buddhism and Shinto in what is called [[shinbutsu shūgō]], or "[[syncretism]] of ''kami'' and buddhas"<ref name="TY87">Tamura (2000:87)</ref>. At that time, the assumption that Japanese ''kami'' and buddhas were essentially the same evolved into a theory called {{nihongo|''[[honji suijaku]]''|本地垂迹}} which held that ''kami'' were manifestations (''avatars'') of buddhas, [[bodhisattva]]s and other Buddhist deities<ref name="TY87"/>. As the theory gradually spread around the country, the concept of ''gongen'' evolved. As a result, under the influence of [[Tendai|Tendai Buddhism]] and [[Shugendō]], the ''gongen'' concept was adapted for example to religious beliefs tied to [[Mount Iwaki]], a volcano, so that female ''kami'' Kuniyasutamahime became associated with Jūichimen [[Kannon Bosatsu]] (eleven-faced Kannon), ''kami'' [[Ōkuninushi]] with [[Yakushi Nyorai]], and [[Kunitokotachi]] no Mikoto with [[Amida Nyorai]]<ref>Breen, Teeuwen (2000:194)</ref>.
The title ''gongen'' started being attached to the names of Shinto ''kami'' and shrines were built within the premises of large Buddhist temples to enshrine their tutelary ''kami''<ref name="TY87">Tamura (2000:87)</ref>. During the Japanese Middle Ages, shrines started being called with the name ''gongen''<ref name="sos3">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=790 Gongen shinkō], accessed on October 5, 2008</ref>. For example, in Eastern Japan there are still many Hakusan shrines where the shrine itself is called either ''gongen'' or ''jinja''<ref name="sos3"/>. Because it represents the application of Buddhist terminology to Shinto ''kami'', the use of the term was legally abolished by the Meiji government with the {{nihongo|[[Shinbutsu bunri|Shinto and Buddhism Separation Order]]|神仏判然令|Shin-butsu Hanzenrei}}, and shrines began to be called ''jinja''<ref name="sos3"/>.
=== Gongen of Japan === * {{nihongo|Izuna Gongen|飯網の権現}} — Also called Izuna [[Myōjin]] and enshrined in Izuna Shrine in Nagano, it is similar to a ''tengu''.<ref>Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=193 Izuna Gongen], accessed on October 6, 2008</ref> * {{nihongo|Izusan Gongen|伊豆山権現}} or {{nihongo|Hashiri-yu Gongen|走湯権現}} — It's the spirit of a hot spring on Izusan, a hill in Shizuoka Prefecture<ref>Jaanus, ''Izusan Gongen'', accessed on October 6, 2008.</ref> * {{nihongo|Kumano Gongen|熊野権現}} — Also known as {{nihongo|[[Kumano Sanzan]]|熊野三山}}<ref name="kumano">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1076 Kumano Shinkō], accessed on October 6, 2008</ref><ref>[http://www.sekaiisan-wakayama.jp/english/s_sanzan.htm Kumano Sanzan], World Heritage Registration Association, accessed on October 13, 2008</ref>. The ''kami'' enshrined in the three Kumano Sanzan Grand Shrines are the three Kumano mountains: Hongū, Shingū, and Nachi<ref name="kumano"/>. * {{nihongo|Seiryū Gongen|清滝権現}} — Enshrined in Jingo-ji in Takao as tutelary ''kami'' of the [[Shingon Buddhism|Shingon sect]] by [[Kūkai]].<ref>Jaanus, ''Seiryū Gongen'', accessed on October 6, 2008</ref> * Tōshō Daigongen — One of the most famous examples of ''gongen'' is the already mentioned {{nihongo|Tōshō Daigongen|東照大権現}}, or Tokugawa Ieyasu, posthumously enshrined in so-called [[Tōshō-gū]] shrines present all over Japan. The original one is [[Nikkō Tōshō-gū]] in [[Nikkō]]. * {{nihongo|Zaō Gongen|蔵王権現}} or {{nihongo|Kongō Zaō Bosatsu|金剛蔵王菩薩}} — A deity worshiped only by Japan's [[Shugendō]] sect.<ref>Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=201 Zaō Gongen], accessed on October 6, 2008</ref>
== ''Gongen-zukuri'' == [[Image:Gongen Zukuri.png|thumb|The blueprint of a gongen-zukuri shrine]] {{main|Ishi-no-ma-zukuri}} {{nihongo|''Gongen-zukuri''|権現造}} is the name of a complex [[Shinto shrine]] structure in which the ''[[haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'', or worship hall, and the [[honden]], or main sanctuary, are interconnected under the same roof in the shape of an H<ref name="sos2">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_g.html#gongen-zukuri ''Gongen-zukuri''] accessed on October 5, 2008</ref><ref>For details about these terms, see the article [[Shinto shrine]].</ref>. One of the oldest examples of ''gongen-zukuri'' is Kitano Tenmangū in [[Kyoto]]<ref name="sos2"/>. The name comes from Nikkō Tōshō-gū in Nikkō because, as we have seen, it enshrines the Tōshō Daigongen and adopts this structure<ref name="Jaa">Jaanus, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/g/gongenzukuri.htm Gongen-zukuri], accessed on October 5, 2008</ref>.
== See also == * The [[Glossary of Shinto]] for an explanation of terms concerning Japanese Shinto, Shinto art, and Shinto shrine architecture.

The original one is [[Nikkō Tōshō-gū]] in [[Nikkō]]. * {{nihongo|Zaō Gongen|蔵王権現}} or {{nihongo|Kongō Zaō Bosatsu|金剛蔵王菩薩}} — A deity worshiped only by Japan's [[Shugendō]] sect.<ref>Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=201 Zaō Gongen], accessed on October 6, 2008</ref>
== ''Gongen-zukuri'' == [[Image:Gongen Zukuri.png|thumb|The blueprint of a gongen-zukuri shrine]] {{main|Ishi-no-ma-zukuri}} {{nihongo|''Gongen-zukuri''|権現造}} is the name of a complex [[Shinto shrine]] structure in which the ''[[haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'', or worship hall, and the [[honden]], or main sanctuary, are interconnected under the same roof in the shape of an H<ref name="sos2">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_g.html#gongen-zukuri ''Gongen-zukuri''] accessed on October 5, 2008</ref><ref>For details about these terms, see the article [[Shinto shrine]].</ref>. As the theory gradually spread around the country, the concept of ''gongen'' evolved. As a result, under the influence of [[Tendai|Tendai Buddhism]] and [[Shugendō]], the ''gongen'' concept was adapted for example to religious beliefs tied to [[Mount Iwaki]], a volcano, so that female ''kami'' Kuniyasutamahime became associated with Jūichimen [[Kannon Bosatsu]] (eleven-faced Kannon), ''kami'' [[Ōkuninushi]] with [[Yakushi Nyorai]], and [[Kunitokotachi]] no Mikoto with [[Amida Nyorai]]<ref>Breen, Teeuwen (2000:194)</ref>.
The title ''gongen'' started being attached to the names of Shinto ''kami'' and shrines were built within the premises of large Buddhist temples to enshrine their tutelary ''kami''<ref name="TY87">Tamura (2000:87)</ref>. During the Japanese Middle Ages, shrines started being called with the name ''gongen''<ref name="sos3">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=790 Gongen shinkō], accessed on October 5, 2008</ref>. For example, in Eastern Japan there are still many Hakusan shrines where the shrine itself is called either ''gongen'' or ''jinja''<ref name="sos3"/>. Because it represents the application of Buddhist terminology to Shinto ''kami'', the use of the term was legally abolished by the Meiji government with the {{nihongo|[[Shinbutsu bunri|Shinto and Buddhism Separation Order]]|神仏判然令|Shin-butsu Hanzenrei}}, and shrines began to be called ''jinja''<ref name="sos3"/>.
=== Gongen of Japan === * {{nihongo|Izuna Gongen|飯網の権現}} — Also called Izuna [[Myōjin]] and enshrined in Izuna Shrine in Nagano, it is similar to a ''tengu''.<ref>Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=193 Izuna Gongen], accessed on October 6, 2008</ref> * {{nihongo|Izusan Gongen|伊豆山権現}} or {{nihongo|Hashiri-yu Gongen|走湯権現}} — It's the spirit of a hot spring on Izusan, a hill in Shizuoka Prefecture<ref>Jaanus, ''Izusan Gongen'', accessed on October 6, 2008.</ref> * {{nihongo|Kumano Gongen|熊野権現}} — Also known as {{nihongo|[[Kumano Sanzan]]|熊野三山}}<ref name="kumano">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1076 Kumano Shinkō], accessed on October 6, 2008</ref><ref>[http://www.sekaiisan-wakayama.jp/english/s_sanzan.htm Kumano Sanzan], World Heritage Registration Association, accessed on October 13, 2008</ref>. The ''kami'' enshrined in the three Kumano Sanzan Grand Shrines are the three Kumano mountains: Hongū, Shingū, and Nachi<ref name="kumano"/>. * {{nihongo|Seiryū Gongen|清滝権現}} — Enshrined in Jingo-ji in Takao as tutelary ''kami'' of the [[Shingon Buddhism|Shingon sect]] by [[Kūkai]].<ref>Jaanus, ''Seiryū Gongen'', accessed on October 6, 2008</ref> * Tōshō Daigongen — One of the most famous examples of ''gongen'' is the already mentioned {{nihongo|Tōshō Daigongen|東照大権現}}, or Tokugawa Ieyasu, posthumously enshrined in so-called [[Tōshō-gū]] shrines present all over Japan. The original one is [[Nikkō Tōshō-gū]] in [[Nikkō]]. * {{nihongo|Zaō Gongen|蔵王権現}} or {{nihongo|Kongō Zaō Bosatsu|金剛蔵王菩薩}} — A deity worshiped only by Japan's [[Shugendō]] sect.<ref>Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=201 Zaō Gongen], accessed on October 6, 2008</ref>
== ''Gongen-zukuri'' == [[Image:Gongen Zukuri.png|thumb|The blueprint of a gongen-zukuri shrine]] {{main|Ishi-no-ma-zukuri}} {{nihongo|''Gongen-zukuri''|権現造}} is the name of a complex [[Shinto shrine]] structure in which the ''[[haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'', or worship hall, and the [[honden]], or main sanctuary, are interconnected under the same roof in the shape of an H<ref name="sos2">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_g.html#gongen-zukuri ''Gongen-zukuri''] accessed on October 5, 2008</ref><ref>For details about these terms, see the article [[Shinto shrine]].</ref>. One of the oldest examples of ''gongen-zukuri'' is Kitano Tenmangū in [[Kyoto]]<ref name="sos2"/>. The name comes from Nikkō Tōshō-gū in Nikkō because, as we have seen, it enshrines the Tōshō Daigongen and adopts this structure<ref name="Jaa">Jaanus, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/g/gongenzukuri.htm Gongen-zukuri], accessed on October 5, 2008</ref>.

[[ja:権現]] [[fi:Gongen]]

[[Image:Gate-nikko-japan.jpg|thumb|250px|Nikkō Tōshō-gū enshrines Tokugawa Ieyasu under the posthumous name of Tōshō Daigongen]] {{main|Honji suijaku}} During the era of ''[[shinbutsu shūgō]]'' (religious syncretism of ''[[kami]]'' and [[Buddhahood|buddhas]]), a ( {{nihongo|'''gongen'''|権現}} in Japan was believed to be a Japanese ''[[kami]]'' which was really just the local manifestation of an Indian [[Buddhahood|buddha]], an entity who had come to Japan to guide the Japanese to salvation.<ref name="SoS">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_g.html#gongen ''Gongen''] accessed on October 5, 2008</ref><ref name="TY87">Tamura (2000:87)</ref> The words {{nihongo|''gonge''|権化}} and {{nihongo|''kegen''|化現}} are [[synonym]]s.<ref name="sos3"/> {{nihongo|''Gongen shinkō''|権現信仰}} is the belief in the existence of ''gongen''.<ref name="sos3"/>
The ''gongen'' concept is the cornerstone of the ''honji suijaku'' theory, according to which Indian Buddhist deities choose to appear to the Japanese as native ''kami'' in order to save them.
== History == [[Image:NikkoToriiTablet5127.jpg|left|thumb|Tablet on ''torii'' at Nikkō Tōshō-gū reads "Tōshō Daigongen" (calligraphy by [[Emperor Go-Mizunoo]])]]It is sometimes assumed that the word ''gongen'' derives from [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]]'s posthumous name (Tōshō Daigongen). The term however was created and started being used in the middle of the [[Heian period]] in an effort to harmonize Buddhism and Shinto in what is called [[shinbutsu shūgō]], or "[[syncretism]] of ''kami'' and buddhas"<ref name="TY87">Tamura (2000:87)</ref>. At that time, the assumption that Japanese ''kami'' and buddhas were essentially the same evolved into a theory called {{nihongo|''[[honji suijaku]]''|本地垂迹}} which held that ''kami'' were manifestations (''avatars'') of buddhas, [[bodhisattva]]s and other Buddhist deities<ref name="TY87"/>. The theory gradually spread around the country and the concept of ''gongen'', a dual entity composed of a ''buddha'' and a ''kami'', evolved. Under the influence of [[Tendai|Tendai Buddhism]] and of [[Shugendō]], the ''gongen'' concept was adapted to religious beliefs tied to [[Mount Iwaki]], a volcano, so that female ''kami'' Kuniyasutamahime became associated with Jūichimen [[Kannon Bosatsu]] (eleven-faced Kannon), ''kami'' [[Ōkuninushi]] with [[Yakushi Nyorai]], and [[Kunitokotachi]] no Mikoto with [[Amida Nyorai]]<ref>Breen, Teeuwen (2000:194)</ref>.
The title ''gongen'' started being attached to the names of ''kami'' and shrines were built within the premises of large Buddhist temples to enshrine their tutelary ''kami''<ref name="TY87">Tamura (2000:87)</ref>. During the Japanese Middle Ages, shrines started being called with the name ''gongen'' to uderline their ties to Buddhism<ref name="sos3">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=790 Gongen shinkō], accessed on October 5, 2008</ref>. For example, in Eastern Japan there are still many Hakusan shrines where the shrine itself is called either ''gongen'' or ''jinja''<ref name="sos3"/>. Because it represents the application of Buddhist terminology to Shinto ''kami'', the use of the term was legally abolished by the Meiji government with the {{nihongo|[[Shinbutsu bunri|Shinto and Buddhism Separation Order]]|神仏判然令|Shin-butsu Hanzenrei}}, and shrines began to be called ''jinja''<ref name="sos3"/>.

The ''kami'' enshrined in the three Kumano Sanzan Grand Shrines are the three Kumano mountains: Hongū, Shingū, and Nachi<ref name="kumano"/>. * {{nihongo|Seiryū Gongen|清滝権現}} — Enshrined in Jingo-ji in Takao as tutelary ''kami'' of the [[Shingon Buddhism|Shingon sect]] by [[Kūkai]].<ref>Jaanus, ''Seiryū Gongen'', accessed on October 6, 2008</ref> * Tōshō Daigongen — One of the most famous examples of ''gongen'' is the already mentioned {{nihongo|Tōshō Daigongen|東照大権現}}, or Tokugawa Ieyasu, posthumously enshrined in so-called [[Tōshō-gū]] shrines present all over Japan. The original one is [[Nikkō Tōshō-gū]] in [[Nikkō]]. * {{nihongo|Zaō Gongen|蔵王権現}} or {{nihongo|Kongō Zaō Bosatsu|金剛蔵王菩薩}} — A deity worshiped only by Japan's [[Shugendō]] sect.<ref>Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=201 Zaō Gongen], accessed on October 6, 2008</ref>
== ''Gongen-zukuri'' == [[Image:Gongen Zukuri.png|thumb|The blueprint of a gongen-zukuri shrine]] {{main|Ishi-no-ma-zukuri}} {{nihongo|''Gongen-zukuri''|権現造}} is the name of a complex [[Shinto shrine]] structure in which the ''[[haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'', or worship hall, and the [[honden]], or main sanctuary, are interconnected under the same roof in the shape of an H<ref name="sos2">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_g.html#gongen-zukuri ''Gongen-zukuri''] accessed on October 5, 2008</ref><ref>For details about these terms, see the article [[Shinto shrine]].</ref>. One of the oldest examples of ''gongen-zukuri'' is Kitano Tenmangū in [[Kyoto]]<ref name="sos2"/>. The name comes from Nikkō Tōshō-gū in Nikkō because, as we have seen, it enshrines the Tōshō Daigongen and adopts this structure<ref name="Jaa">Jaanus, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/g/gongenzukuri.htm Gongen-zukuri], accessed on October 5, 2008</ref>.

Because it represents the application of Buddhist terminology to Shinto ''kami'', the use of the term was legally abolished by the Meiji government with the {{nihongo|[[Shinbutsu bunri|Shinto and Buddhism Separation Order]]|神仏判然令|Shin-butsu Hanzenrei}}, and shrines began to be called ''jinja''<ref name="sos3"/>.
=== Gongen of Japan === * {{nihongo|Izuna Gongen|飯網の権現}} — Also called Izuna [[Myōjin]] and enshrined in Izuna Shrine in Nagano, it is similar to a ''tengu''.<ref>Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=193 Izuna Gongen], accessed on October 6, 2008</ref> * {{nihongo|Izusan Gongen|伊豆山権現}} or {{nihongo|Hashiri-yu Gongen|走湯権現}} — It's the spirit of a hot spring on Izusan, a hill in Shizuoka Prefecture<ref>Jaanus, ''Izusan Gongen'', accessed on October 6, 2008.</ref> * {{nihongo|Kumano Gongen|熊野権現}} — Also known as {{nihongo|[[Kumano Sanzan]]|熊野三山}}<ref name="kumano">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1076 Kumano Shinkō], accessed on October 6, 2008</ref><ref>[http://www.sekaiisan-wakayama.jp/english/s_sanzan.htm Kumano Sanzan], World Heritage Registration Association, accessed on October 13, 2008</ref>. The ''kami'' enshrined in the three Kumano Sanzan Grand Shrines are the three Kumano mountains: Hongū, Shingū, and Nachi<ref name="kumano"/>. * {{nihongo|Seiryū Gongen|清滝権現}} — Enshrined in Jingo-ji in Takao as tutelary ''kami'' of the [[Shingon Buddhism|Shingon sect]] by [[Kūkai]].<ref>Jaanus, ''Seiryū Gongen'', accessed on October 6, 2008</ref> * Tōshō Daigongen — One of the most famous examples of ''gongen'' is the already mentioned {{nihongo|Tōshō Daigongen|東照大権現}}, or Tokugawa Ieyasu, posthumously enshrined in so-called [[Tōshō-gū]] shrines present all over Japan. The original one is [[Nikkō Tōshō-gū]] in [[Nikkō]]. * {{nihongo|Zaō Gongen|蔵王権現}} or {{nihongo|Kongō Zaō Bosatsu|金剛蔵王菩薩}} — A deity worshiped only by Japan's [[Shugendō]] sect.<ref>Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=201 Zaō Gongen], accessed on October 6, 2008</ref>
== ''Gongen-zukuri'' == [[Image:Gongen Zukuri.png|thumb|The blueprint of a gongen-zukuri shrine]] {{main|Ishi-no-ma-zukuri}} {{nihongo|''Gongen-zukuri''|権現造}} is the name of a complex [[Shinto shrine]] structure in which the ''[[haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'', or worship hall, and the [[honden]], or main sanctuary, are interconnected under the same roof in the shape of an H<ref name="sos2">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_g.html#gongen-zukuri ''Gongen-zukuri''] accessed on October 5, 2008</ref><ref>For details about these terms, see the article [[Shinto shrine]].</ref>. One of the oldest examples of ''gongen-zukuri'' is Kitano Tenmangū in [[Kyoto]]<ref name="sos2"/>. The name comes from Nikkō Tōshō-gū in Nikkō because, as we have seen, it enshrines the Tōshō Daigongen and adopts this structure<ref name="Jaa">Jaanus, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/g/gongenzukuri.htm Gongen-zukuri], accessed on October 5, 2008</ref>.
== See also == * The [[Glossary of Shinto]] for an explanation of terms concerning Japanese Shinto, Shinto art, and Shinto shrine architecture.
==Notes==

revision.text:
[[Image:Gate-nikko-japan.jpg|thumb|250px|Nikkō Tōshō-gū enshrines Tokugawa Ieyasu under the posthumous name of Tōshō Daigongen]] {{main|Honji suijaku}} During the era of ''[[shinbutsu shūgō]]'' (religious syncretism of ''[[kami]]'' and [[Buddhahood|buddhas]]), in Japan a ( {{nihongo|'''gongen'''|権現}} was believed to be a Japanese ''[[kami]]'' which was really just the local manifestation of an Indian [[Buddhahood|buddha]], an entity who had come to Japan to guide the Japanese to salvation.<ref name="SoS">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_g.html#gongen ''Gongen''] accessed on October 5, 2008</ref><ref name="TY87">Tamura (2000:87)</ref> The words {{nihongo|''gonge''|権化}} and {{nihongo|''kegen''|化現}} are [[synonym]]s of ''gongen''.<ref name="sos3"/> {{nihongo|''Gongen shinkō''|権現信仰|lit. gongen belief}} is the belief in the existence of ''gongen''.<ref name="sos3"/>
The ''gongen'' concept is the cornerstone of the ''[[honji suijaku]]'' theory, according to which Indian Buddhist deities choose to appear to the Japanese as native ''kami'' in order to save them.
== History == [[Image:NikkoToriiTablet5127.jpg|left|thumb|Tablet on ''torii'' at Nikkō Tōshō-gū reads "Tōshō Daigongen" (calligraphy by [[Emperor Go-Mizunoo]])]]It is sometimes assumed that the word ''gongen'' derives from [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]]'s posthumous name (Tōshō Daigongen). The term however was created and started being used in the middle of the [[Heian period]] in an effort to harmonize Buddhism and Shinto in what is called [[shinbutsu shūgō]], or "[[syncretism]] of ''kami'' and buddhas"<ref name="TY87">Tamura (2000:87)</ref>. At that time, the assumption that Japanese ''kami'' and buddhas were essentially the same evolved into a theory called {{nihongo|''[[honji suijaku]]''|本地垂迹}} which held that ''kami'' were manifestations (''avatars'') of buddhas, [[bodhisattva]]s and other Buddhist deities<ref name="TY87"/>. The theory gradually spread around the country and the concept of ''gongen'', a dual entity composed of a ''buddha'' and a ''kami'', evolved. Under the influence of [[Tendai|Tendai Buddhism]] and of [[Shugendō]], the ''gongen'' concept was adapted to religious beliefs tied to [[Mount Iwaki]], a volcano, so that female ''kami'' Kuniyasutamahime became associated with Jūichimen [[Kannon Bosatsu]] (eleven-faced Kannon), ''kami'' [[Ōkuninushi]] with [[Yakushi Nyorai]], and [[Kunitokotachi]] no Mikoto with [[Amida Nyorai]]<ref>Breen, Teeuwen (2000:194)</ref>.
The title ''gongen'' started being attached to the names of ''kami'' and shrines were built within the premises of large Buddhist temples to enshrine their tutelary ''kami''<ref name="TY87">Tamura (2000:87)</ref>. During the Japanese Middle Ages, shrines started being called with the name ''gongen'' to uderline their ties to Buddhism<ref name="sos3">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=790 Gongen shinkō], accessed on October 5, 2008</ref>. For example, in Eastern Japan there are still many Hakusan shrines where the shrine itself is called either ''gongen'' or ''jinja''<ref name="sos3"/>. Because it represents the application of Buddhist terminology to Shinto ''kami'', the use of the term was legally abolished by the Meiji government with the {{nihongo|[[Shinbutsu bunri|Shinto and Buddhism Separation Order]]|神仏判然令|Shin-butsu Hanzenrei}}, and shrines began to be called ''jinja''<ref name="sos3"/>.
=== Gongen of Japan === * {{nihongo|Izuna Gongen|飯網の権現}} — Also called Izuna [[Myōjin]] and enshrined in Izuna Shrine in Nagano, it is similar to a ''tengu''.<ref>Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=193 Izuna Gongen], accessed on October 6, 2008</ref> * {{nihongo|Izusan Gongen|伊豆山権現}} or {{nihongo|Hashiri-yu Gongen|走湯権現}} — It's the spirit of a hot spring on Izusan, a hill in Shizuoka Prefecture<ref>Jaanus, ''Izusan Gongen'', accessed on October 6, 2008.</ref> * {{nihongo|Kumano Gongen|熊野権現}} — Also known as {{nihongo|[[Kumano Sanzan]]|熊野三山}}<ref name="kumano">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1076 Kumano Shinkō], accessed on October 6, 2008</ref><ref>[http://www.sekaiisan-wakayama.jp/english/s_sanzan.htm Kumano Sanzan], World Heritage Registration Association, accessed on October 13, 2008</ref>. The ''kami'' enshrined in the three Kumano Sanzan Grand Shrines are the three Kumano mountains: Hongū, Shingū, and Nachi<ref name="kumano"/>. * {{nihongo|Seiryū Gongen|清滝権現}} — Enshrined in Jingo-ji in Takao as tutelary ''kami'' of the [[Shingon Buddhism|Shingon sect]] by [[Kūkai]].<ref>Jaanus, ''Seiryū Gongen'', accessed on October 6, 2008</ref> * Tōshō Daigongen — One of the most famous examples of ''gongen'' is the already mentioned {{nihongo|Tōshō Daigongen|東照大権現}}, or Tokugawa Ieyasu, posthumously enshrined in so-called [[Tōshō-gū]] shrines present all over Japan. The original one is [[Nikkō Tōshō-gū]] in [[Nikkō]]. * {{nihongo|Zaō Gongen|蔵王権現}} or {{nihongo|Kongō Zaō Bosatsu|金剛蔵王菩薩}} — A deity worshiped only by Japan's [[Shugendō]] sect.<ref>Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=201 Zaō Gongen], accessed on October 6, 2008</ref>
== ''Gongen-zukuri'' == [[Image:Gongen Zukuri.png|thumb|The blueprint of a gongen-zukuri shrine]] {{main|Ishi-no-ma-zukuri}} {{nihongo|''Gongen-zukuri''|権現造}} is the name of a complex [[Shinto shrine]] structure in which the ''[[haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'', or worship hall, and the [[honden]], or main sanctuary, are interconnected under the same roof in the shape of an H<ref name="sos2">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_g.html#gongen-zukuri ''Gongen-zukuri''] accessed on October 5, 2008</ref><ref>For details about these terms, see the article [[Shinto shrine]].</ref>. One of the oldest examples of ''gongen-zukuri'' is Kitano Tenmangū in [[Kyoto]]<ref name="sos2"/>. The name comes from Nikkō Tōshō-gū in Nikkō because, as we have seen, it enshrines the Tōshō Daigongen and adopts this structure<ref name="Jaa">Jaanus, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/g/gongenzukuri.htm Gongen-zukuri], accessed on October 5, 2008</ref>.
== See also == * The [[Glossary of Shinto]] for an explanation of terms concerning Japanese Shinto, Shinto art, and Shinto shrine architecture.
==Notes==

== References == *{{cite book |last=Tamura |first=Yoshiro |title=Japanese Buddhism — A Cultural History |publisher=Kosei Publishing Company |location=Tokyo|year=2000 |edition=First Edition |pages=232 pages |isbn=4-333-01684-3 |language= }} * {{cite book | last = John Breen | first = Mark Teeuwen (editors) | coauthors = | title = Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami | publisher = University of Hawaii Press | date = July 2000 | location = Honolulu | language = | isbn = 978-0824823634 | oclc = 43487317 }}

[[Image:Gate-nikko-japan.jpg|thumb|250px|Nikkō Tōshō-gū enshrines Tokugawa Ieyasu under the posthumous name of Tōshō Daigongen]] {{main|Honji suijaku}} During the era of ''[[shinbutsu shūgō]]'' (religious syncretism of ''[[kami]]'' and [[Buddhahood|buddhas]]), in Japan a ( {{nihongo|'''gongen'''|権現}} was believed to be a Japanese ''[[kami]]'' which was really just the local manifestation of an Indian [[Buddhahood|buddha]], an entity who had come to Japan to guide the Japanese to salvation.<ref name="SoS">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_g.html#gongen ''Gongen''] accessed on October 5, 2008</ref><ref name="TY87">Tamura (2000:87)</ref> The words {{nihongo|''gonge''|権化}} and {{nihongo|''kegen''|化現}} are [[synonym]]s of ''gongen''.<ref name="sos3"/> {{nihongo|''Gongen shinkō''|権現信仰|lit. gongen belief}} is the belief in the existence of ''gongen''.<ref name="sos3"/>
The ''gongen'' concept is the cornerstone of the ''[[honji suijaku]]'' theory, according to which Indian Buddhist deities choose to appear to the Japanese as native ''kami'' in order to save them.

Under the influence of [[Tendai|Tendai Buddhism]] and of [[Shugendō]], the ''gongen'' concept was adapted to religious beliefs tied to [[Mount Iwaki]], a volcano, so that female ''kami'' Kuniyasutamahime became associated with Jūichimen [[Kannon Bosatsu]] (eleven-faced Kannon), ''kami'' [[Ōkuninushi]] with [[Yakushi Nyorai]], and [[Kunitokotachi]] no Mikoto with [[Amida Nyorai]].<ref>Breen, Teeuwen (2000:194)</ref>
The title ''gongen'' started being attached to the names of ''kami'' and shrines were built within the premises of large Buddhist temples to enshrine their tutelary ''kami''.<ref name="TY87">Tamura (2000:87)</ref> During the Japanese Middle Ages, shrines started being called with the name ''gongen'' to uderline their ties to Buddhism.<ref name="sos3">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=790 Gongen shinkō], accessed on October 5, 2008</ref> For example, in Eastern Japan there are still many Hakusan shrines where the shrine itself is called either ''gongen'' or ''jinja''.<ref name="sos3"/> Because it represents the application of Buddhist terminology to Shinto ''kami'', the use of the term was legally abolished by the Meiji government with the {{nihongo|[[Shinbutsu bunri|Shinto and Buddhism Separation Order]]|神仏判然令|Shin-butsu Hanzenrei}}, and shrines began to be called ''jinja''.<ref name="sos3"/>
=== Gongen of Japan === * {{nihongo|Izuna Gongen|飯網の権現}} — Also called Izuna [[Myōjin]] and enshrined in Izuna Shrine in Nagano, it is similar to a ''tengu''.<ref>Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=193 Izuna Gongen], accessed on October 6, 2008</ref> * {{nihongo|Izusan Gongen|伊豆山権現}} or {{nihongo|Hashiri-yu Gongen|走湯権現}} — It's the spirit of a hot spring on Izusan, a hill in Shizuoka Prefecture<ref>Jaanus, ''Izusan Gongen'', accessed on October 6, 2008.</ref> * {{nihongo|Kumano Gongen|熊野権現}} — Also known as {{nihongo|[[Kumano Sanzan]]|熊野三山}}.<ref name="kumano">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1076 Kumano Shinkō], accessed on October 6, 2008</ref><ref>[http://www.sekaiisan-wakayama.jp/english/s_sanzan.htm Kumano Sanzan], World Heritage Registration Association, accessed on October 13, 2008</ref> The ''kami'' enshrined in the three Kumano Sanzan Grand Shrines are the three Kumano mountains: Hongū, Shingū, and Nachi.<ref name="kumano"/> * {{nihongo|Seiryū Gongen|清滝権現}} — Enshrined in Jingo-ji in Takao as tutelary ''kami'' of the [[Shingon Buddhism|Shingon sect]] by [[Kūkai]].<ref>Jaanus, ''Seiryū Gongen'', accessed on October 6, 2008</ref> * Tōshō Daigongen — One of the most famous examples of ''gongen'' is the already mentioned {{nihongo|Tōshō Daigongen|東照大権現}}, or Tokugawa Ieyasu, posthumously enshrined in so-called [[Tōshō-gū]] shrines present all over Japan. The original one is [[Nikkō Tōshō-gū]] in [[Nikkō]]. * {{nihongo|Zaō Gongen|蔵王権現}} or {{nihongo|Kongō Zaō Bosatsu|金剛蔵王菩薩}} — A deity worshiped only by Japan's [[Shugendō]] sect.<ref>Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=201 Zaō Gongen], accessed on October 6, 2008</ref>
== ''Gongen-zukuri'' == [[Image:Gongen Zukuri.png|thumb|The blueprint of a gongen-zukuri shrine]] {{main|Ishi-no-ma-zukuri}} {{nihongo|''Gongen-zukuri''|権現造}} is the name of a complex [[Shinto shrine]] structure in which the ''[[haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'', or worship hall, and the [[honden]], or main sanctuary, are interconnected under the same roof in the shape of an H.<ref name="sos2">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_g.html#gongen-zukuri ''Gongen-zukuri''] accessed on October 5, 2008</ref><ref>For details about these terms, see the article [[Shinto shrine]].</ref> One of the oldest examples of ''gongen-zukuri'' is Kitano Tenmangū in [[Kyoto]].<ref name="sos2"/> The name comes from Nikkō Tōshō-gū in Nikkō because, as we have seen, it enshrines the Tōshō Daigongen and adopts this structure.<ref name="Jaa">Jaanus, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/g/gongenzukuri.htm Gongen-zukuri], accessed on October 5, 2008</ref>
== See also == * The [[Glossary of Shinto]] for an explanation of terms concerning Japanese Shinto, Shinto art, and Shinto shrine architecture.

[[Category:Shinto]] [[Category:Japanese religions terms]] [[Category:Buddhism in Japan]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]]
[[ja:権現]] [[fi:Gongen]]

[[Image:Gate-nikko-japan.jpg|thumb|250px|Nikkō Tōshō-gū enshrines Tokugawa Ieyasu under the posthumous name of Tōshō Daigongen]] {{main|Honji suijaku}} During the era of ''[[shinbutsu shūgō]]'' (religious syncretism of ''[[kami]]'' and [[Buddhahood|buddhas]]), in Japan a ( {{nihongo|'''gongen'''|権現}} was believed to be a Japanese ''[[kami]]'' which was really just the local manifestation of an Indian [[Buddhahood|buddha]], an entity who had come to Japan to guide the Japanese to salvation.<ref name="SoS">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_g.html#gongen ''Gongen''] accessed on October 5, 2008</ref><ref name="TY87">Tamura (2000:87)</ref> The words {{nihongo|''gonge''|権化}} and {{nihongo|''kegen''|化現}} are [[synonym]]s of ''gongen''.<ref name="sos3"/> {{nihongo|''Gongen shinkō''|権現信仰|lit. gongen belief}} is the belief in the existence of ''gongen''.<ref name="sos3"/>
The ''gongen'' concept is the cornerstone of the ''[[honji suijaku]]'' theory, according to which Indian Buddhist deities choose to appear to the Japanese as native ''kami'' in order to save them.
== History == [[Image:NikkoToriiTablet5127.jpg|left|thumb|Tablet on ''torii'' at Nikkō Tōshō-gū reads "Tōshō Daigongen" (calligraphy by [[Emperor Go-Mizunoo]])]]It is sometimes assumed that the word ''gongen'' derives from [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]]'s posthumous name (Tōshō Daigongen). The term however was created and started being used in the middle of the [[Heian period]] in an effort to harmonize Buddhism and Shinto in what is called [[shinbutsu shūgō]], or "[[syncretism]] of ''kami'' and buddhas".<ref name="TY87">Tamura (2000:87)</ref> At that time, the assumption that Japanese ''kami'' and buddhas were essentially the same evolved into a theory called {{nihongo|''[[honji suijaku]]''|本地垂迹}} which held that ''kami'' were manifestations (''avatars'') of buddhas, [[bodhisattva]]s and other Buddhist deities.<ref name="TY87"/> The theory gradually spread around the country and the concept of ''gongen'', a dual entity composed of a ''buddha'' and a ''kami'', evolved. Under the influence of [[Tendai|Tendai Buddhism]] and of [[Shugendō]], the ''gongen'' concept was adapted to religious beliefs tied to [[Mount Iwaki]], a volcano, so that female ''kami'' Kuniyasutamahime became associated with Jūichimen [[Kannon Bosatsu]] (eleven-faced Kannon), ''kami'' [[Ōkuninushi]] with [[Yakushi Nyorai]], and [[Kunitokotachi]] no Mikoto with [[Amida Nyorai]].<ref>Breen, Teeuwen (2000:194)</ref>
The title ''gongen'' started being attached to the names of ''kami'' and shrines were built within the premises of large Buddhist temples to enshrine their tutelary ''kami''.<ref name="TY87">Tamura (2000:87)</ref> During the Japanese Middle Ages, shrines started being called with the name ''gongen'' to uderline their ties to Buddhism.<ref name="sos3">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=790 Gongen shinkō], accessed on October 5, 2008</ref> For example, in Eastern Japan there are still many Hakusan shrines where the shrine itself is called either ''gongen'' or ''jinja''.<ref name="sos3"/> Because it represents the application of Buddhist terminology to Shinto ''kami'', the use of the term was legally abolished by the Meiji government with the {{nihongo|[[Shinbutsu bunri|Shinto and Buddhism Separation Order]]|神仏判然令|Shin-butsu Hanzenrei}}, and shrines began to be called ''jinja''.<ref name="sos3"/>

The original one is [[Nikkō Tōshō-gū]] in [[Nikkō]]. * {{nihongo|Zaō Gongen|蔵王権現}} or {{nihongo|Kongō Zaō Bosatsu|金剛蔵王菩薩}} — A deity worshiped only by Japan's [[Shugendō]] sect.<ref>Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=201 Zaō Gongen], accessed on October 6, 2008</ref>
== ''Gongen-zukuri'' == [[Image:Gongen Zukuri.png|thumb|The blueprint of a gongen-zukuri shrine]] {{main|Ishi-no-ma-zukuri}} {{nihongo|''Gongen-zukuri''|権現造}} is the name of a complex [[Shinto shrine]] structure in which the ''[[haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'', or worship hall, and the [[honden]], or main sanctuary, are interconnected under the same roof in the shape of an H.<ref name="sos2">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_g.html#gongen-zukuri ''Gongen-zukuri''] accessed on October 5, 2008</ref><ref>For details about these terms, see the article [[Shinto shrine]].</ref> One of the oldest examples of ''gongen-zukuri'' is Kitano Tenmangū in [[Kyoto]].<ref name="sos2"/> The name comes from Nikkō Tōshō-gū in Nikkō because, as we have seen, it enshrines the Tōshō Daigongen and adopts this structure.<ref name="Jaa">Jaanus, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/g/gongenzukuri.htm Gongen-zukuri], accessed on October 5, 2008</ref>

Under the influence of [[Tendai|Tendai Buddhism]] and of [[Shugendō]], the ''gongen'' concept was adapted to religious beliefs tied to [[Mount Iwaki]], a volcano, so that female ''kami'' Kuniyasutamahime became associated with Jūichimen [[Kannon Bosatsu]] (eleven-faced Kannon), ''kami'' [[Ōkuninushi]] with [[Yakushi Nyorai]], and [[Kunitokotachi]] no Mikoto with [[Amida Nyorai]].<ref>Breen, Teeuwen (2000:194)</ref>
The title ''gongen'' started being attached to the names of ''kami'' and shrines were built within the premises of large Buddhist temples to enshrine their tutelary ''kami''.<ref name="TY87">Tamura (2000:87)</ref> During the Japanese Middle Ages, shrines started being called with the name ''gongen'' to uderline their ties to Buddhism.<ref name="sos3">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=790 Gongen shinkō], accessed on October 5, 2008</ref> For example, in Eastern Japan there are still many Hakusan shrines where the shrine itself is called either ''gongen'' or ''jinja''.<ref name="sos3"/> Because it represents the application of Buddhist terminology to Shinto ''kami'', the use of the term was legally abolished by the Meiji government with the {{nihongo|[[Shinbutsu bunri|Shinto and Buddhism Separation Order]]|神仏判然令|Shin-butsu Hanzenrei}}, and shrines began to be called ''jinja''.<ref name="sos3"/>
=== Gongen of Japan === * {{nihongo|Izuna Gongen|飯網の権現}} — Also called Izuna Myōjin and enshrined in Izuna Shrine in [[Nagano]], it is similar to a [[tengu]].<ref>Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=193 Izuna Gongen], accessed on October 6, 2008</ref> * {{nihongo|Izusan Gongen|伊豆山権現}} or {{nihongo|Hashiri-yu Gongen|走湯権現}} — It's the spirit of a hot spring on Izusan, a hill in Shizuoka Prefecture<ref>Jaanus, ''Izusan Gongen'', accessed on October 6, 2008.</ref> * {{nihongo|Kumano Gongen|熊野権現}} — Also known as {{nihongo|[[Kumano Sanzan]]|熊野三山}}.<ref name="kumano">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1076 Kumano Shinkō], accessed on October 6, 2008</ref><ref>[http://www.sekaiisan-wakayama.jp/english/s_sanzan.htm Kumano Sanzan], World Heritage Registration Association, accessed on October 13, 2008</ref> The ''kami'' enshrined in the three Kumano Sanzan Grand Shrines are the three Kumano mountains: Hongū, Shingū, and Nachi.<ref name="kumano"/> * {{nihongo|Seiryū Gongen|清滝権現}} — Enshrined in Jingo-ji in Takao as tutelary ''kami'' of the [[Shingon Buddhism|Shingon sect]] by [[Kūkai]].<ref>Jaanus, ''Seiryū Gongen'', accessed on October 6, 2008</ref> * Tōshō Daigongen — One of the most famous examples of ''gongen'' is the already mentioned {{nihongo|Tōshō Daigongen|東照大権現}}, or Tokugawa Ieyasu, posthumously enshrined in so-called [[Tōshō-gū]] shrines present all over Japan. The original one is [[Nikkō Tōshō-gū]] in [[Nikkō]]. * {{nihongo|Zaō Gongen|蔵王権現}} or {{nihongo|Kongō Zaō Bosatsu|金剛蔵王菩薩}} — A deity worshiped only by Japan's [[Shugendō]] sect.<ref>Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=201 Zaō Gongen], accessed on October 6, 2008</ref>
== ''Gongen-zukuri'' == [[Image:Gongen Zukuri.png|thumb|The blueprint of a gongen-zukuri shrine]] {{main|Ishi-no-ma-zukuri}} {{nihongo|''Gongen-zukuri''|権現造}} is the name of a complex [[Shinto shrine]] structure in which the ''[[haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'', or worship hall, and the [[honden]], or main sanctuary, are interconnected under the same roof in the shape of an H.<ref name="sos2">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_g.html#gongen-zukuri ''Gongen-zukuri''] accessed on October 5, 2008</ref><ref>For details about these terms, see the article [[Shinto shrine]].</ref> One of the oldest examples of ''gongen-zukuri'' is Kitano Tenmangū in [[Kyoto]].<ref name="sos2"/> The name comes from Nikkō Tōshō-gū in Nikkō because, as we have seen, it enshrines the Tōshō Daigongen and adopts this structure.<ref name="Jaa">Jaanus, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/g/gongenzukuri.htm Gongen-zukuri], accessed on October 5, 2008</ref>
== See also == * The [[Glossary of Shinto]] for an explanation of terms concerning Japanese Shinto, Shinto art, and Shinto shrine architecture.

[[ja:権現]] [[fi:Gongen]]

[[Image:Gate-nikko-japan.jpg|thumb|250px|Nikkō Tōshō-gū enshrines [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]] under the posthumous name of Tōshō Daigongen]] {{main|Honji suijaku}} During the era of ''[[shinbutsu shūgō]]'' (religious syncretism of ''[[kami]]'' and [[Buddhahood|buddhas]]), in Japan a ( {{nihongo|'''gongen'''|権現}} was believed to be a Japanese ''[[kami]]'' which was really just the local manifestation of an Indian [[Buddhahood|buddha]], an entity who had come to guide the Japanese to [[salvation]].<ref name="SoS">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_g.html#gongen ''Gongen''] accessed on October 5, 2008</ref><ref name="TY87">Tamura (2000:87)</ref> The words {{nihongo|''gonge''|権化}} and {{nihongo|''kegen''|化現}} are [[synonym]]s of ''gongen''.<ref name="sos3"/> {{nihongo|''Gongen shinkō''|権現信仰|lit. gongen belief}} is the belief in the existence of ''gongen''.<ref name="sos3"/>
The ''gongen'' concept is the cornerstone of the ''[[honji suijaku]]'' theory, according to which Indian Buddhist deities choose to appear to the Japanese as native ''kami'' in order to save them.
== History == [[Image:NikkoToriiTablet5127.jpg|left|thumb|Tablet on ''torii'' at Nikkō Tōshō-gū reads "Tōshō Daigongen" (calligraphy by [[Emperor Go-Mizunoo]])]]It is sometimes assumed that the word ''gongen'' derives from [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]]'s posthumous name (Tōshō Daigongen). The term however was created and started being used in the middle of the [[Heian period]] in an effort to harmonize Buddhism and Shinto in what is called [[shinbutsu shūgō]], or "[[syncretism]] of ''kami'' and buddhas".<ref name="TY87">Tamura (2000:87)</ref> At that time, the assumption that Japanese ''kami'' and buddhas were essentially the same evolved into a theory called {{nihongo|''[[honji suijaku]]''|本地垂迹}} which held that ''kami'' were manifestations (''avatars'') of buddhas, [[bodhisattva]]s and other Buddhist deities.<ref name="TY87"/> The theory gradually spread around the country and the concept of ''gongen'', a dual entity composed of a ''buddha'' and a ''kami'', evolved. Under the influence of [[Tendai|Tendai Buddhism]] and of [[Shugendō]], the ''gongen'' concept was adapted to religious beliefs tied to [[Mount Iwaki]], a volcano, so that female ''kami'' Kuniyasutamahime became associated with Jūichimen [[Kannon Bosatsu]] (eleven-faced Kannon), ''kami'' [[Ōkuninushi]] with [[Yakushi Nyorai]], and [[Kunitokotachi]] no Mikoto with [[Amida Nyorai]].<ref>Breen, Teeuwen (2000:194)</ref>
The title ''gongen'' started being attached to the names of ''kami'' and shrines were built within the premises of large Buddhist temples to enshrine their tutelary ''kami''.<ref name="TY87">Tamura (2000:87)</ref> During the Japanese Middle Ages, shrines started being called with the name ''gongen'' to uderline their ties to Buddhism.<ref name="sos3">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=790 Gongen shinkō], accessed on October 5, 2008</ref> For example, in Eastern Japan there are still many Hakusan shrines where the shrine itself is called either ''gongen'' or ''jinja''.<ref name="sos3"/> Because it represents the application of Buddhist terminology to Shinto ''kami'', the use of the term was legally abolished by the Meiji government with the {{nihongo|[[Shinbutsu bunri|Shinto and Buddhism Separation Order]]|神仏判然令|Shin-butsu Hanzenrei}}, and shrines began to be called ''jinja''.<ref name="sos3"/>

The original one is [[Nikkō Tōshō-gū]] in [[Nikkō]]. * {{nihongo|Zaō Gongen|蔵王権現}} or {{nihongo|Kongō Zaō Bosatsu|金剛蔵王菩薩}} — A deity worshiped only by Japan's [[Shugendō]] sect.<ref>Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=201 Zaō Gongen], accessed on October 6, 2008</ref>
== ''Gongen-zukuri'' == [[Image:Gongen Zukuri.png|thumb|The blueprint of a gongen-zukuri shrine]] {{main|Ishi-no-ma-zukuri}} {{nihongo|''Gongen-zukuri''|権現造}} is the name of a complex [[Shinto shrine]] structure in which the ''[[haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'', or worship hall, and the [[honden]], or main sanctuary, are interconnected under the same roof in the shape of an H.<ref name="sos2">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_g.html#gongen-zukuri ''Gongen-zukuri''] accessed on October 5, 2008</ref><ref>For details about these terms, see the article [[Shinto shrine]].</ref> One of the oldest examples of ''gongen-zukuri'' is Kitano Tenmangū in [[Kyoto]].<ref name="sos2"/> The name comes from Nikkō Tōshō-gū in Nikkō because, as we have seen, it enshrines the Tōshō Daigongen and adopts this structure.<ref name="Jaa">Jaanus, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/g/gongenzukuri.htm Gongen-zukuri], accessed on October 5, 2008</ref>

Under the influence of [[Tendai|Tendai Buddhism]] and of [[Shugendō]], the ''gongen'' concept was adapted to religious beliefs tied to [[Mount Iwaki]], a volcano, so that female ''kami'' Kuniyasutamahime became associated with Jūichimen [[Kannon Bosatsu]] (eleven-faced Kannon), ''kami'' [[Ōkuninushi]] with [[Yakushi Nyorai]], and [[Kunitokotachi]] no Mikoto with [[Amida Nyorai]].<ref>Breen, Teeuwen (2000:194)</ref>

=== Gongen of Japan === * {{nihongo|Izuna Gongen|飯網の権現}} — Also called Izuna Myōjin and enshrined in Izuna Shrine in [[Nagano]], it is similar to a [[tengu]].<ref>Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=193 Izuna Gongen], accessed on October 6, 2008</ref> * {{nihongo|Izusan Gongen|伊豆山権現}} or {{nihongo|Hashiri-yu Gongen|走湯権現}} — It's the spirit of a hot spring on Izusan, a hill in Shizuoka Prefecture<ref>Jaanus, ''Izusan Gongen'', accessed on October 6, 2008.</ref> * {{nihongo|Kumano Gongen|熊野権現}} — Also known as {{nihongo|[[Kumano Sanzan]]|熊野三山}}.<ref name="kumano">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1076 Kumano Shinkō], accessed on October 6, 2008</ref><ref>[http://www.sekaiisan-wakayama.jp/english/s_sanzan.htm Kumano Sanzan], World Heritage Registration Association, accessed on October 13, 2008</ref> The ''kami'' enshrined in the three Kumano Sanzan Grand Shrines are the three Kumano mountains: Hongū, Shingū, and Nachi.<ref name="kumano"/> * {{nihongo|Seiryū Gongen|清滝権現}} — Enshrined in Jingo-ji in Takao as tutelary ''kami'' of the [[Shingon Buddhism|Shingon sect]] by [[Kūkai]].<ref>Jaanus, ''Seiryū Gongen'', accessed on October 6, 2008</ref> * Tōshō Daigongen — One of the most famous examples of ''gongen'' is the already mentioned {{nihongo|Tōshō Daigongen|東照大権現}}, or Tokugawa Ieyasu, posthumously enshrined in so-called [[Tōshō-gū]] shrines present all over Japan. The original one is [[Nikkō Tōshō-gū]] in [[Nikkō]]. * {{nihongo|Zaō Gongen|蔵王権現}} or {{nihongo|Kongō Zaō Bosatsu|金剛蔵王菩薩}} — A deity worshiped only by Japan's [[Shugendō]] sect.<ref>Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=201 Zaō Gongen], accessed on October 6, 2008</ref>
== ''Gongen-zukuri'' == [[File:Gongen Zukuri.png|thumb|The blueprint of a gongen-zukuri shrine]] {{main|Ishi-no-ma-zukuri}} {{nihongo|''Gongen-zukuri''|権現造}} is the name of a complex [[Shinto shrine]] structure in which the ''[[haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'', or worship hall, and the [[honden]], or main sanctuary, are interconnected under the same roof in the shape of an H.<ref name="sos2">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_g.html#gongen-zukuri ''Gongen-zukuri''] accessed on October 5, 2008</ref><ref>For details about these terms, see the article [[Shinto shrine]].</ref> One of the oldest examples of ''gongen-zukuri'' is Kitano Tenmangū in [[Kyoto]].<ref name="sos2"/> The name comes from Nikkō Tōshō-gū in Nikkō because, as we have seen, it enshrines the Tōshō Daigongen and adopts this structure.<ref name="Jaa">Jaanus, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/g/gongenzukuri.htm Gongen-zukuri], accessed on October 5, 2008</ref>
== See also == * The [[Glossary of Shinto]] for an explanation of terms concerning Japanese Shinto, Shinto art, and Shinto shrine architecture.

{{Commons category|Gongen}} [[Category:Shinto]] [[Category:Japanese religions terms]] [[Category:Buddhism in Japan]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]]
[[ja:権現]] [[fi:Gongen]]

[[File:Gate-nikko-japan.jpg|thumb|250px|Nikkō Tōshō-gū enshrines [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]] under the posthumous name of Tōshō Daigongen]] {{main|Honji suijaku}} During the era of ''[[shinbutsu shūgō]]'' (religious syncretism of ''[[kami]]'' and [[Buddhahood|buddhas]]), in Japan a ( {{nihongo|'''gongen'''|権現}} was believed to be a Japanese ''[[kami]]'' which was really just the local manifestation of an Indian [[Buddhahood|buddha]], an entity who had come to guide the Japanese to [[salvation]].<ref name="SoS">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_g.html#gongen ''Gongen''] accessed on October 5, 2008</ref><ref name="TY87">Tamura (2000:87)</ref> The words {{nihongo|''gonge''|権化}} and {{nihongo|''kegen''|化現}} are [[synonym]]s of ''gongen''.<ref name="sos3"/> {{nihongo|''Gongen shinkō''|権現信仰|lit. gongen belief}} is the belief in the existence of ''gongen''.<ref name="sos3"/>
The ''gongen'' concept is the cornerstone of the ''[[honji suijaku]]'' theory, according to which Indian Buddhist deities choose to appear to the Japanese as native ''kami'' in order to save them.
== History == [[File:NikkoToriiTablet5127.jpg|left|thumb|Tablet on ''torii'' at Nikkō Tōshō-gū reads "Tōshō Daigongen" (calligraphy by [[Emperor Go-Mizunoo]])]]It is sometimes assumed that the word ''gongen'' derives from [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]]'s posthumous name (Tōshō Daigongen). The term however was created and started being used in the middle of the [[Heian period]] in an effort to harmonize Buddhism and Shinto in what is called [[shinbutsu shūgō]], or "[[syncretism]] of ''kami'' and buddhas".<ref name="TY87">Tamura (2000:87)</ref> At that time, the assumption that Japanese ''kami'' and buddhas were essentially the same evolved into a theory called {{nihongo|''[[honji suijaku]]''|本地垂迹}} which held that ''kami'' were manifestations (''avatars'') of buddhas, [[bodhisattva]]s and other Buddhist deities.<ref name="TY87"/> The theory gradually spread around the country and the concept of ''gongen'', a dual entity composed of a ''buddha'' and a ''kami'', evolved. Under the influence of [[Tendai|Tendai Buddhism]] and of [[Shugendō]], the ''gongen'' concept was adapted to religious beliefs tied to [[Mount Iwaki]], a volcano, so that female ''kami'' Kuniyasutamahime became associated with Jūichimen [[Kannon Bosatsu]] (eleven-faced Kannon), ''kami'' [[Ōkuninushi]] with [[Yakushi Nyorai]], and [[Kunitokotachi]] no Mikoto with [[Amida Nyorai]].<ref>Breen, Teeuwen (2000:194)</ref>
The title ''gongen'' started being attached to the names of ''kami'' and shrines were built within the premises of large Buddhist temples to enshrine their tutelary ''kami''.<ref name="TY87">Tamura (2000:87)</ref> During the Japanese Middle Ages, shrines started being called with the name ''gongen'' to uderline their ties to Buddhism.<ref name="sos3">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=790 Gongen shinkō], accessed on October 5, 2008</ref> For example, in Eastern Japan there are still many Hakusan shrines where the shrine itself is called either ''gongen'' or ''jinja''.<ref name="sos3"/> Because it represents the application of Buddhist terminology to Shinto ''kami'', the use of the term was legally abolished by the Meiji government with the {{nihongo|[[Shinbutsu bunri|Shinto and Buddhism Separation Order]]|神仏判然令|Shin-butsu Hanzenrei}}, and shrines began to be called ''jinja''.<ref name="sos3"/>
