The original one is [[Nikkō Tōshō-gū]] in [[Nikkō]]. * {{nihongo|Zaō Gongen|蔵王権現}} or {{nihongo|Kongō Zaō Bosatsu|金剛蔵王菩薩}} — A deity worshiped only by Japan's [[Shugendō]] sect.<ref>Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=201 Zaō Gongen], accessed on October 6, 2008</ref>
== ''Gongen-zukuri'' == [[File:Gongen Zukuri.png|thumb|The blueprint of a gongen-zukuri shrine]] {{main|Ishi-no-ma-zukuri}} {{nihongo|''Gongen-zukuri''|権現造}} is the name of a complex [[Shinto shrine]] structure in which the ''[[haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'', or worship hall, and the [[honden]], or main sanctuary, are interconnected under the same roof in the shape of an H.<ref name="sos2">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_g.html#gongen-zukuri ''Gongen-zukuri''] accessed on October 5, 2008</ref><ref>For details about these terms, see the article [[Shinto shrine]].</ref> One of the oldest examples of ''gongen-zukuri'' is Kitano Tenmangū in [[Kyoto]].<ref name="sos2"/> The name comes from Nikkō Tōshō-gū in Nikkō because, as we have seen, it enshrines the Tōshō Daigongen and adopts this structure.<ref name="Jaa">Jaanus, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/g/gongenzukuri.htm Gongen-zukuri], accessed on October 5, 2008</ref>

Under the influence of [[Tendai|Tendai Buddhism]] and of [[Shugendō]], the ''gongen'' concept was adapted to religious beliefs tied to [[Mount Iwaki]], a volcano, so that female ''kami'' Kuniyasutamahime became associated with Jūichimen [[Kannon Bosatsu]] (eleven-faced Kannon), ''kami'' [[Ōkuninushi]] with [[Yakushi Nyorai]], and [[Kunitokotachi]] no Mikoto with [[Amida Nyorai]].<ref>Breen, Teeuwen (2000:194)</ref>
The title ''gongen'' started being attached to the names of ''kami'' and shrines were built within the premises of large Buddhist temples to enshrine their tutelary ''kami''.<ref name="TY87">Tamura (2000:87)</ref> During the Japanese Middle Ages, shrines started being called with the name ''gongen'' to uderline their ties to Buddhism.<ref name="sos3">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=790 Gongen shinkō], accessed on October 5, 2008</ref> For example, in Eastern Japan there are still many Hakusan shrines where the shrine itself is called either ''gongen'' or ''jinja''.<ref name="sos3"/> Because it represents the application of Buddhist terminology to Shinto ''kami'', the use of the term was legally abolished by the Meiji government with the {{nihongo|[[Shinbutsu bunri|Shinto and Buddhism Separation Order]]|神仏判然令|Shin-butsu Hanzenrei}}, and shrines began to be called ''jinja''.<ref name="sos3"/>
=== Gongen of Japan === * {{nihongo|Izuna Gongen|飯網の権現}} — Also called Izuna Myōjin and enshrined in Izuna Shrine in [[Nagano]], it is similar to a [[tengu]].<ref>Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=193 Izuna Gongen], accessed on October 6, 2008</ref> * {{nihongo|Izusan Gongen|伊豆山権現}} or {{nihongo|Hashiri-yu Gongen|走湯権現}} — It's the spirit of a hot spring on Izusan, a hill in Shizuoka Prefecture<ref>Jaanus, ''Izusan Gongen'', accessed on October 6, 2008.</ref> * {{nihongo|Kumano Gongen|熊野権現}} — Also known as {{nihongo|[[Kumano Sanzan]]|熊野三山}}.<ref name="kumano">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1076 Kumano Shinkō], accessed on October 6, 2008</ref><ref>[http://www.sekaiisan-wakayama.jp/english/s_sanzan.htm Kumano Sanzan], World Heritage Registration Association, accessed on October 13, 2008</ref> The ''kami'' enshrined in the three Kumano Sanzan Grand Shrines are the three Kumano mountains: Hongū, Shingū, and Nachi.<ref name="kumano"/> * {{nihongo|Seiryū Gongen|清滝権現}} — Enshrined in Jingo-ji in Takao as tutelary ''kami'' of the [[Shingon Buddhism|Shingon sect]] by [[Kūkai]].<ref>Jaanus, ''Seiryū Gongen'', accessed on October 6, 2008</ref> * Tōshō Daigongen — One of the most famous examples of ''gongen'' is the already mentioned {{nihongo|Tōshō Daigongen|東照大権現}}, or Tokugawa Ieyasu, posthumously enshrined in so-called [[Tōshō-gū]] shrines present all over Japan. The original one is [[Nikkō Tōshō-gū]] in [[Nikkō]]. * {{nihongo|Zaō Gongen|蔵王権現}} or {{nihongo|Kongō Zaō Bosatsu|金剛蔵王菩薩}} — A deity worshiped only by Japan's [[Shugendō]] sect.<ref>Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=201 Zaō Gongen], accessed on October 6, 2008</ref>
== ''Gongen-zukuri'' == [[File:Gongen Zukuri.png|thumb|The blueprint of a gongen-zukuri shrine]] {{main|Ishi-no-ma-zukuri}} {{nihongo|''Gongen-zukuri''|権現造}} is the name of a complex [[Shinto shrine]] structure in which the ''[[haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'', or worship hall, and the [[honden]], or main sanctuary, are interconnected under the same roof in the shape of an H.<ref name="sos2">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_g.html#gongen-zukuri ''Gongen-zukuri''] accessed on October 5, 2008</ref><ref>For details about these terms, see the article [[Shinto shrine]].</ref> One of the oldest examples of ''gongen-zukuri'' is Kitano Tenmangū in [[Kyoto (city)|Kyoto]].<ref name="sos2"/> The name comes from Nikkō Tōshō-gū in Nikkō because, as we have seen, it enshrines the Tōshō Daigongen and adopts this structure.<ref name="Jaa">Jaanus, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/g/gongenzukuri.htm Gongen-zukuri], accessed on October 5, 2008</ref>
== See also == * The [[Glossary of Shinto]] for an explanation of terms concerning Japanese Shinto, Shinto art, and Shinto shrine architecture.

[[File:Gate-nikko-japan.jpg|thumb|250px|Nikkō Tōshō-gū enshrines [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]] under the posthumous name of Tōshō Daigongen]] {{main|Honji suijaku}} During the era of ''[[shinbutsu shūgō]]'' (religious syncretism of ''[[kami]]'' and [[Buddhahood|buddhas]]) in Japan, a {{nihongo|'''''gongen'''''|権現}} was believed to be the manifestation of an Indian [[Buddhahood|buddha]] in the form of a local ''[[kami]]'', an entity who had come to guide the people to [[salvation]].<ref name="SoS">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_g.html#gongen ''Gongen''] accessed on October 5, 2008</ref><ref name="TY87">Tamura (2000:87)</ref> The words {{nihongo|''gonge''|権化}} and {{nihongo|''kegen''|化現}} are [[synonym]]s of ''gongen''.<ref name="sos3"/> {{nihongo|''Gongen shinkō''|権現信仰|lit. gongen belief}} is the belief in the existence of ''gongen''.<ref name="sos3"/>
The ''gongen'' concept is the cornerstone of the ''[[honji suijaku]]'' theory, according to which Indian Buddhist deities choose to appear to the Japanese as native ''kami'' in order to save them.
== History == [[File:NikkoToriiTablet5127.jpg|left|thumb|Tablet on ''torii'' at Nikkō Tōshō-gū reads "Tōshō Daigongen" (calligraphy by [[Emperor Go-Mizunoo]])]]It is sometimes assumed that the word ''gongen'' derives from [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]]'s posthumous name (Tōshō Daigongen). The term however was created and started being used in the middle of the [[Heian period]] in an effort to harmonize Buddhism and Shinto in what is called [[shinbutsu shūgō]], or "[[syncretism]] of ''kami'' and buddhas".<ref name="TY87">Tamura (2000:87)</ref> At that time, the assumption that Japanese ''kami'' and buddhas were essentially the same evolved into a theory called {{nihongo|''[[honji suijaku]]''|本地垂迹}} which held that ''kami'' were manifestations (''avatars'') of buddhas, [[bodhisattva]]s and other Buddhist deities.<ref name="TY87"/> The theory gradually spread around the country and the concept of ''gongen'', a dual entity composed of a ''buddha'' and a ''kami'', evolved. Under the influence of [[Tendai|Tendai Buddhism]] and of [[Shugendō]], the ''gongen'' concept was adapted to religious beliefs tied to [[Mount Iwaki]], a volcano, so that female ''kami'' Kuniyasutamahime became associated with Jūichimen [[Kannon Bosatsu]] (eleven-faced Kannon), ''kami'' [[Ōkuninushi]] with [[Yakushi Nyorai]], and [[Kunitokotachi]] no Mikoto with [[Amida Nyorai]].<ref>Breen, Teeuwen (2000:194)</ref>
The title ''gongen'' started being attached to the names of ''kami'' and shrines were built within the premises of large Buddhist temples to enshrine their tutelary ''kami''.<ref name="TY87">Tamura (2000:87)</ref> During the Japanese Middle Ages, shrines started being called with the name ''gongen'' to uderline their ties to Buddhism.<ref name="sos3">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=790 Gongen shinkō], accessed on October 5, 2008</ref> For example, in Eastern Japan there are still many Hakusan shrines where the shrine itself is called either ''gongen'' or ''jinja''.<ref name="sos3"/> Because it represents the application of Buddhist terminology to Shinto ''kami'', the use of the term was legally abolished by the Meiji government with the {{nihongo|[[Shinbutsu bunri|Shinto and Buddhism Separation Order]]|神仏判然令|Shin-butsu Hanzenrei}}, and shrines began to be called ''jinja''.<ref name="sos3"/>
=== Gongen of Japan === * {{nihongo|Izuna Gongen|飯網の権現}} — Also called Izuna Myōjin and enshrined in Izuna Shrine in [[Nagano]], it is similar to a [[tengu]].<ref>Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=193 Izuna Gongen], accessed on October 6, 2008</ref> * {{nihongo|Izusan Gongen|伊豆山権現}} or {{nihongo|Hashiri-yu Gongen|走湯権現}} — It's the spirit of a hot spring on Izusan, a hill in Shizuoka Prefecture<ref>Jaanus, ''Izusan Gongen'', accessed on October 6, 2008.</ref> * {{nihongo|Kumano Gongen|熊野権現}} — Also known as {{nihongo|[[Kumano Sanzan]]|熊野三山}}.<ref name="kumano">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1076 Kumano Shinkō], accessed on October 6, 2008</ref><ref>[http://www.sekaiisan-wakayama.jp/english/s_sanzan.htm Kumano Sanzan], World Heritage Registration Association, accessed on October 13, 2008</ref> The ''kami'' enshrined in the three Kumano Sanzan Grand Shrines are the three Kumano mountains: Hongū, Shingū, and Nachi.<ref name="kumano"/> * {{nihongo|Seiryū Gongen|清滝権現}} — Enshrined in Jingo-ji in Takao as tutelary ''kami'' of the [[Shingon Buddhism|Shingon sect]] by [[Kūkai]].<ref>Jaanus, ''Seiryū Gongen'', accessed on October 6, 2008</ref> * Tōshō Daigongen — One of the most famous examples of ''gongen'' is the already mentioned {{nihongo|Tōshō Daigongen|東照大権現}}, or Tokugawa Ieyasu, posthumously enshrined in so-called [[Tōshō-gū]] shrines present all over Japan. The original one is [[Nikkō Tōshō-gū]] in [[Nikkō]]. * {{nihongo|Zaō Gongen|蔵王権現}} or {{nihongo|Kongō Zaō Bosatsu|金剛蔵王菩薩}} — A deity worshiped only by Japan's [[Shugendō]] sect.<ref>Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=201 Zaō Gongen], accessed on October 6, 2008</ref>
== ''Gongen-zukuri'' == [[File:Gongen Zukuri.png|thumb|The blueprint of a gongen-zukuri shrine]] {{main|Ishi-no-ma-zukuri}} {{nihongo|''Gongen-zukuri''|権現造}} is the name of a complex [[Shinto shrine]] structure in which the ''[[haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'', or worship hall, and the [[honden]], or main sanctuary, are interconnected under the same roof in the shape of an H.<ref name="sos2">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_g.html#gongen-zukuri ''Gongen-zukuri''] accessed on October 5, 2008</ref><ref>For details about these terms, see the article [[Shinto shrine]].</ref> One of the oldest examples of ''gongen-zukuri'' is Kitano Tenmangū in [[Kyoto]].<ref name="sos2"/> The name comes from Nikkō Tōshō-gū in Nikkō because, as we have seen, it enshrines the Tōshō Daigongen and adopts this structure.<ref name="Jaa">Jaanus, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/g/gongenzukuri.htm Gongen-zukuri], accessed on October 5, 2008</ref>

The term however was created and started being used in the middle of the [[Heian period]] in an effort to harmonize Buddhism and Shinto in what is called [[shinbutsu shūgō]], or "[[syncretism]] of ''kami'' and buddhas".<ref name="TY87">Tamura (2000:87)</ref> At that time, the assumption that Japanese ''kami'' and buddhas were essentially the same evolved into a theory called {{nihongo|''[[honji suijaku]]''|本地垂迹}} which held that ''kami'' were manifestations (''avatars'') of buddhas, [[bodhisattva]]s and other Buddhist deities.<ref name="TY87"/> The theory gradually spread around the country and the concept of ''gongen'', a dual entity composed of a ''buddha'' and a ''kami'', evolved. Under the influence of [[Tendai|Tendai Buddhism]] and of [[Shugendō]], the ''gongen'' concept was adapted to religious beliefs tied to [[Mount Iwaki]], a volcano, so that female ''kami'' Kuniyasutamahime became associated with Jūichimen [[Kannon Bosatsu]] (eleven-faced Kannon), ''kami'' [[Ōkuninushi]] with [[Yakushi Nyorai]], and [[Kunitokotachi]] no Mikoto with [[Amida Nyorai]].<ref>Breen, Teeuwen (2000:194)</ref>
The title ''gongen'' started being attached to the names of ''kami'' and shrines were built within the premises of large Buddhist temples to enshrine their tutelary ''kami''.<ref name="TY87">Tamura (2000:87)</ref> During the Japanese Middle Ages, shrines started being called with the name ''gongen'' to uderline their ties to Buddhism.<ref name="sos3">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=790 Gongen shinkō], accessed on October 5, 2008</ref> For example, in Eastern Japan there are still many Hakusan shrines where the shrine itself is called either ''gongen'' or ''jinja''.<ref name="sos3"/> Because it represents the application of Buddhist terminology to Shinto ''kami'', the use of the term was legally abolished by the Meiji government with the {{nihongo|[[Shinbutsu bunri|Shinto and Buddhism Separation Order]]|神仏判然令|Shin-butsu Hanzenrei}}, and shrines began to be called ''jinja''.<ref name="sos3"/>
=== Gongen of Japan === * {{nihongo|Izuna Gongen|飯網の権現}} — Also called Izuna Myōjin and enshrined in Izuna Shrine in [[Nagano]], it is similar to a [[tengu]].<ref>Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=193 Izuna Gongen], accessed on October 6, 2008</ref> * {{nihongo|Izusan Gongen|伊豆山権現}} or {{nihongo|Hashiri-yu Gongen|走湯権現}} — It's the spirit of a hot spring on Izusan, a hill in Shizuoka Prefecture<ref>Jaanus, ''Izusan Gongen'', accessed on October 6, 2008.</ref> * {{nihongo|Kumano Gongen|熊野権現}} — Also known as {{nihongo|[[Kumano Sanzan]]|熊野三山}}.<ref name="kumano">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1076 Kumano Shinkō], accessed on October 6, 2008</ref><ref>[http://www.sekaiisan-wakayama.jp/english/s_sanzan.htm Kumano Sanzan], World Heritage Registration Association, accessed on October 13, 2008</ref> The ''kami'' enshrined in the three Kumano Sanzan Grand Shrines are the three Kumano mountains: Hongū, Shingū, and Nachi.<ref name="kumano"/> * {{nihongo|Seiryū Gongen|清滝権現}} — Enshrined in Jingo-ji in Takao as tutelary ''kami'' of the [[Shingon Buddhism|Shingon sect]] by [[Kūkai]].<ref>Jaanus, ''Seiryū Gongen'', accessed on October 6, 2008</ref> * Tōshō Daigongen — One of the most famous examples of ''gongen'' is the already mentioned {{nihongo|Tōshō Daigongen|東照大権現}}, or Tokugawa Ieyasu, posthumously enshrined in so-called [[Tōshō-gū]] shrines present all over Japan. The original one is [[Nikkō Tōshō-gū]] in [[Nikkō]]. * {{nihongo|Zaō Gongen|蔵王権現}} or {{nihongo|Kongō Zaō Bosatsu|金剛蔵王菩薩}} — A deity worshiped only by Japan's [[Shugendō]] sect.<ref>Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=201 Zaō Gongen], accessed on October 6, 2008</ref>
== ''Gongen-zukuri'' == [[File:Gongen Zukuri.png|thumb|The blueprint of a gongen-zukuri shrine]] {{main|Ishi-no-ma-zukuri}} {{nihongo|''Gongen-zukuri''|権現造}} is the name of a complex [[Shinto shrine]] structure in which the ''[[haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'', or worship hall, and the [[honden]], or main sanctuary, are interconnected under the same roof in the shape of an H.<ref name="sos2">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_g.html#gongen-zukuri ''Gongen-zukuri''] accessed on October 5, 2008</ref><ref>For details about these terms, see the article [[Shinto shrine]].</ref> One of the oldest examples of ''gongen-zukuri'' is Kitano Tenmangū in [[Kyoto]].<ref name="sos2"/> The name comes from Nikkō Tōshō-gū in Nikkō because, as we have seen, it enshrines the Tōshō Daigongen and adopts this structure.<ref name="Jaa">Jaanus, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/g/gongenzukuri.htm Gongen-zukuri], accessed on October 5, 2008</ref>

[[File:Gate-nikko-japan.jpg|thumb|250px|Nikkō Tōshō-gū enshrines [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]] under the posthumous name of Tōshō Daigongen]] {{main|Honji suijaku}} During the era of [[shinbutsu-shūgō]] (religious syncretism of ''[[kami]]'' and [[Buddhahood|buddhas]]) in Japan, a {{nihongo|'''''gongen'''''|権現}} was believed to be the manifestation of an [[Buddhahood|buddha]] in the form of an indigenous [[kami]], an entity who had come to guide the people to [[salvation]].<ref name="SoS">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_g.html#gongen ''Gongen''] accessed on October 5, 2008</ref><ref name="TY87">Tamura (2000:87)</ref> The words {{nihongo|''gonge''|権化}} and {{nihongo|''kegen''|化現}} are [[synonym]]s of ''gongen''.<ref name="sos3"/> {{nihongo|''Gongen shinkō''|権現信仰|lit. gongen belief}} is the belief in the existence of ''gongen''.<ref name="sos3"/>
The ''gongen'' concept is the cornerstone of [[honji suijaku]] theory, according to which Buddhist deities choose to appear to the Japanese as native ''kami'' in order to save them, which is based on the notion of [[upaya]] or "skillful means" teaching.
== History == [[File:NikkoToriiTablet5127.jpg|left|thumb|Tablet on [[torii]] at [[Nikkō Tōshō-gū]] reads "Tōshō Daigongen" (calligraphy by [[Emperor Go-Mizunoo]])]]It is sometimes assumed that the word ''gongen'' derives from [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]]'s posthumous name (Tōshō Daigongen). However, the term was created and started being used in the middle of the [[Heian period]] in an effort to harmonize Buddhism and [[ethnic religion|indigenous religious practice]] in what is called shinbutsu-shūgō or "[[syncretism]] of kami and buddhas".<ref name="TY87">Tamura (2000:87)</ref> At that time, the assumption that Japanese ''kami'' and buddhas were essentially the same evolved into a theory called {{nihongo|''honji suijaku''|本地垂迹}}, which held that native kami were manifestations or avatars of buddhas, [[bodhisattva]]s and other Buddhist deities.<ref name="TY87"/> The theory gradually spread around the country and the concept of gongen, a dual entity composed of a buddha and a kami, evolved.
Under the influence of [[Tendai|Tendai Buddhism]] and [[Shugendō]], the gongen concept was adapted to religious beliefs tied to [[Mount Iwaki]], a volcano, so that female kami Kuniyasutamahime became associated with [[Avalokiteśvara]] ''ekadaśamukha'' (Jūichimen Kannon Bosatsu, "Eleven-Faced [[Guanyin]]"), [[Ōkuninushi]] with [[Bhaisajyaguru]] (Yakushi Nyōrai) and [[Kunitokotachi]] with [[Amitābha]] (Amida Nyōrai).<ref>Breen, Teeuwen (2000:194)</ref>
The title "gongen" started being attached to the names of kami and shrines were built within the premises of large Buddhist temples to enshrine their tutelary kami.<ref name="TY87">Tamura (2000:87)</ref> During the Japanese Middle Ages, shrines started being called with the name gongen to underline their ties to Buddhism.<ref name="sos3">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=790 Gongen shinkō], accessed on October 5, 2008</ref> For example, in Eastern Japan there are still many [[Mount Haku]] shrines where the shrine itself is called either gongen or jinja.<ref name="sos3"/> Because it represents the application of Buddhist terminology to native kami, the use of the term was legally abolished in the [[Meiji Restoration]] with the {{nihongo|[[Shinbutsu bunri|Shinto and Buddhism Separation Order]]|神仏判然令|Shin-butsu Hanzenrei}} and shrines began to be called ''jinja''.<ref name="sos3"/>
=== Gongen of Japan === * {{nihongo|Izuna Gongen|飯網の権現}}, also called "Izuna Myōjin" and enshrined in Izuna Shrine in [[Nagano, Nagano|Nagano]], is similar to a [[tengu]] and represents the kami of [[Mount Iizuna]].<ref>Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=193 Izuna Gongen], accessed on October 6, 2008</ref> * {{nihongo|Izusan Gongen|伊豆山権現}} or {{nihongo|Hashiri-yu Gongen|走湯権現}} is the spirit of a hot spring on Izusan, a hill in [[Shizuoka Prefecture]], enshrined in the [[Izusan Jinja]]<ref>Jaanus, ''Izusan Gongen'', accessed on October 6, 2008.</ref> * {{nihongo|Kumano Gongen|熊野権現}}, also known as {{nihongo|Three Mountains of Kumano|熊野三山}}.<ref name="kumano">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1076 Kumano Shinkō], accessed on October 6, 2008</ref><ref>[http://www.sekaiisan-wakayama.jp/english/s_sanzan.htm Kumano Sanzan], World Heritage Registration Association, accessed on October 13, 2008</ref>. The kami enshrined in the three Kumano Sanzan Grand Shrines and worshipped in [[Kumano shrine]]s are the three Kumano mountains: Hongū, Shingū, and Nachi.<ref name="kumano"/> * {{nihongo|Seiryū Gongen|清滝権現}} was enshrined in [[Jingo-ji]] in Takao as the tutelary kami of [[Shingon Buddhism]] by [[Kūkai]].<ref>Jaanus, ''Seiryū Gongen'', accessed on October 6, 2008</ref> * {{nihongo|Tōshō Daigongen|東照大権現}} is one of the most famous examples of gongen, representing [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]] posthumously enshrined in so-called [[Tōshō-gū]] shrines present all over Japan. The original one is [[Nikkō Tōshō-gū]] in [[Nikkō, Tochigi]]. * {{nihongo|Zaō Gongen|蔵王権現}} or {{nihongo|Kongō Zaō Bosatsu|金剛蔵王菩薩}} is a deity worshiped in Shugendō.<ref>Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=201 Zaō Gongen], accessed on October 6, 2008</ref>
== ''Gongen-zukuri'' == [[File:Gongen Zukuri.png|thumb|The blueprint of a gongen-zukuri shrine]] {{main|Ishi-no-ma-zukuri}} {{nihongo|''Gongen-zukuri''|権現造}} is the name of a complex [[Shinto shrine]] structure in which the ''[[haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'', or worship hall, and the [[honden]], or main sanctuary, are interconnected under the same roof in the shape of an H.<ref name="sos2">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_g.html#gongen-zukuri ''Gongen-zukuri''] accessed on October 5, 2008</ref><ref>For details about these terms, see the article [[Shinto shrine]].</ref> One of the oldest examples of ''gongen-zukuri'' is Kitano Tenmangū in [[Kyoto]].<ref name="sos2"/> The name comes from Nikkō Tōshō-gū in Nikkō because, as we have seen, it enshrines the Tōshō Daigongen and adopts this structure.<ref name="Jaa">Jaanus, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/g/gongenzukuri.htm Gongen-zukuri], accessed on October 5, 2008</ref>
== See also == * The [[Glossary of Shinto]] for an explanation of terms concerning Japanese Shinto, Shinto art, and Shinto shrine architecture.

[[File:Gate-nikko-japan.jpg|thumb|250px|Nikkō Tōshō-gū enshrines [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]] under the posthumous name of Tōshō Daigongen]] {{main|Honji suijaku}} During the era of [[shinbutsu-shūgō]] (religious syncretism of ''[[kami]]'' and [[Buddhahood|buddhas]]) in Japan, a {{nihongo|'''''gongen'''''|権現}} was believed to be the manifestation of an [[Buddhahood|buddha]] in the form of an indigenous [[kami]], an entity who had come to guide the people to [[salvation]].<ref name="SoS">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_g.html#gongen ''Gongen''] accessed on October 5, 2008</ref><ref name="TY87">Tamura (2000:87)</ref> The words {{nihongo|''gonge''|権化}} and {{nihongo|''kegen''|化現}} are [[synonym]]s of ''gongen''.<ref name="sos3"/> {{nihongo|''Gongen shinkō''|権現信仰|lit. gongen belief}} is the belief in the existence of ''gongen''.<ref name="sos3"/>
The ''gongen'' concept is the cornerstone of [[honji suijaku]] theory, according to which Buddhist deities choose to appear to the Japanese as native ''kami'' in order to save them, which is based on the notion of [[upaya]] or "skillful means" teaching.
== History == [[File:NikkoToriiTablet5127.jpg|left|thumb|Tablet on [[torii]] at [[Nikkō Tōshō-gū]] reads "Tōshō Daigongen" (calligraphy by [[Emperor Go-Mizunoo]])]]It is sometimes assumed that the word ''gongen'' derives from [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]]'s posthumous name (Tōshō Daigongen). However, the term was created and started being used in the middle of the [[Heian period]] in an effort to harmonize Buddhism and [[ethnic religion|indigenous religious practice]] in what is called shinbutsu-shūgō or "[[syncretism]] of kami and buddhas".<ref name="TY87">Tamura (2000:87)</ref> At that time, the assumption that Japanese ''kami'' and buddhas were essentially the same evolved into a theory called {{nihongo|''honji suijaku''|本地垂迹}}, which held that native kami were manifestations or avatars of buddhas, [[bodhisattva]]s and other Buddhist deities.<ref name="TY87"/> The theory gradually spread around the country and the concept of gongen, a dual entity composed of a buddha and a kami, evolved.
Under the influence of [[Tendai|Tendai Buddhism]] and [[Shugendō]], the gongen concept was adapted to religious beliefs tied to [[Mount Iwaki]], a volcano, so that female kami Kuniyasutamahime became associated with [[Avalokiteśvara]] ''ekadaśamukha'' (Jūichimen Kannon Bosatsu, "Eleven-Faced [[Guanyin]]"), [[Ōkuninushi]] with [[Bhaisajyaguru]] (Yakushi Nyōrai) and [[Kunitokotachi]] with [[Amitābha]] (Amida Nyōrai).<ref>Breen, Teeuwen (2000:194)</ref>
The title "gongen" started being attached to the names of kami and shrines were built within the premises of large Buddhist temples to enshrine their tutelary kami.<ref name="TY87">Tamura (2000:87)</ref> During the Japanese Middle Ages, shrines started being called with the name gongen to underline their ties to Buddhism.<ref name="sos3">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=790 Gongen shinkō], accessed on October 5, 2008</ref> For example, in Eastern Japan there are still many [[Mount Haku]] shrines where the shrine itself is called either gongen or jinja.<ref name="sos3"/> Because it represents the application of Buddhist terminology to native kami, the use of the term was legally abolished in the [[Meiji Restoration]] with the {{nihongo|[[Shinbutsu bunri|Shinto and Buddhism Separation Order]]|神仏判然令|Shin-butsu Hanzenrei}} and shrines began to be called ''jinja''.<ref name="sos3"/>
=== Gongen of Japan === * {{nihongo|Izuna Gongen|飯網の権現}}, also called "Izuna Myōjin" and enshrined in Izuna Shrine in [[Nagano, Nagano|Nagano]], is similar to a [[tengu]] and represents the kami of [[Mount Iizuna]].<ref>Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=193 Izuna Gongen], accessed on October 6, 2008</ref> * {{nihongo|Izusan Gongen|伊豆山権現}} or {{nihongo|Hashiri-yu Gongen|走湯権現}} is the spirit of a hot spring on Izusan, a hill in [[Shizuoka Prefecture]], enshrined in the [[Izusan Jinja]]<ref>Jaanus, ''Izusan Gongen'', accessed on October 6, 2008.</ref> * {{nihongo|Kumano Gongen|熊野権現}}, also known as {{nihongo|Three Mountains of Kumano|熊野三山}}.<ref name="kumano">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1076 Kumano Shinkō], accessed on October 6, 2008</ref><ref>[http://www.sekaiisan-wakayama.jp/english/s_sanzan.htm Kumano Sanzan], World Heritage Registration Association, accessed on October 13, 2008</ref> The kami enshrined in the three Kumano Sanzan Grand Shrines and worshipped in [[Kumano shrine]]s are the three Kumano mountains: Hongū, Shingū, and Nachi.<ref name="kumano"/> * {{nihongo|Seiryū Gongen|清滝権現}} was enshrined in [[Jingo-ji]] in Takao as the tutelary kami of [[Shingon Buddhism]] by [[Kūkai]].<ref>Jaanus, ''Seiryū Gongen'', accessed on October 6, 2008</ref> * {{nihongo|Tōshō Daigongen|東照大権現}} is one of the most famous examples of gongen, representing [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]] posthumously enshrined in so-called [[Tōshō-gū]] shrines present all over Japan. The original one is [[Nikkō Tōshō-gū]] in [[Nikkō, Tochigi]]. * {{nihongo|Zaō Gongen|蔵王権現}} or {{nihongo|Kongō Zaō Bosatsu|金剛蔵王菩薩}} is a deity worshiped in Shugendō.<ref>Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=201 Zaō Gongen], accessed on October 6, 2008</ref>
== ''Gongen-zukuri'' == [[File:Gongen Zukuri.png|thumb|The blueprint of a gongen-zukuri shrine]] {{main|Ishi-no-ma-zukuri}} {{nihongo|''Gongen-zukuri''|権現造}} is the name of a complex [[Shinto shrine]] structure in which the ''[[haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'', or worship hall, and the [[honden]], or main sanctuary, are interconnected under the same roof in the shape of an H.<ref name="sos2">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_g.html#gongen-zukuri ''Gongen-zukuri''] accessed on October 5, 2008</ref><ref>For details about these terms, see the article [[Shinto shrine]].</ref> One of the oldest examples of ''gongen-zukuri'' is Kitano Tenmangū in [[Kyoto]].<ref name="sos2"/> The name comes from Nikkō Tōshō-gū in Nikkō because, as we have seen, it enshrines the Tōshō Daigongen and adopts this structure.<ref name="Jaa">Jaanus, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/g/gongenzukuri.htm Gongen-zukuri], accessed on October 5, 2008</ref>

[[File:Gate-nikko-japan.jpg|thumb|250px|Nikkō Tōshō-gū enshrines [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]] under the posthumous name of Tōshō Daigongen]] {{main|Honji suijaku}} During the era of [[shinbutsu-shūgō]] (religious syncretism of ''[[kami]]'' and [[Buddhahood|buddhas]]) in Japan, a {{nihongo|'''''gongen'''''|権現}} was believed to be the manifestation of a [[Buddhahood|buddha]] in the form of an indigenous [[kami]], an entity who had come to guide the people to [[salvation]].<ref name="SoS">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_g.html#gongen ''Gongen''] accessed on October 5, 2008</ref><ref name="TY87">Tamura (2000:87)</ref> The words {{nihongo|''gonge''|権化}} and {{nihongo|''kegen''|化現}} are [[synonym]]s of ''gongen''.<ref name="sos3"/> {{nihongo|''Gongen shinkō''|権現信仰|lit. gongen belief}} is the belief in the existence of ''gongen''.<ref name="sos3"/>
The ''gongen'' concept is the cornerstone of [[honji suijaku]] theory, according to which Buddhist deities choose to appear to the Japanese as native ''kami'' in order to save them, which is based on the notion of [[upaya]] or "skillful means" teaching.

However, the term was created and started being used in the middle of the [[Heian period]] in an effort to harmonize Buddhism and [[ethnic religion|indigenous religious practice]] in what is called shinbutsu-shūgō or "[[syncretism]] of kami and buddhas".<ref name="TY87">Tamura (2000:87)</ref> At that time, the assumption that Japanese ''kami'' and buddhas were essentially the same evolved into a theory called {{nihongo|''honji suijaku''|本地垂迹}}, which held that native kami were manifestations or avatars of buddhas, [[bodhisattva]]s and other Buddhist deities.<ref name="TY87"/> The theory gradually spread around the country and the concept of gongen, a dual entity composed of a buddha and a kami, evolved.
Under the influence of [[Tendai|Tendai Buddhism]] and [[Shugendō]], the gongen concept was adapted to religious beliefs tied to [[Mount Iwaki]], a volcano, so that female kami Kuniyasutamahime became associated with [[Avalokiteśvara]] ''ekadaśamukha'' (Jūichimen Kannon Bosatsu, "Eleven-Faced [[Guanyin]]"), [[Ōkuninushi]] with [[Bhaisajyaguru]] (Yakushi Nyōrai) and [[Kunitokotachi]] with [[Amitābha]] (Amida Nyōrai).<ref>Breen, Teeuwen (2000:194)</ref>
The title "gongen" started being attached to the names of kami and shrines were built within the premises of large Buddhist temples to enshrine their tutelary kami.<ref name="TY87">Tamura (2000:87)</ref> During the Japanese Middle Ages, shrines started being called with the name gongen to underline their ties to Buddhism.<ref name="sos3">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=790 Gongen shinkō], accessed on October 5, 2008</ref> For example, in Eastern Japan there are still many [[Mount Haku]] shrines where the shrine itself is called either gongen or jinja.<ref name="sos3"/> Because it represents the application of Buddhist terminology to native kami, the use of the term was legally abolished in the [[Meiji Restoration]] with the {{nihongo|[[Shinbutsu bunri|Shinto and Buddhism Separation Order]]|神仏判然令|Shin-butsu Hanzenrei}} and shrines began to be called ''jinja''.<ref name="sos3"/>
=== Gongen of Japan === * {{nihongo|Izuna Gongen|飯網の権現}}, also called "Izuna Myōjin" and enshrined in Izuna Shrine in [[Nagano, Nagano|Nagano]], is similar to a [[tengu]] and represents the kami of [[Mount Iizuna]].<ref>Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=193 Izuna Gongen], accessed on October 6, 2008</ref> * {{nihongo|Izusan Gongen|伊豆山権現}} or {{nihongo|Hashiri-yu Gongen|走湯権現}} is the spirit of a hot spring on Izusan, a hill in [[Shizuoka Prefecture]], enshrined in the [[Izusan Jinja]]<ref>Jaanus, ''Izusan Gongen'', accessed on October 6, 2008.</ref> * {{nihongo|Kumano Gongen|熊野権現}}, also known as {{nihongo|Three Mountains of Kumano|熊野三山}}.<ref name="kumano">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1076 Kumano Shinkō], accessed on October 6, 2008</ref><ref>[http://www.sekaiisan-wakayama.jp/english/s_sanzan.htm Kumano Sanzan], World Heritage Registration Association, accessed on October 13, 2008</ref> The kami enshrined in the three Kumano Sanzan Grand Shrines and worshipped in [[Kumano shrine]]s are the three Kumano mountains: Hongū, Shingū, and Nachi.<ref name="kumano"/> * {{nihongo|Seiryū Gongen|清滝権現}} was enshrined in [[Jingo-ji]] in Takao as the tutelary kami of [[Shingon Buddhism]] by [[Kūkai]].<ref>Jaanus, ''Seiryū Gongen'', accessed on October 6, 2008</ref> * {{nihongo|Tōshō Daigongen|東照大権現}} is one of the most famous examples of gongen, representing [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]] posthumously enshrined in so-called [[Tōshō-gū]] shrines present all over Japan. The original one is [[Nikkō Tōshō-gū]] in [[Nikkō, Tochigi]]. * {{nihongo|Zaō Gongen|蔵王権現}} or {{nihongo|Kongō Zaō Bosatsu|金剛蔵王菩薩}} is a deity worshiped in Shugendō.<ref>Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=201 Zaō Gongen], accessed on October 6, 2008</ref>
== ''Gongen-zukuri'' == [[File:Gongen Zukuri.png|thumb|The blueprint of a gongen-zukuri shrine]] {{main|Ishi-no-ma-zukuri}} {{nihongo|''Gongen-zukuri''|権現造}} is the name of a complex [[Shinto shrine]] structure in which the ''[[haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'', or worship hall, and the [[honden]], or main sanctuary, are interconnected under the same roof in the shape of an H.<ref name="sos2">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_g.html#gongen-zukuri ''Gongen-zukuri''] accessed on October 5, 2008</ref><ref>For details about these terms, see the article [[Shinto shrine]].</ref> One of the oldest examples of ''gongen-zukuri'' is Kitano Tenmangū in [[Kyoto]].<ref name="sos2"/> The name comes from Nikkō Tōshō-gū in Nikkō because, as we have seen, it enshrines the Tōshō Daigongen and adopts this structure.<ref name="Jaa">Jaanus, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/g/gongenzukuri.htm Gongen-zukuri], accessed on October 5, 2008</ref>

[[File:Gate-nikko-japan.jpg|thumb|250px|Nikkō Tōshō-gū enshrines [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]] under the posthumous name of Tōshō Daigongen]] {{main|Honji suijaku}} A {{nihongo||権現|'''''gongen'''''}}, literally "incarnation", was believed to be the manifestation of a [[Buddhahood|buddha]] in the form of an indigenous [[kami]], an entity who had come to guide the people to [[salvation]], during the era of [[shinbutsu-shūgō]] in premodern Japan.<ref name="SoS">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_g.html#gongen ''Gongen''] accessed on October 5, 2008</ref><ref name="TY87">Tamura (2000:87)</ref> The words {{nihongo||権化|gonge}} and {{nihongo||化現|kegen}} are [[synonym]]s for gongen.<ref name="sos3"/> {{nihongo||権現信仰|Gongen shinkō}} is the term for belief in the existence of gongen.<ref name="sos3"/>
The gongen concept is the cornerstone of the [[honji suijaku]] theory, according to which Buddhist deities choose to appear to the Japanese as native kami in order to save them, which is based on the [[Mahayana|Mahayana Buddhist]] notion of [[upaya]], "expedient means".
== History == [[File:NikkoToriiTablet5127.jpg|left|thumb|Tablet on [[torii]] at [[Nikkō Tōshō-gū]] reads "Tōshō Daigongen" (calligraphy by [[Emperor Go-Mizunoo]])]]It is sometimes assumed that the word ''gongen'' derives from [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]]'s posthumous name (Tōshō Daigongen). However, the term was created and started being used in the middle of the [[Heian period]] in an effort to harmonize Buddhism and [[ethnic religion|indigenous religious practice]] in what is called shinbutsu-shūgō or "[[syncretism]] of kami and buddhas".<ref name="TY87">Tamura (2000:87)</ref> At that time, the assumption that Japanese ''kami'' and buddhas were essentially the same evolved into a theory called {{nihongo|''honji suijaku''|本地垂迹}}, which held that native kami were manifestations or avatars of buddhas, [[bodhisattva]]s and other Buddhist deities.<ref name="TY87"/> The theory gradually spread around the country and the concept of gongen, a dual entity composed of a buddha and a kami, evolved.
Under the influence of [[Tendai|Tendai Buddhism]] and [[Shugendō]], the gongen concept was adapted to religious beliefs tied to [[Mount Iwaki]], a volcano, so that female kami Kuniyasutamahime became associated with [[Avalokiteśvara]] ''ekadaśamukha'' (Jūichimen Kannon Bosatsu, "Eleven-Faced [[Guanyin]]"), [[Ōkuninushi]] with [[Bhaisajyaguru]] (Yakushi Nyōrai) and [[Kunitokotachi]] with [[Amitābha]] (Amida Nyōrai).<ref>Breen, Teeuwen (2000:194)</ref>
The title "gongen" started being attached to the names of kami and shrines were built within the premises of large Buddhist temples to enshrine their tutelary kami.<ref name="TY87">Tamura (2000:87)</ref> During the Japanese Middle Ages, shrines started being called with the name gongen to underline their ties to Buddhism.<ref name="sos3">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=790 Gongen shinkō], accessed on October 5, 2008</ref> For example, in Eastern Japan there are still many [[Mount Haku]] shrines where the shrine itself is called either gongen or jinja.<ref name="sos3"/> Because it represents the application of Buddhist terminology to native kami, the use of the term was legally abolished in the [[Meiji Restoration]] with the {{nihongo|[[Shinbutsu bunri|Shinto and Buddhism Separation Order]]|神仏判然令|Shin-butsu Hanzenrei}} and shrines began to be called ''jinja''.<ref name="sos3"/>
=== Gongen of Japan === * {{nihongo|Izuna Gongen|飯網の権現}}, also called "Izuna Myōjin" and enshrined in Izuna Shrine in [[Nagano, Nagano|Nagano]], is similar to a [[tengu]] and represents the kami of [[Mount Iizuna]].<ref>Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=193 Izuna Gongen], accessed on October 6, 2008</ref> * {{nihongo|Izusan Gongen|伊豆山権現}} or {{nihongo|Hashiri-yu Gongen|走湯権現}} is the spirit of a hot spring on Izusan, a hill in [[Shizuoka Prefecture]], enshrined in the [[Izusan Jinja]]<ref>Jaanus, ''Izusan Gongen'', accessed on October 6, 2008.</ref> * {{nihongo|Kumano Gongen|熊野権現}}, also known as {{nihongo|Three Mountains of Kumano|熊野三山}}.<ref name="kumano">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1076 Kumano Shinkō], accessed on October 6, 2008</ref><ref>[http://www.sekaiisan-wakayama.jp/english/s_sanzan.htm Kumano Sanzan], World Heritage Registration Association, accessed on October 13, 2008</ref> The kami enshrined in the three Kumano Sanzan Grand Shrines and worshipped in [[Kumano shrine]]s are the three Kumano mountains: Hongū, Shingū, and Nachi.<ref name="kumano"/> * {{nihongo|Seiryū Gongen|清滝権現}} was enshrined in [[Jingo-ji]] in Takao as the tutelary kami of [[Shingon Buddhism]] by [[Kūkai]].<ref>Jaanus, ''Seiryū Gongen'', accessed on October 6, 2008</ref> * {{nihongo|Tōshō Daigongen|東照大権現}} is one of the most famous examples of gongen, representing [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]] posthumously enshrined in so-called [[Tōshō-gū]] shrines present all over Japan. The original one is [[Nikkō Tōshō-gū]] in [[Nikkō, Tochigi]]. * {{nihongo|Zaō Gongen|蔵王権現}} or {{nihongo|Kongō Zaō Bosatsu|金剛蔵王菩薩}} is a deity worshiped in Shugendō.<ref>Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=201 Zaō Gongen], accessed on October 6, 2008</ref>
== ''Gongen-zukuri'' == [[File:Gongen Zukuri.png|thumb|The blueprint of a gongen-zukuri shrine]] {{main|Ishi-no-ma-zukuri}} {{nihongo|''Gongen-zukuri''|権現造}} is the name of a complex [[Shinto shrine]] structure in which the ''[[haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'', or worship hall, and the [[honden]], or main sanctuary, are interconnected under the same roof in the shape of an H.<ref name="sos2">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_g.html#gongen-zukuri ''Gongen-zukuri''] accessed on October 5, 2008</ref><ref>For details about these terms, see the article [[Shinto shrine]].</ref> One of the oldest examples of ''gongen-zukuri'' is Kitano Tenmangū in [[Kyoto]].<ref name="sos2"/> The name comes from Nikkō Tōshō-gū in Nikkō because, as we have seen, it enshrines the Tōshō Daigongen and adopts this structure.<ref name="Jaa">Jaanus, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/g/gongenzukuri.htm Gongen-zukuri], accessed on October 5, 2008</ref>

revision.text:
[[File:Gate-nikko-japan.jpg|thumb|250px|Nikkō Tōshō-gū enshrines [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]] under the posthumous name of Tōshō Daigongen]] {{main|Honji suijaku}} A {{nihongo||権現|'''''gongen'''''}}, literally "incarnation", was believed to be the manifestation of a [[Buddhahood|buddha]] in the form of an indigenous [[kami]], an entity who had come to guide the people to [[salvation]], during the era of [[shinbutsu-shūgō]] in premodern Japan.<ref name="SoS">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_g.html#gongen ''Gongen''] accessed on October 5, 2008</ref><ref name="TY87">Tamura (2000:87)</ref> The words {{nihongo||権化|gonge}} and {{nihongo||化現|kegen}} are [[synonym]]s for gongen.<ref name="sos3"/> {{nihongo||権現信仰|Gongen shinkō}} is the term for belief in the existence of gongen.<ref name="sos3"/>

Under the influence of [[Tendai|Tendai Buddhism]] and [[Shugendō]], the gongen concept was adapted to religious beliefs tied to [[Mount Iwaki]], a volcano, so that female kami Kuniyasutamahime became associated with [[Avalokiteśvara]] ''ekadaśamukha'' (Jūichimen Kannon Bosatsu, "Eleven-Faced [[Guanyin]]"), [[Ōkuninushi]] with [[Bhaisajyaguru]] (Yakushi Nyōrai) and [[Kuninotokotachi]] with [[Amitābha]] (Amida Nyōrai).<ref>Breen, Teeuwen (2000:194)</ref>
The title "gongen" started being attached to the names of kami and shrines were built within the premises of large Buddhist temples to enshrine their tutelary kami.<ref name="TY87">Tamura (2000:87)</ref> During the Japanese Middle Ages, shrines started being called with the name gongen to underline their ties to Buddhism.<ref name="sos3">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=790 Gongen shinkō], accessed on October 5, 2008</ref> For example, in Eastern Japan there are still many [[Mount Haku]] shrines where the shrine itself is called either gongen or jinja.<ref name="sos3"/> Because it represents the application of Buddhist terminology to native kami, the use of the term was legally abolished in the [[Meiji Restoration]] with the {{nihongo|[[Shinbutsu bunri|Shinto and Buddhism Separation Order]]|神仏判然令|Shin-butsu Hanzenrei}} and shrines began to be called ''jinja''.<ref name="sos3"/>
=== Gongen of Japan === * {{nihongo|Izuna Gongen|飯網の権現}}, also called "Izuna Myōjin" and enshrined in Izuna Shrine in [[Nagano, Nagano|Nagano]], is similar to a [[tengu]] and represents the kami of [[Mount Iizuna]].<ref>Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=193 Izuna Gongen], accessed on October 6, 2008</ref> * {{nihongo|Izusan Gongen|伊豆山権現}} or {{nihongo|Hashiri-yu Gongen|走湯権現}} is the spirit of a hot spring on Izusan, a hill in [[Shizuoka Prefecture]], enshrined in the [[Izusan Jinja]]<ref>Jaanus, ''Izusan Gongen'', accessed on October 6, 2008.</ref> * {{nihongo|Kumano Gongen|熊野権現}}, also known as {{nihongo|Three Mountains of Kumano|熊野三山}}.<ref name="kumano">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1076 Kumano Shinkō], accessed on October 6, 2008</ref><ref>[http://www.sekaiisan-wakayama.jp/english/s_sanzan.htm Kumano Sanzan], World Heritage Registration Association, accessed on October 13, 2008</ref> The kami enshrined in the three Kumano Sanzan Grand Shrines and worshipped in [[Kumano shrine]]s are the three Kumano mountains: Hongū, Shingū, and Nachi.<ref name="kumano"/> * {{nihongo|Seiryū Gongen|清滝権現}} was enshrined in [[Jingo-ji]] in Takao as the tutelary kami of [[Shingon Buddhism]] by [[Kūkai]].<ref>Jaanus, ''Seiryū Gongen'', accessed on October 6, 2008</ref> * {{nihongo|Tōshō Daigongen|東照大権現}} is one of the most famous examples of gongen, representing [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]] posthumously enshrined in so-called [[Tōshō-gū]] shrines present all over Japan. The original one is [[Nikkō Tōshō-gū]] in [[Nikkō, Tochigi]]. * {{nihongo|Zaō Gongen|蔵王権現}} or {{nihongo|Kongō Zaō Bosatsu|金剛蔵王菩薩}} is a deity worshiped in Shugendō.<ref>Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=201 Zaō Gongen], accessed on October 6, 2008</ref>
== ''Gongen-zukuri'' == [[File:Gongen Zukuri.png|thumb|The blueprint of a gongen-zukuri shrine]] {{main|Ishi-no-ma-zukuri}} {{nihongo|''Gongen-zukuri''|権現造}} is the name of a complex [[Shinto shrine]] structure in which the ''[[haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'', or worship hall, and the [[honden]], or main sanctuary, are interconnected under the same roof in the shape of an H.<ref name="sos2">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_g.html#gongen-zukuri ''Gongen-zukuri''] accessed on October 5, 2008</ref><ref>For details about these terms, see the article [[Shinto shrine]].</ref> One of the oldest examples of ''gongen-zukuri'' is Kitano Tenmangū in [[Kyoto]].<ref name="sos2"/> The name comes from Nikkō Tōshō-gū in Nikkō because, as we have seen, it enshrines the Tōshō Daigongen and adopts this structure.<ref name="Jaa">Jaanus, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/g/gongenzukuri.htm Gongen-zukuri], accessed on October 5, 2008</ref>

{{Italic title|reason=[[:Category:Japanese words and phrases]]}} [[File:Gate-nikko-japan.jpg|thumb|250px|Nikkō Tōshō-gū enshrines [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]] under the posthumous name of Tōshō Daigongen]] {{main|Honji suijaku}} A {{nihongo||権現|'''''gongen'''''}}, literally "incarnation", was believed to be the manifestation of a [[Buddhahood|buddha]] in the form of an indigenous [[kami]], an entity who had come to guide the people to [[salvation]], during the era of [[shinbutsu-shūgō]] in premodern Japan.<ref name="SoS">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_g.html#gongen ''Gongen''] accessed on October 5, 2008</ref><ref name="TY87">Tamura (2000:87)</ref> The words {{nihongo||権化|gonge}} and {{nihongo||化現|kegen}} are [[synonym]]s for gongen.<ref name="sos3"/> {{nihongo||権現信仰|Gongen shinkō}} is the term for belief in the existence of gongen.<ref name="sos3"/>
The gongen concept is the cornerstone of the [[honji suijaku]] theory, according to which Buddhist deities choose to appear to the Japanese as native kami in order to save them, which is based on the [[Mahayana|Mahayana Buddhist]] notion of [[upaya]], "expedient means".
== History == [[File:NikkoToriiTablet5127.jpg|left|thumb|Tablet on [[torii]] at [[Nikkō Tōshō-gū]] reads "Tōshō Daigongen" (calligraphy by [[Emperor Go-Mizunoo]])]]It is sometimes assumed that the word ''gongen'' derives from [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]]'s posthumous name (Tōshō Daigongen). However, the term was created and started being used in the middle of the [[Heian period]] in an effort to harmonize Buddhism and [[ethnic religion|indigenous religious practice]] in what is called shinbutsu-shūgō or "[[syncretism]] of kami and buddhas".<ref name="TY87">Tamura (2000:87)</ref> At that time, the assumption that Japanese ''kami'' and buddhas were essentially the same evolved into a theory called {{nihongo|''honji suijaku''|本地垂迹}}, which held that native kami were manifestations or avatars of buddhas, [[bodhisattva]]s and other Buddhist deities.<ref name="TY87"/> The theory gradually spread around the country and the concept of gongen, a dual entity composed of a buddha and a kami, evolved.
Under the influence of [[Tendai|Tendai Buddhism]] and [[Shugendō]], the gongen concept was adapted to religious beliefs tied to [[Mount Iwaki]], a volcano, so that female kami Kuniyasutamahime became associated with [[Avalokiteśvara]] ''ekadaśamukha'' (Jūichimen Kannon Bosatsu, "Eleven-Faced [[Guanyin]]"), [[Ōkuninushi]] with [[Bhaisajyaguru]] (Yakushi Nyōrai) and [[Kuninotokotachi]] with [[Amitābha]] (Amida Nyōrai).<ref>Breen, Teeuwen (2000:194)</ref>
The title "gongen" started being attached to the names of kami and shrines were built within the premises of large Buddhist temples to enshrine their tutelary kami.<ref name="TY87">Tamura (2000:87)</ref> During the Japanese Middle Ages, shrines started being called with the name gongen to underline their ties to Buddhism.<ref name="sos3">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=790 Gongen shinkō], accessed on October 5, 2008</ref> For example, in Eastern Japan there are still many [[Mount Haku]] shrines where the shrine itself is called either gongen or jinja.<ref name="sos3"/> Because it represents the application of Buddhist terminology to native kami, the use of the term was legally abolished in the [[Meiji Restoration]] with the {{nihongo|[[Shinbutsu bunri|Shinto and Buddhism Separation Order]]|神仏判然令|Shin-butsu Hanzenrei}} and shrines began to be called ''jinja''.<ref name="sos3"/>

The original one is [[Nikkō Tōshō-gū]] in [[Nikkō, Tochigi]]. * {{nihongo|Zaō Gongen|蔵王権現}} or {{nihongo|Kongō Zaō Bosatsu|金剛蔵王菩薩}} is a deity worshiped in Shugendō.<ref>Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=201 Zaō Gongen], accessed on October 6, 2008</ref>
== ''Gongen-zukuri'' == [[File:Gongen Zukuri.png|thumb|The blueprint of a gongen-zukuri shrine]] {{main|Ishi-no-ma-zukuri}} {{nihongo|''Gongen-zukuri''|権現造}} is the name of a complex [[Shinto shrine]] structure in which the ''[[haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'', or worship hall, and the [[honden]], or main sanctuary, are interconnected under the same roof in the shape of an H.<ref name="sos2">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_g.html#gongen-zukuri ''Gongen-zukuri''] accessed on October 5, 2008</ref><ref>For details about these terms, see the article [[Shinto shrine]].</ref> One of the oldest examples of ''gongen-zukuri'' is Kitano Tenmangū in [[Kyoto]].<ref name="sos2"/> The name comes from Nikkō Tōshō-gū in Nikkō because, as we have seen, it enshrines the Tōshō Daigongen and adopts this structure.<ref name="Jaa">Jaanus, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/g/gongenzukuri.htm Gongen-zukuri], accessed on October 5, 2008</ref>

Under the influence of [[Tendai|Tendai Buddhism]] and [[Shugendō]], the gongen concept was adapted to religious beliefs tied to [[Mount Iwaki]], a volcano, so that female kami Kuniyasutamahime became associated with [[Avalokiteśvara]] ''ekadaśamukha'' (Jūichimen Kannon Bosatsu, "Eleven-Faced [[Guanyin]]"), [[Ōkuninushi]] with [[Bhaisajyaguru]] (Yakushi Nyōrai) and [[Kuninotokotachi]] with [[Amitābha]] (Amida Nyōrai).<ref>Breen, Teeuwen (2000:194)</ref>
The title "gongen" started being attached to the names of kami and shrines were built within the premises of large Buddhist temples to enshrine their tutelary kami.<ref name="TY87">Tamura (2000:87)</ref> During the Japanese Middle Ages, shrines started being called with the name gongen to underline their ties to Buddhism.<ref name="sos3">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=790 Gongen shinkō], accessed on October 5, 2008</ref> For example, in Eastern Japan there are still many [[Mount Haku]] shrines where the shrine itself is called either gongen or jinja.<ref name="sos3"/> Because it represents the application of Buddhist terminology to native kami, the use of the term was legally abolished in the [[Meiji Restoration]] with the {{nihongo|[[Shinbutsu bunri|Shinto and Buddhism Separation Order]]|神仏判然令|Shin-butsu Hanzenrei}} and shrines began to be called ''jinja''.<ref name="sos3"/>
=== Gongen of Japan === * {{nihongo|Izuna Gongen|飯網の権現}}, also called "Izuna Myōjin" and enshrined in Izuna Shrine in [[Nagano, Nagano|Nagano]], is similar to a [[tengu]] and represents the kami of [[Mount Iizuna]].<ref>Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=193 Izuna Gongen], accessed on October 6, 2008</ref> * {{nihongo|Izusan Gongen|伊豆山権現}} or {{nihongo|Hashiri-yu Gongen|走湯権現}} is the spirit of a hot spring on Izusan, a hill in [[Shizuoka Prefecture]], enshrined in the [[Izusan Jinja]]<ref>Jaanus, ''Izusan Gongen'', accessed on October 6, 2008.</ref> * {{nihongo|Kumano Gongen|熊野権現}}, also known as {{nihongo|Three Mountains of Kumano|熊野三山}}.<ref name="kumano">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1076 Kumano Shinkō], accessed on October 6, 2008</ref><ref>[http://www.sekaiisan-wakayama.jp/english/s_sanzan.htm Kumano Sanzan], World Heritage Registration Association, accessed on October 13, 2008</ref> The kami enshrined in the three Kumano Sanzan Grand Shrines and worshipped in [[Kumano shrine]]s are the three Kumano mountains: Hongū, Shingū, and Nachi.<ref name="kumano"/> * {{nihongo|Seiryū Gongen|清滝権現}} was enshrined in [[Jingo-ji]] in Takao as the tutelary kami of [[Shingon Buddhism]] by [[Kūkai]].<ref>Jaanus, ''Seiryū Gongen'', accessed on October 6, 2008</ref> * {{nihongo|Tōshō Daigongen|東照大権現}} is one of the most famous examples of gongen, representing [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]] posthumously enshrined in so-called [[Tōshō-gū]] shrines present all over Japan. The original one is [[Nikkō Tōshō-gū]] in [[Nikkō, Tochigi]]. * {{nihongo|Zaō Gongen|蔵王権現}} or {{nihongo|Kongō Zaō Bosatsu|金剛蔵王菩薩}} is a deity worshiped in Shugendō.<ref>Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=201 Zaō Gongen], accessed on October 6, 2008</ref>
== ''Gongen-zukuri'' == [[File:Gongen Zukuri.png|thumb|The blueprint of a gongen-zukuri shrine]] {{main|Ishi-no-ma-zukuri}} {{nihongo|''Gongen-zukuri''|権現造}} is the name of a complex [[Shinto shrine]] structure in which the ''[[haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'', or worship hall, and the [[honden]], or main sanctuary, are interconnected under the same roof in the shape of an H.<ref name="sos2">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_g.html#gongen-zukuri ''Gongen-zukuri''] accessed on October 5, 2008</ref><ref>For details about these terms, see the article [[Shinto shrine]].</ref> One of the oldest examples of ''gongen-zukuri'' is Kitano Tenmangū in [[Kyoto]].<ref name="sos2"/> The name comes from Nikkō Tōshō-gū in Nikkō because, as we have seen, it enshrines the Tōshō Daigongen and adopts this structure.<ref name="Jaa">Jaanus, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/g/gongenzukuri.htm Gongen-zukuri], accessed on October 5, 2008</ref>

{{Italic title|reason=[[:Category:Japanese words and phrases]]}} [[File:Gate-nikko-japan.jpg|thumb|250px|Nikkō Tōshō-gū enshrines [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]] under the posthumous name of Tōshō Daigongen]] {{main|Honji suijaku}} A {{nihongo||権現|'''''gongen'''''}}, literally "incarnation", was believed to be the manifestation of a [[Buddhahood|buddha]] in the form of an indigenous [[kami]], an entity who had come to guide the people to [[salvation]], during the era of [[shinbutsu-shūgō]] in premodern Japan.<ref name="SoS">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_g.html#gongen ''Gongen''] accessed on October 5, 2008</ref><ref name="TY87">Tamura (2000:87)</ref> The words {{nihongo||権化|gonge}} and {{nihongo||化現|kegen}} are [[synonym]]s for gongen.<ref name="sos3"/> {{nihongo||権現信仰|Gongen shinkō}} is the term for belief in the existence of gongen.<ref name="sos3"/>
The gongen concept is the cornerstone of the [[honji suijaku]] theory, according to which Buddhist deities choose to appear to the Japanese as native kami in order to save them, which is based on the [[Mahayana|Mahayana Buddhist]] notion of [[upaya]], "expedient means".
== History == [[File:NikkoToriiTablet5127.jpg|left|thumb|Tablet on [[torii]] at [[Nikkō Tōshō-gū]] reads "Tōshō Daigongen" (calligraphy by [[Emperor Go-Mizunoo]])]]It is sometimes assumed that the word ''gongen'' derives from [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]]'s posthumous name (Tōshō Daigongen). However, the term was created and started being used in the middle of the [[Heian period]] in an effort to harmonize Buddhism and [[ethnic religion|indigenous religious practice]] in what is called shinbutsu-shūgō or "[[syncretism]] of kami and buddhas".<ref name="TY87">Tamura (2000:87)</ref> At that time, the assumption that Japanese ''kami'' and buddhas were essentially the same evolved into a theory called {{nihongo|''honji suijaku''|本地垂迹}}, which held that native kami were manifestations or avatars of buddhas, [[bodhisattva]]s and other Buddhist deities.<ref name="TY87"/> The theory gradually spread around the country and the concept of gongen, a dual entity composed of a buddha and a kami, evolved.
Under the influence of [[Tendai|Tendai Buddhism]] and [[Shugendō]], the gongen concept was adapted to religious beliefs tied to [[Mount Iwaki]], a volcano, so that female kami Kuniyasutamahime became associated with [[Avalokiteśvara]] ''ekadaśamukha'' (Jūichimen Kannon Bosatsu, "Eleven-Faced [[Guanyin]]"), [[Ōkuninushi]] with [[Bhaisajyaguru]] (Yakushi Nyōrai) and [[Kuninotokotachi]] with [[Amitābha]] (Amida Nyōrai).<ref>Breen, Teeuwen (2000:194)</ref>
The title "gongen" started being attached to the names of kami and shrines were built within the premises of large Buddhist temples to enshrine their tutelary kami.<ref name="TY87">Tamura (2000:87)</ref> During the Japanese Middle Ages, shrines started being called with the name gongen to underline their ties to Buddhism.<ref name="sos3">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=790 Gongen shinkō], accessed on October 5, 2008</ref> For example, in Eastern Japan there are still many [[Mount Haku]] shrines where the shrine itself is called either gongen or jinja.<ref name="sos3"/> Because it represents the application of Buddhist terminology to native kami, the use of the term was legally abolished in the [[Meiji Restoration]] with the {{nihongo|[[Shinbutsu bunri|Shinto and Buddhism Separation Order]]|神仏判然令|Shin-butsu Hanzenrei}} and shrines began to be called ''jinja''.<ref name="sos3"/>

The original one is [[Nikkō Tōshō-gū]] in [[Nikkō, Tochigi]]. * {{nihongo|Zaō Gongen|蔵王権現}} or {{nihongo|Kongō Zaō Bosatsu|金剛蔵王菩薩}} is a deity worshiped in Shugendō.<ref>Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=201 Zaō Gongen], accessed on October 6, 2008</ref>
== ''Gongen-zukuri'' == [[File:Gongen Zukuri.png|thumb|The blueprint of a gongen-zukuri shrine]] {{main|Ishi-no-ma-zukuri}} {{nihongo|''Gongen-zukuri''|権現造}} is the name of a complex [[Shinto shrine]] structure in which the ''[[haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'', or worship hall, and the [[honden]], or main sanctuary, are interconnected under the same roof in the shape of an H.<ref name="sos2">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_g.html#gongen-zukuri ''Gongen-zukuri''] accessed on October 5, 2008</ref><ref>For details about these terms, see the article [[Shinto shrine]].</ref> One of the oldest examples of ''gongen-zukuri'' is Kitano Tenmangū in [[Kyoto]].<ref name="sos2"/> The name comes from Nikkō Tōshō-gū in Nikkō because, as we have seen, it enshrines the Tōshō Daigongen and adopts this structure.<ref name="Jaa">Jaanus, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/g/gongenzukuri.htm Gongen-zukuri], accessed on October 5, 2008</ref>

Under the influence of [[Tendai|Tendai Buddhism]] and [[Shugendō]], the gongen concept was adapted to religious beliefs tied to [[Mount Iwaki]], a volcano, so that female kami Kuniyasutamahime became associated with [[Avalokiteśvara]] ''ekadaśamukha'' (Jūichimen Kannon Bosatsu, "Eleven-Faced [[Guanyin]]"), [[Ōkuninushi]] with [[Bhaisajyaguru]] (Yakushi Nyōrai) and [[Kuninotokotachi]] with [[Amitābha]] (Amida Nyōrai).<ref>Breen, Teeuwen (2000:194)</ref>
The title "gongen" started being attached to the names of kami and shrines were built within the premises of large Buddhist temples to enshrine their tutelary kami.<ref name="TY87">Tamura (2000:87)</ref> During the Japanese Middle Ages, shrines started being called with the name gongen to underline their ties to Buddhism.<ref name="sos3">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=790 Gongen shinkō], accessed on October 5, 2008</ref> For example, in Eastern Japan there are still many [[Mount Haku]] shrines where the shrine itself is called either gongen or jinja.<ref name="sos3"/> Because it represents the application of Buddhist terminology to native kami, the use of the term was legally abolished in the [[Meiji Restoration]] with the {{nihongo|[[Shinbutsu bunri|Shinto and Buddhism Separation Order]]|神仏判然令|Shin-butsu Hanzenrei}} and shrines began to be called ''jinja''.<ref name="sos3"/>
=== Gongen of Japan === * {{nihongo|Izuna Gongen|飯網の権現}}, also called "Izuna Myōjin" and enshrined in Izuna Shrine in [[Nagano, Nagano|Nagano]], is similar to a [[tengu]] and represents the kami of [[Mount Iizuna]].<ref>Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=193 Izuna Gongen], accessed on October 6, 2008</ref> * {{nihongo|Izusan Gongen|伊豆山権現}} or {{nihongo|Hashiri-yu Gongen|走湯権現}} is the spirit of a hot spring on Izusan, a hill in [[Shizuoka Prefecture]], enshrined in the [[Izusan Jinja]]<ref>Jaanus, ''Izusan Gongen'', accessed on October 6, 2008.</ref> * {{nihongo|Kumano Gongen|熊野権現}}, also known as {{nihongo|Three Mountains of Kumano|熊野三山}}.<ref name="kumano">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1076 Kumano Shinkō], accessed on October 6, 2008</ref><ref>[http://www.sekaiisan-wakayama.jp/english/s_sanzan.htm Kumano Sanzan], World Heritage Registration Association, accessed on October 13, 2008</ref> The kami enshrined in the three Kumano Sanzan Grand Shrines and worshipped in [[Kumano shrine]]s are the three Kumano mountains: Hongū, Shingū, and Nachi.<ref name="kumano"/> * {{nihongo|Seiryū Gongen|清滝権現}} was enshrined in [[Jingo-ji]] in Takao as the tutelary kami of [[Shingon Buddhism]] by [[Kūkai]].<ref>Jaanus, ''Seiryū Gongen'', accessed on October 6, 2008</ref> * {{nihongo|Tōshō Daigongen|東照大権現}} is one of the most famous examples of gongen, representing [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]] posthumously enshrined in so-called [[Tōshō-gū]] shrines present all over Japan. The original one is [[Nikkō Tōshō-gū]] in [[Nikkō, Tochigi]]. * {{nihongo|Zaō Gongen|蔵王権現}} or {{nihongo|Kongō Zaō Bosatsu|金剛蔵王菩薩}} is a deity worshiped in Shugendō.<ref>Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=201 Zaō Gongen], accessed on October 6, 2008</ref>
== ''Gongen-zukuri'' == [[File:Gongen Zukuri.png|thumb|The blueprint of a gongen-zukuri shrine]] {{main|Ishi-no-ma-zukuri}} {{nihongo|''Gongen-zukuri''|権現造}} is the name of a complex [[Shinto shrine]] structure in which the ''[[haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'', or worship hall, and the [[honden]], or main sanctuary, are interconnected under the same roof in the shape of an H.<ref name="sos2">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_g.html#gongen-zukuri ''Gongen-zukuri''] accessed on October 5, 2008</ref><ref>For details about these terms, see the article [[Shinto shrine]].</ref> One of the oldest examples of ''gongen-zukuri'' is Kitano Tenmangū in [[Kyoto]].<ref name="sos2"/> The name comes from Nikkō Tōshō-gū in Nikkō because, as we have seen, it enshrines the Tōshō Daigongen and adopts this structure.<ref name="Jaa">Jaanus, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/g/gongenzukuri.htm Gongen-zukuri], accessed on October 5, 2008</ref>
== See also == * The [[Glossary of Shinto]] for an explanation of terms concerning Japanese Shinto, Shinto art, and Shinto shrine architecture
==Notes==
{{Reflist|2}}
== References == *{{cite book |last=Tamura |first=Yoshiro |title=Japanese Buddhism — A Cultural History |publisher=Kosei Publishing Company |location=Tokyo|year=2000 |edition=First |pages=232 pages |isbn=4-333-01684-3 |language= }} * {{cite book | last = [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] | first = [[Mark Teeuwen]] (editors) | coauthors = | title = Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami | publisher = University of Hawaii Press | date = July 2000 | location = Honolulu | language = | isbn = 978-0-8248-2363-4 | oclc = 43487317 }}

{{Italic title|reason=[[:Category:Japanese words and phrases]]}} [[File:Gate-nikko-japan.jpg|thumb|250px|Nikkō Tōshō-gū enshrines [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]] under the posthumous name of Tōshō Daigongen]] {{main|Honji suijaku}} A {{nihongo||権現|'''''gongen'''''}}, literally "incarnation", was believed to be the manifestation of a [[Buddhahood|buddha]] in the form of an indigenous [[kami]], an entity who had come to guide the people to [[salvation]], during the era of [[shinbutsu-shūgō]] in premodern Japan.<ref name="SoS">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_g.html#gongen ''Gongen''] accessed on October 5, 2008</ref><ref name="TY87">Tamura (2000:87)</ref> The words {{nihongo||権化|gonge}} and {{nihongo||化現|kegen}} are [[synonym]]s for gongen.<ref name="sos3"/> {{nihongo||権現信仰|Gongen shinkō}} is the term for belief in the existence of gongen.<ref name="sos3"/>
The gongen concept is the cornerstone of the [[honji suijaku]] theory, according to which Buddhist deities choose to appear to the Japanese as native kami in order to save them, which is based on the [[Mahayana|Mahayana Buddhist]] notion of [[upaya]], "expedient means".
== History == [[File:NikkoToriiTablet5127.jpg|left|thumb|Tablet on [[torii]] at [[Nikkō Tōshō-gū]] reads "Tōshō Daigongen" (calligraphy by [[Emperor Go-Mizunoo]])]]It is sometimes assumed that the word ''gongen'' derives from [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]]'s posthumous name (Tōshō Daigongen). However, the term was created and started being used in the middle of the [[Heian period]] in an effort to harmonize Buddhism and [[ethnic religion|indigenous religious practice]] in what is called shinbutsu-shūgō or "[[syncretism]] of kami and buddhas".<ref name="TY87">Tamura (2000:87)</ref> At that time, the assumption that Japanese ''kami'' and buddhas were essentially the same evolved into a theory called {{nihongo|''honji suijaku''|本地垂迹}}, which held that native kami were manifestations or avatars of buddhas, [[bodhisattva]]s and other Buddhist deities.<ref name="TY87"/> The theory gradually spread around the country and the concept of gongen, a dual entity composed of a buddha and a kami, evolved.
Under the influence of [[Tendai|Tendai Buddhism]] and [[Shugendō]], the gongen concept was adapted to religious beliefs tied to [[Mount Iwaki]], a volcano, so that female kami Kuniyasutamahime became associated with [[Avalokiteśvara]] ''ekadaśamukha'' (Jūichimen Kannon Bosatsu, "Eleven-Faced [[Guanyin]]"), [[Ōkuninushi]] with [[Bhaisajyaguru]] (Yakushi Nyōrai) and [[Kuninotokotachi]] with [[Amitābha]] (Amida Nyōrai).<ref>Breen, Teeuwen (2000:194)</ref>
The title "gongen" started being attached to the names of kami and shrines were built within the premises of large Buddhist temples to enshrine their tutelary kami.<ref name="TY87">Tamura (2000:87)</ref> During the Japanese Middle Ages, shrines started being called with the name gongen to underline their ties to Buddhism.<ref name="sos3">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=790 Gongen shinkō], accessed on October 5, 2008</ref> For example, in Eastern Japan there are still many [[Mount Haku]] shrines where the shrine itself is called either gongen or jinja.<ref name="sos3"/> Because it represents the application of Buddhist terminology to native kami, the use of the term was legally abolished in the [[Meiji Restoration]] with the {{nihongo|[[Shinbutsu bunri|Shinto and Buddhism Separation Order]]|神仏判然令|Shin-butsu Hanzenrei}} and shrines began to be called ''jinja''.<ref name="sos3"/>
=== Gongen of Japan === * {{nihongo|Izuna Gongen|飯網の権現}}, also called "Izuna Myōjin" and enshrined in Izuna Shrine in [[Nagano, Nagano|Nagano]], is similar to a [[tengu]] and represents the kami of [[Mount Iizuna]].<ref>Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=193 Izuna Gongen], accessed on October 6, 2008</ref> * {{nihongo|Izusan Gongen|伊豆山権現}} or {{nihongo|Hashiri-yu Gongen|走湯権現}} is the spirit of a hot spring on Izusan, a hill in [[Shizuoka Prefecture]], enshrined in the [[Izusan Jinja]]<ref>Jaanus, ''Izusan Gongen'', accessed on October 6, 2008.</ref> * {{nihongo|Kumano Gongen|熊野権現}}, also known as {{nihongo|Three Mountains of Kumano|熊野三山}}.<ref name="kumano">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1076 Kumano Shinkō], accessed on October 6, 2008</ref><ref>[http://www.sekaiisan-wakayama.jp/english/s_sanzan.htm Kumano Sanzan], World Heritage Registration Association, accessed on October 13, 2008</ref> The kami enshrined in the three Kumano Sanzan Grand Shrines and worshipped in [[Kumano shrine]]s are the three Kumano mountains: Hongū, Shingū, and Nachi.<ref name="kumano"/> * {{nihongo|Seiryū Gongen|清滝権現}} was enshrined in [[Jingo-ji]] in Takao as the tutelary kami of [[Shingon Buddhism]] by [[Kūkai]].<ref>Jaanus, ''Seiryū Gongen'', accessed on October 6, 2008</ref> * {{nihongo|Tōshō Daigongen|東照大権現}} is one of the most famous examples of gongen, representing [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]] posthumously enshrined in so-called [[Tōshō-gū]] shrines present all over Japan. The original one is [[Nikkō Tōshō-gū]] in [[Nikkō, Tochigi]]. * {{nihongo|Zaō Gongen|蔵王権現}} or {{nihongo|Kongō Zaō Bosatsu|金剛蔵王菩薩}} is a deity worshiped in Shugendō.<ref>Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=201 Zaō Gongen], accessed on October 6, 2008</ref>
== ''Gongen-zukuri'' == [[File:Gongen Zukuri.png|thumb|The blueprint of a gongen-zukuri shrine]] {{main|Ishi-no-ma-zukuri}} {{nihongo|''Gongen-zukuri''|権現造}} is the name of a complex [[Shinto shrine]] structure in which the ''[[haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'', or worship hall, and the [[honden]], or main sanctuary, are interconnected under the same roof in the shape of an H.<ref name="sos2">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_g.html#gongen-zukuri ''Gongen-zukuri''] accessed on October 5, 2008</ref><ref>For details about these terms, see the article [[Shinto shrine]].</ref> One of the oldest examples of ''gongen-zukuri'' is Kitano Tenmangū in [[Kyoto]].<ref name="sos2"/> The name comes from Nikkō Tōshō-gū in Nikkō because, as we have seen, it enshrines the Tōshō Daigongen and adopts this structure.<ref name="Jaa">Jaanus, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/g/gongenzukuri.htm Gongen-zukuri], accessed on October 5, 2008</ref>
== See also == * The [[Glossary of Shinto]] for an explanation of terms concerning Japanese Shinto, Shinto art, and Shinto shrine architecture

Under the influence of [[Tendai|Tendai Buddhism]] and [[Shugendō]], the gongen concept was adapted to religious beliefs tied to [[Mount Iwaki]], a volcano, so that female kami Kuniyasutamahime became associated with [[Avalokiteśvara]] ''ekadaśamukha'' (Jūichimen Kannon Bosatsu, "Eleven-Faced [[Guanyin]]"), [[Ōkuninushi]] with [[Bhaisajyaguru]] (Yakushi Nyōrai) and [[Kuninotokotachi]] with [[Amitābha]] (Amida Nyōrai).<ref>Breen, Teeuwen (2000:194)</ref>
The title "gongen" started being attached to the names of kami and shrines were built within the premises of large Buddhist temples to enshrine their tutelary kami.<ref name="TY87">Tamura (2000:87)</ref> During the Japanese Middle Ages, shrines started being called with the name gongen to underline their ties to Buddhism.<ref name="sos3">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=790 Gongen shinkō], accessed on October 5, 2008</ref> For example, in Eastern Japan there are still many [[Mount Haku]] shrines where the shrine itself is called either gongen or jinja.<ref name="sos3"/> Because it represents the application of Buddhist terminology to native kami, the use of the term was legally abolished in the [[Meiji Restoration]] with the {{nihongo|[[Shinbutsu bunri|Shinto and Buddhism Separation Order]]|神仏判然令|Shin-butsu Hanzenrei}} and shrines began to be called ''jinja''.<ref name="sos3"/>
=== Gongen of Japan === * {{nihongo|Izuna Gongen|飯網の権現}}, also called "Izuna Myōjin" and enshrined in Izuna Shrine in [[Nagano, Nagano|Nagano]], is similar to a [[tengu]] and represents the kami of [[Mount Iizuna]].<ref>Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=193 Izuna Gongen], accessed on October 6, 2008</ref> * {{nihongo|Izusan Gongen|伊豆山権現}} or {{nihongo|Hashiri-yu Gongen|走湯権現}} is the spirit of a hot spring on Izusan, a hill in [[Shizuoka Prefecture]], enshrined in the [[Izusan Jinja]]<ref>Jaanus, ''Izusan Gongen'', accessed on October 6, 2008.</ref> * {{nihongo|Kumano Gongen|熊野権現}}, also known as {{nihongo|Three Mountains of Kumano|熊野三山}}.<ref name="kumano">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1076 Kumano Shinkō], accessed on October 6, 2008</ref><ref>[http://www.sekaiisan-wakayama.jp/english/s_sanzan.htm Kumano Sanzan] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080609225919/http://www.sekaiisan-wakayama.jp/english/s_sanzan.htm |date=2008-06-09 }}, World Heritage Registration Association, accessed on October 13, 2008</ref> The kami enshrined in the three Kumano Sanzan Grand Shrines and worshipped in [[Kumano shrine]]s are the three Kumano mountains: Hongū, Shingū, and Nachi.<ref name="kumano"/> * {{nihongo|Seiryū Gongen|清滝権現}} was enshrined in [[Jingo-ji]] in Takao as the tutelary kami of [[Shingon Buddhism]] by [[Kūkai]].<ref>Jaanus, ''Seiryū Gongen'', accessed on October 6, 2008</ref> * {{nihongo|Tōshō Daigongen|東照大権現}} is one of the most famous examples of gongen, representing [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]] posthumously enshrined in so-called [[Tōshō-gū]] shrines present all over Japan. The original one is [[Nikkō Tōshō-gū]] in [[Nikkō, Tochigi]]. * {{nihongo|Zaō Gongen|蔵王権現}} or {{nihongo|Kongō Zaō Bosatsu|金剛蔵王菩薩}} is a deity worshiped in Shugendō.<ref>Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=201 Zaō Gongen], accessed on October 6, 2008</ref>
== ''Gongen-zukuri'' == [[File:Gongen Zukuri.png|thumb|The blueprint of a gongen-zukuri shrine]] {{main|Ishi-no-ma-zukuri}} {{nihongo|''Gongen-zukuri''|権現造}} is the name of a complex [[Shinto shrine]] structure in which the ''[[haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'', or worship hall, and the [[honden]], or main sanctuary, are interconnected under the same roof in the shape of an H.<ref name="sos2">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_g.html#gongen-zukuri ''Gongen-zukuri''] accessed on October 5, 2008</ref><ref>For details about these terms, see the article [[Shinto shrine]].</ref> One of the oldest examples of ''gongen-zukuri'' is Kitano Tenmangū in [[Kyoto]].<ref name="sos2"/> The name comes from Nikkō Tōshō-gū in Nikkō because, as we have seen, it enshrines the Tōshō Daigongen and adopts this structure.<ref name="Jaa">Jaanus, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/g/gongenzukuri.htm Gongen-zukuri], accessed on October 5, 2008</ref>
