{{Italic title|reason=[[:Category:Japanese words and phrases]]}} [[File:Gate-nikko-japan.jpg|thumb|250px|Nikkō Tōshō-gū enshrines [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]] under the posthumous name of Tōshō Daigongen]] {{main|Honji suijaku}} A {{nihongo||権現|'''''gongen'''''}}, literally "incarnation", was believed to be the manifestation of a [[Buddhahood|buddha]] in the form of an indigenous [[kami]], an entity who had come to guide the people to [[salvation]], during the era of [[shinbutsu-shūgō]] in premodern Japan.<ref name="SoS">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_g.html#gongen ''Gongen''] accessed on October 5, 2008</ref><ref name="TY87">Tamura (2000:87)</ref> The words {{nihongo||権化|gonge}} and {{nihongo||化現|kegen}} are [[synonym]]s for gongen.<ref name="sos3"/> {{nihongo||権現信仰|Gongen shinkō}} is the term for belief in the existence of gongen.<ref name="sos3"/>
The gongen concept is the cornerstone of the [[honji suijaku]] theory, according to which Buddhist deities choose to appear to the Japanese as native kami in order to save them, which is based on the [[Mahayana|Mahayana Buddhist]] notion of [[upaya]], "expedient means".
== History == [[File:NikkoToriiTablet5127.jpg|left|thumb|Tablet on [[torii]] at [[Nikkō Tōshō-gū]] reads "Tōshō Daigongen" (calligraphy by [[Emperor Go-Mizunoo]])]]It is sometimes assumed that the word ''gongen'' derives from [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]]'s posthumous name (Tōshō Daigongen). However, the term was created and started being used in the middle of the [[Heian period]] in an effort to harmonize Buddhism and [[ethnic religion|indigenous religious practice]] in what is called shinbutsu-shūgō or "[[syncretism]] of kami and buddhas".<ref name="TY87">Tamura (2000:87)</ref> At that time, the assumption that Japanese ''kami'' and buddhas were essentially the same evolved into a theory called {{nihongo|''honji suijaku''|本地垂迹}}, which held that native kami were manifestations or avatars of buddhas, [[bodhisattva]]s and other Buddhist deities.<ref name="TY87"/> The theory gradually spread around the country and the concept of gongen, a dual entity composed of a buddha and a kami, evolved.
Under the influence of [[Tendai|Tendai Buddhism]] and [[Shugendō]], the gongen concept was adapted to religious beliefs tied to [[Mount Iwaki]], a volcano, so that female kami Kuniyasutamahime became associated with [[Avalokiteśvara]] ''ekadaśamukha'' (Jūichimen Kannon Bosatsu, "Eleven-Faced [[Guanyin]]"), [[Ōkuninushi]] with [[Bhaisajyaguru]] (Yakushi Nyōrai) and [[Kuninotokotachi]] with [[Amitābha]] (Amida Nyōrai).<ref>Breen, Teeuwen (2000:194)</ref>
The title "gongen" started being attached to the names of kami and shrines were built within the premises of large Buddhist temples to enshrine their tutelary kami.<ref name="TY87">Tamura (2000:87)</ref> During the Japanese Middle Ages, shrines started being called with the name gongen to underline their ties to Buddhism.<ref name="sos3">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=790 Gongen shinkō], accessed on October 5, 2008</ref> For example, in Eastern Japan there are still many [[Mount Haku]] shrines where the shrine itself is called either gongen or jinja.<ref name="sos3"/> Because it represents the application of Buddhist terminology to native kami, the use of the term was legally abolished in the [[Meiji Restoration]] with the {{nihongo|[[Shinbutsu bunri|Shinto and Buddhism Separation Order]]|神仏判然令|Shin-butsu Hanzenrei}} and shrines began to be called ''jinja''.<ref name="sos3"/>

The original one is [[Nikkō Tōshō-gū]] in [[Nikkō, Tochigi]]. * {{nihongo|Zaō Gongen|蔵王権現}} or {{nihongo|Kongō Zaō Bosatsu|金剛蔵王菩薩}} is a deity worshiped in Shugendō.<ref>Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=201 Zaō Gongen], accessed on October 6, 2008</ref>
== ''Gongen-zukuri'' == [[File:Gongen Zukuri.png|thumb|The blueprint of a gongen-zukuri shrine]] {{main|Ishi-no-ma-zukuri}} {{nihongo|''Gongen-zukuri''|権現造}} is the name of a complex [[Shinto shrine]] structure in which the ''[[haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'', or worship hall, and the [[honden]], or main sanctuary, are interconnected under the same roof in the shape of an H.<ref name="sos2">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_g.html#gongen-zukuri ''Gongen-zukuri''] accessed on October 5, 2008</ref><ref>For details about these terms, see the article [[Shinto shrine]].</ref> One of the oldest examples of ''gongen-zukuri'' is Kitano Tenmangū in [[Kyoto]].<ref name="sos2"/> The name comes from Nikkō Tōshō-gū in Nikkō because, as we have seen, it enshrines the Tōshō Daigongen and adopts this structure.<ref name="Jaa">Jaanus, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/g/gongenzukuri.htm Gongen-zukuri], accessed on October 5, 2008</ref>

Under the influence of [[Tendai|Tendai Buddhism]] and [[Shugendō]], the gongen concept was adapted to religious beliefs tied to [[Mount Iwaki]], a volcano, so that female kami Kuniyasutamahime became associated with [[Avalokiteśvara]] ''ekadaśamukha'' (Jūichimen Kannon Bosatsu, "Eleven-Faced [[Guanyin]]"), [[Ōkuninushi]] with [[Bhaisajyaguru]] (Yakushi Nyōrai) and [[Kuninotokotachi]] with [[Amitābha]] (Amida Nyōrai).<ref>Breen, Teeuwen (2000:194)</ref>
The title "gongen" started being attached to the names of kami and shrines were built within the premises of large Buddhist temples to enshrine their tutelary kami.<ref name="TY87">Tamura (2000:87)</ref> During the Japanese Middle Ages, shrines started being called with the name gongen to underline their ties to Buddhism.<ref name="sos3">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=790 Gongen shinkō], accessed on October 5, 2008</ref> For example, in Eastern Japan there are still many [[Mount Haku]] shrines where the shrine itself is called either gongen or jinja.<ref name="sos3"/> Because it represents the application of Buddhist terminology to native kami, the use of the term was legally abolished in the [[Meiji Restoration]] with the {{nihongo|[[Shinbutsu bunri|Shinto and Buddhism Separation Order]]|神仏判然令|Shin-butsu Hanzenrei}} and shrines began to be called ''jinja''.<ref name="sos3"/>
=== Gongen of Japan === * {{nihongo|Izuna Gongen|飯網の権現}}, also called "Izuna Myōjin" and enshrined in Izuna Shrine in [[Nagano, Nagano|Nagano]], is similar to a [[tengu]] and represents the kami of [[Mount Iizuna]].<ref>Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=193 Izuna Gongen], accessed on October 6, 2008</ref> * {{nihongo|Izusan Gongen|伊豆山権現}} or {{nihongo|Hashiri-yu Gongen|走湯権現}} is the spirit of a hot spring on Izusan, a hill in [[Shizuoka Prefecture]], enshrined in the [[Izusan Jinja]]<ref>Jaanus, ''Izusan Gongen'', accessed on October 6, 2008.</ref> * {{nihongo|Kumano Gongen|熊野権現}}, also known as {{nihongo|Three Mountains of Kumano|熊野三山}}.<ref name="kumano">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1076 Kumano Shinkō], accessed on October 6, 2008</ref><ref>[http://www.sekaiisan-wakayama.jp/english/s_sanzan.htm Kumano Sanzan] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080609225919/http://www.sekaiisan-wakayama.jp/english/s_sanzan.htm |date=2008-06-09 }}, World Heritage Registration Association, accessed on October 13, 2008</ref> The kami enshrined in the three Kumano Sanzan Grand Shrines and worshipped in [[Kumano shrine]]s are the three Kumano mountains: Hongū, Shingū, and Nachi.<ref name="kumano"/> * {{nihongo|Seiryū Gongen|清滝権現}} was enshrined in [[Jingo-ji]] in Takao as the tutelary kami of [[Shingon Buddhism]] by [[Kūkai]].<ref>Jaanus, ''Seiryū Gongen'', accessed on October 6, 2008</ref> * {{nihongo|Tōshō Daigongen|東照大権現}} is one of the most famous examples of gongen, representing [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]] posthumously enshrined in so-called [[Tōshō-gū]] shrines present all over Japan. The original one is [[Nikkō Tōshō-gū]] in [[Nikkō, Tochigi]]. * {{nihongo|Zaō Gongen|蔵王権現}} or {{nihongo|Kongō Zaō Bosatsu|金剛蔵王菩薩}} is a deity worshiped in Shugendō.<ref>Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=201 Zaō Gongen], accessed on October 6, 2008</ref>
== ''Gongen-zukuri'' == [[File:Gongen Zukuri.png|thumb|The blueprint of a gongen-zukuri shrine]] {{main|Ishi-no-ma-zukuri}} {{nihongo|''Gongen-zukuri''|権現造}} is the name of a complex [[Shinto shrine]] structure in which the ''[[haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'', or worship hall, and the [[honden]], or main sanctuary, are interconnected under the same roof in the shape of an H.<ref name="sos2">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_g.html#gongen-zukuri ''Gongen-zukuri''] accessed on October 5, 2008</ref><ref>For details about these terms, see the article [[Shinto shrine]].</ref> One of the oldest examples of ''gongen-zukuri'' is Kitano Tenmangū in [[Kyoto]].<ref name="sos2"/> The name comes from Nikkō Tōshō-gū in Nikkō because it enshrines the Tōshō Daigongen and adopts this structure.<ref name="Jaa">Jaanus, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/g/gongenzukuri.htm Gongen-zukuri], accessed on October 5, 2008</ref>
== See also == * The [[Glossary of Shinto]] for an explanation of terms concerning Japanese Shinto, Shinto art, and Shinto shrine architecture

revision.comment: Merging [[:Category:Japanese Buddhist terminology]] to [[:Category:Buddhism in Japan]] per [[Wikipedia:Categories for discussion/Log/2021 April 25#Buddhist terminology]]
revision.text:
{{Italic title|reason=[[:Category:Japanese words and phrases]]}} [[File:Gate-nikko-japan.jpg|thumb|250px|[[Nikkō Tōshō-gū]] enshrines [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]] under the posthumous name of Tōshō Daigongen]] {{main|Honji suijaku}} A {{nihongo||権現|'''''gongen'''''}}, literally "incarnation", was believed to be the manifestation of a [[Buddhahood|buddha]] in the form of an indigenous [[kami]], an entity who had come to guide the people to [[salvation]], during the era of [[shinbutsu-shūgō]] in premodern Japan.<ref name="SoS">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_g.html#gongen ''Gongen''] accessed on October 5, 2008</ref><ref name="TY87">Tamura (2000:87)</ref> The words {{nihongo||権化|gonge}} and {{nihongo||化現|kegen}} are [[synonym]]s for gongen.<ref name="sos3"/> {{nihongo||権現信仰|Gongen shinkō}} is the term for belief in the existence of gongen.<ref name="sos3"/>
The gongen concept is the cornerstone of the [[honji suijaku]] theory, according to which Buddhist deities choose to appear to the Japanese as native kami in order to save them, which is based on the [[Mahayana|Mahayana Buddhist]] notion of [[upaya]], "expedient means".
== History == [[File:NikkoToriiTablet5127.jpg|left|thumb|Tablet on [[torii]] at [[Nikkō Tōshō-gū]] reads "Tōshō Daigongen" (calligraphy by [[Emperor Go-Mizunoo]])]]It is sometimes assumed that the word ''gongen'' derives from [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]]'s posthumous name (Tōshō Daigongen). However, the term was created and started being used in the middle of the [[Heian period]] in an effort to harmonize Buddhism and [[ethnic religion|indigenous religious practice]] in what is called shinbutsu-shūgō or "[[syncretism]] of kami and buddhas".<ref name="TY87">Tamura (2000:87)</ref> At that time, the assumption that Japanese ''kami'' and buddhas were essentially the same evolved into a theory called {{nihongo|''honji suijaku''|本地垂迹}}, which held that native kami were manifestations or avatars of buddhas, [[bodhisattva]]s and other Buddhist deities.<ref name="TY87"/> The theory gradually spread around the country and the concept of gongen, a dual entity composed of a buddha and a kami, evolved.
Under the influence of [[Tendai|Tendai Buddhism]] and [[Shugendō]], the gongen concept was adapted to religious beliefs tied to [[Mount Iwaki]], a volcano, so that female kami Kuniyasutamahime became associated with [[Avalokiteśvara]] ''ekadaśamukha'' (Jūichimen Kannon Bosatsu, "Eleven-Faced [[Guanyin]]"), [[Ōkuninushi]] with [[Bhaisajyaguru]] (Yakushi Nyōrai) and [[Kuninotokotachi]] with [[Amitābha]] (Amida Nyōrai).<ref>Breen, Teeuwen (2000:194)</ref>
The title "gongen" started being attached to the names of kami and shrines were built within the premises of large Buddhist temples to enshrine their tutelary kami.<ref name="TY87">Tamura (2000:87)</ref> During the Japanese Middle Ages, shrines started being called with the name gongen to underline their ties to Buddhism.<ref name="sos3">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=790 Gongen shinkō], accessed on October 5, 2008</ref> For example, in Eastern Japan there are still many [[Mount Haku]] shrines where the shrine itself is called either gongen or jinja.<ref name="sos3"/> Because it represents the application of Buddhist terminology to native kami, the use of the term was legally abolished in the [[Meiji Restoration]] with the {{nihongo|[[Shinbutsu bunri|Shinto and Buddhism Separation Order]]|神仏判然令|Shin-butsu Hanzenrei}} and shrines began to be called ''jinja''.<ref name="sos3"/>
=== Gongen of Japan === * {{nihongo|Izuna Gongen|飯網の権現}}, also called "Izuna Myōjin" and enshrined in Izuna Shrine in [[Nagano, Nagano|Nagano]], is similar to a [[tengu]] and represents the kami of [[Mount Iizuna]].<ref>Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=193 Izuna Gongen], accessed on October 6, 2008</ref> * {{nihongo|Izusan Gongen|伊豆山権現}} or {{nihongo|Hashiri-yu Gongen|走湯権現}} is the spirit of a hot spring on Izusan, a hill in [[Shizuoka Prefecture]], enshrined in the [[Izusan Jinja]]<ref>Jaanus, ''Izusan Gongen'', accessed on October 6, 2008.</ref> * {{nihongo|Kumano Gongen|熊野権現}}, also known as {{nihongo|Three Mountains of Kumano|熊野三山}}.<ref name="kumano">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1076 Kumano Shinkō], accessed on October 6, 2008</ref><ref>[http://www.sekaiisan-wakayama.jp/english/s_sanzan.htm Kumano Sanzan] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080609225919/http://www.sekaiisan-wakayama.jp/english/s_sanzan.htm |date=2008-06-09 }}, World Heritage Registration Association, accessed on October 13, 2008</ref> The kami enshrined in the three Kumano Sanzan Grand Shrines and worshipped in [[Kumano shrine]]s are the three Kumano mountains: Hongū, Shingū, and Nachi.<ref name="kumano"/> * {{nihongo|Seiryū Gongen|清滝権現}} was enshrined in [[Jingo-ji]] in Takao as the tutelary kami of [[Shingon Buddhism]] by [[Kūkai]].<ref>Jaanus, ''Seiryū Gongen'', accessed on October 6, 2008</ref> * {{nihongo|Tōshō Daigongen|東照大権現}} is one of the most famous examples of gongen, representing [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]] posthumously enshrined in so-called [[Tōshō-gū]] shrines present all over Japan. The original one is [[Nikkō Tōshō-gū]] in [[Nikkō, Tochigi]]. * {{nihongo|Zaō Gongen|蔵王権現}} or {{nihongo|Kongō Zaō Bosatsu|金剛蔵王菩薩}} is a deity worshiped in Shugendō.<ref>Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=201 Zaō Gongen], accessed on October 6, 2008</ref>
== ''Gongen-zukuri'' == [[File:Gongen Zukuri.png|thumb|The blueprint of a gongen-zukuri shrine]] {{main|Ishi-no-ma-zukuri}} {{nihongo|''Gongen-zukuri''|権現造}} is the name of a complex [[Shinto shrine]] structure in which the ''[[haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'', or worship hall, and the [[honden]], or main sanctuary, are interconnected under the same roof in the shape of an H.<ref name="sos2">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_g.html#gongen-zukuri ''Gongen-zukuri''] accessed on October 5, 2008</ref><ref>For details about these terms, see the article [[Shinto shrine]].</ref> One of the oldest examples of ''gongen-zukuri'' is Kitano Tenmangū in [[Kyoto]].<ref name="sos2"/> The name comes from Nikkō Tōshō-gū in Nikkō because it enshrines the Tōshō Daigongen and adopts this structure.<ref name="Jaa">Jaanus, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/g/gongenzukuri.htm Gongen-zukuri], accessed on October 5, 2008</ref>
== See also == * The [[Glossary of Shinto]] for an explanation of terms concerning Japanese Shinto, Shinto art, and Shinto shrine architecture

{{Italic title|reason=[[:Category:Japanese words and phrases]]}} [[File:Gate-nikko-japan.jpg|thumb|250px|[[Nikkō Tōshō-gū]] enshrines [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]] under the posthumous name of Tōshō Daigongen]] {{main|Honji suijaku}} A {{nihongo||権現|'''''gongen'''''}}, literally "incarnation", was believed to be the manifestation of a [[Buddhahood|buddha]] in the form of an indigenous [[kami]], an entity who had come to guide the people to [[salvation]], during the era of [[shinbutsu-shūgō]] in premodern Japan.<ref name="SoS">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_g.html#gongen ''Gongen''] accessed on October 5, 2008</ref><ref name="TY87">Tamura (2000:87)</ref> The words {{nihongo||権化|gonge}} and {{nihongo||化現|kegen}} are [[synonym]]s for gongen.<ref name="sos3"/> {{nihongo||権現信仰|Gongen shinkō}} is the term for belief in the existence of gongen.<ref name="sos3"/>

However, the term was created and started being used in the middle of the [[Heian period]] in an effort to harmonize Buddhism and [[ethnic religion|indigenous religious practice]] in what is called shinbutsu-shūgō or "[[syncretism]] of kami and buddhas".<ref name="TY87">Tamura (2000:87)</ref> At that time, the assumption that Japanese ''kami'' and buddhas were essentially the same evolved into a theory called {{nihongo|''honji suijaku''|本地垂迹}}, which held that native kami were manifestations or avatars of buddhas, [[bodhisattva]]s and other Buddhist deities.<ref name="TY87"/> The theory gradually spread around the country and the concept of gongen, a dual entity composed of a buddha and a kami, evolved.
Under the influence of [[Tendai|Tendai Buddhism]] and [[Shugendō]], the gongen concept was adapted to religious beliefs tied to [[Mount Iwaki]], a volcano, so that female kami Kuniyasutamahime became associated with [[Avalokiteśvara]] ''ekadaśamukha'' (Jūichimen Kannon Bosatsu, "Eleven-Faced [[Guanyin]]"), [[Ōkuninushi]] with [[Bhaisajyaguru]] (Yakushi Nyōrai) and [[Kuninotokotachi]] with [[Amitābha]] (Amida Nyōrai).<ref>Breen, Teeuwen (2000:194)</ref>
The title "gongen" started being attached to the names of kami and shrines were built within the premises of large Buddhist temples to enshrine their tutelary kami.<ref name="TY87">Tamura (2000:87)</ref> During the Japanese Middle Ages, shrines started being called with the name gongen to underline their ties to Buddhism.<ref name="sos3">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=790 Gongen shinkō], accessed on October 5, 2008</ref> For example, in Eastern Japan there are still many [[Mount Haku]] shrines where the shrine itself is called either gongen or jinja.<ref name="sos3"/> Because it represents the application of Buddhist terminology to native kami, the use of the term was legally abolished in the [[Meiji Restoration]] with the {{nihongo|[[Shinbutsu bunri|Shinto and Buddhism Separation Order]]|神仏判然令|Shin-butsu Hanzenrei}} and shrines began to be called ''jinja''.<ref name="sos3"/>
=== Gongen of Japan === * {{nihongo|Izuna Gongen|飯網の権現}}, also called "Izuna Myōjin" and enshrined in Izuna Shrine in [[Nagano, Nagano|Nagano]], is similar to a [[tengu]] and represents the kami of [[Mount Iizuna]].<ref>Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=193 Izuna Gongen], accessed on October 6, 2008</ref> * {{nihongo|Izusan Gongen|伊豆山権現}} or {{nihongo|Hashiri-yu Gongen|走湯権現}} is the spirit of a hot spring on Izusan, a hill in [[Shizuoka Prefecture]], enshrined in the [[Izusan Jinja]]<ref>Jaanus, ''Izusan Gongen'', accessed on October 6, 2008.</ref> * {{nihongo|Kumano Gongen|熊野権現}}, also known as {{nihongo|Three Mountains of Kumano|熊野三山}}.<ref name="kumano">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1076 Kumano Shinkō], accessed on October 6, 2008</ref><ref>[http://www.sekaiisan-wakayama.jp/english/s_sanzan.htm Kumano Sanzan] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080609225919/http://www.sekaiisan-wakayama.jp/english/s_sanzan.htm |date=2008-06-09 }}, World Heritage Registration Association, accessed on October 13, 2008</ref> The kami enshrined in the three Kumano Sanzan Grand Shrines and worshipped in [[Kumano shrine]]s are the three Kumano mountains: Hongū, Shingū, and Nachi.<ref name="kumano"/> * {{nihongo|Seiryū Gongen|清滝権現}} was enshrined in [[Jingo-ji]] in Takao as the tutelary kami of [[Shingon Buddhism]] by [[Kūkai]].<ref>Jaanus, ''Seiryū Gongen'', accessed on October 6, 2008</ref> * {{nihongo|Tōshō Daigongen|東照大権現}} is one of the most famous examples of gongen, representing [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]] posthumously enshrined in so-called [[Tōshō-gū]] shrines present all over Japan. The original one is [[Nikkō Tōshō-gū]] in [[Nikkō, Tochigi]]. * {{nihongo|Zaō Gongen|蔵王権現}} or {{nihongo|Kongō Zaō Bosatsu|金剛蔵王菩薩}} is a deity worshiped in [[Shugendō]].<ref>Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=201 Zaō Gongen], accessed on October 6, 2008</ref> * {{nihongo|Sannō Gongen|山王権現}} or {{nihongo|Hiyoshi Sannō Daigongen|日吉山王大権現}} is a guardian deity worshiped in [[Tendai]] spread from [[Mount Hiei]].
== ''Gongen-zukuri'' == [[File:Gongen Zukuri.png|thumb|Plan of a gongen-zukuri shrine]] {{main|Ishi-no-ma-zukuri}} {{nihongo|''Gongen-zukuri''|権現造}} is the name of a complex [[Shinto shrine]] structure in which the ''[[haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'', or worship hall, and the [[honden]], or main sanctuary, are interconnected under the same roof in the shape of an H.<ref name="sos2">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_g.html#gongen-zukuri ''Gongen-zukuri''] accessed on October 5, 2008</ref><ref>For details about these terms, see the article [[Shinto shrine]].</ref> One of the oldest examples of ''gongen-zukuri'' is Kitano Tenmangū in [[Kyoto]].<ref name="sos2"/> The name comes from Nikkō Tōshō-gū in Nikkō because it enshrines the Tōshō Daigongen and adopts this structure.<ref name="Jaa">Jaanus, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/g/gongenzukuri.htm Gongen-zukuri], accessed on October 5, 2008</ref>
== See also == * The [[Glossary of Shinto]] for an explanation of terms concerning Japanese Shinto, Shinto art, and Shinto shrine architecture

{{Italic title|reason=[[:Category:Japanese words and phrases]]}} [[File:Gate-nikko-japan.jpg|thumb|250px|[[Nikkō Tōshō-gū]] enshrines [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]] under the posthumous name of Tōshō Daigongen]] {{main|Honji suijaku}} A {{nihongo||権現|'''''gongen'''''}}, literally "incarnation", was believed to be the manifestation of a [[Buddhahood|buddha]] in the form of an indigenous [[kami]], an entity who had come to guide the people to [[salvation]], during the era of [[shinbutsu-shūgō]] in premodern Japan.<ref name="SoS">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_g.html#gongen ''Gongen''] accessed on October 5, 2008</ref><ref name="TY87">Tamura (2000:87)</ref> The words {{nihongo||権化|gonge}} and {{nihongo||化現|kegen}} are [[synonym]]s for gongen.<ref name="sos3"/> {{nihongo||権現信仰|Gongen shinkō}} is the term for belief in the existence of gongen.<ref name="sos3"/>
The gongen concept is the cornerstone of the [[honji suijaku]] theory, according to which Buddhist deities choose to appear to the Japanese as native kami in order to save them, which is based on the [[Mahayana|Mahayana Buddhist]] notion of [[upaya]], "expedient means".

== History == [[File:NikkoToriiTablet5127.jpg|left|thumb|Tablet on [[torii]] at [[Nikkō Tōshō-gū]] reads "Tōshō Daigongen" (calligraphy by [[Emperor Go-Mizunoo]])]]It is sometimes assumed that the word ''gongen'' derives from [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]]'s posthumous name (Tōshō Daigongen). However, the term was created and started being used in the middle of the [[Heian period]] in an effort to harmonize Buddhism and [[ethnic religion|indigenous religious practice]] in what is called shinbutsu-shūgō or "[[syncretism]] of kami and buddhas".<ref name="TY87">Tamura (2000:87)</ref> At that time, the assumption that Japanese ''kami'' and buddhas were essentially the same evolved into a theory called {{nihongo|''honji suijaku''|本地垂迹}}, which held that native kami were manifestations or avatars of buddhas, [[bodhisattva]]s and other Buddhist deities.<ref name="TY87"/> The theory gradually spread around the country and the concept of gongen, a dual entity composed of a buddha and a kami, evolved.
Under the influence of [[Tendai|Tendai Buddhism]] and [[Shugendō]], the gongen concept was adapted to religious beliefs tied to [[Mount Iwaki]], a volcano, so that female kami Kuniyasutamahime became associated with [[Avalokiteśvara]] ''ekadaśamukha'' (Jūichimen Kannon Bosatsu, "Eleven-Faced [[Guanyin]]"), [[Ōkuninushi]] with [[Bhaisajyaguru]] (Yakushi Nyōrai) and [[Kuninotokotachi]] with [[Amitābha]] (Amida Nyōrai).<ref>Breen, Teeuwen (2000:194)</ref>
The title "gongen" started being attached to the names of kami and shrines were built within the premises of large Buddhist temples to enshrine their tutelary kami.<ref name="TY87">Tamura (2000:87)</ref> During the Japanese Middle Ages, shrines started being called with the name gongen to underline their ties to Buddhism.<ref name="sos3">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=790 Gongen shinkō], accessed on October 5, 2008</ref> For example, in Eastern Japan there are still many [[Mount Haku]] shrines where the shrine itself is called either gongen or jinja.<ref name="sos3"/> Because it represents the application of Buddhist terminology to native kami, the use of the term was legally abolished in the [[Meiji Restoration]] with the {{nihongo|[[Shinbutsu bunri|Shinto and Buddhism Separation Order]]|神仏判然令|Shin-butsu Hanzenrei}} and shrines began to be called ''jinja''.<ref name="sos3"/>
=== Gongen of Japan === * {{nihongo|Izuna Gongen|飯網の権現}}, also called "Izuna Myōjin" and enshrined in Izuna Shrine in [[Nagano, Nagano|Nagano]], is similar to a [[tengu]] and represents the kami of [[Mount Iizuna]].<ref>Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=193 Izuna Gongen], accessed on October 6, 2008</ref> * {{nihongo|Izusan Gongen|伊豆山権現}} or {{nihongo|Hashiri-yu Gongen|走湯権現}} is the spirit of a hot spring on Izusan, a hill in [[Shizuoka Prefecture]], enshrined in the [[Izusan Jinja]]<ref>Jaanus, ''Izusan Gongen'', accessed on October 6, 2008.</ref> * {{nihongo|Kumano Gongen|熊野権現}}, also known as {{nihongo|Three Mountains of Kumano|熊野三山}}.<ref name="kumano">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1076 Kumano Shinkō], accessed on October 6, 2008</ref><ref>[http://www.sekaiisan-wakayama.jp/english/s_sanzan.htm Kumano Sanzan] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080609225919/http://www.sekaiisan-wakayama.jp/english/s_sanzan.htm |date=2008-06-09 }}, World Heritage Registration Association, accessed on October 13, 2008</ref> The kami enshrined in the three Kumano Sanzan Grand Shrines and worshipped in [[Kumano shrine]]s are the three Kumano mountains: Hongū, Shingū, and Nachi.<ref name="kumano"/> * {{nihongo|Seiryū Gongen|清滝権現}} was enshrined in [[Jingo-ji]] in Takao as the tutelary kami of [[Shingon Buddhism]] by [[Kūkai]].<ref>Jaanus, ''Seiryū Gongen'', accessed on October 6, 2008</ref> * {{nihongo|Tōshō Daigongen|東照大権現}} is one of the most famous examples of gongen, representing [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]] posthumously enshrined in so-called [[Tōshō-gū]] shrines present all over Japan. The original one is [[Nikkō Tōshō-gū]] in [[Nikkō, Tochigi]]. * {{nihongo|Zaō Gongen|蔵王権現}} or {{nihongo|Kongō Zaō Bosatsu|金剛蔵王菩薩}} is a deity worshiped in [[Shugendō]].<ref>Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=201 Zaō Gongen], accessed on October 6, 2008</ref> * {{nihongo|Sannō Gongen|山王権現}} or {{nihongo|Hie (alternatively ''Hiyoshi'') Sannō Daigongen|日吉山王大権現}} is a guardian deity worshiped in [[Tendai]] spread from [[Mount Hiei]]. It is treated as a Buddhist title of Shinto [[Oyamakui no Kami]].
== ''Gongen-zukuri'' == [[File:Gongen Zukuri.png|thumb|Plan of a gongen-zukuri shrine]] {{main|Ishi-no-ma-zukuri}} {{nihongo|''Gongen-zukuri''|権現造}} is the name of a complex [[Shinto shrine]] structure in which the ''[[haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'', or worship hall, and the [[honden]], or main sanctuary, are interconnected under the same roof in the shape of an H.<ref name="sos2">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_g.html#gongen-zukuri ''Gongen-zukuri''] accessed on October 5, 2008</ref><ref>For details about these terms, see the article [[Shinto shrine]].</ref> One of the oldest examples of ''gongen-zukuri'' is Kitano Tenmangū in [[Kyoto]].<ref name="sos2"/> The name comes from Nikkō Tōshō-gū in Nikkō because it enshrines the Tōshō Daigongen and adopts this structure.<ref name="Jaa">Jaanus, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/g/gongenzukuri.htm Gongen-zukuri], accessed on October 5, 2008</ref>
== See also == * The [[Glossary of Shinto]] for an explanation of terms concerning Japanese Shinto, Shinto art, and Shinto shrine architecture

{{Italic title|reason=[[:Category:Japanese words and phrases]]}} [[File:Gate-nikko-japan.jpg|thumb|250px|[[Nikkō Tōshō-gū]] enshrines [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]] under the posthumous name of Tōshō Daigongen]] {{main|Honji suijaku}} A {{nihongo||権現|'''''gongen'''''}}, literally "incarnation", was believed to be the manifestation of a [[Buddhahood|buddha]] in the form of an indigenous [[kami]], an entity who had come to guide the people to [[salvation]], during the era of [[shinbutsu-shūgō]] in premodern Japan.<ref name="SoS">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_g.html#gongen ''Gongen''] accessed on October 5, 2008</ref><ref name="TY87">Tamura (2000:87)</ref> The words {{nihongo||権化|gonge}} and {{nihongo||化現|kegen}} are [[synonym]]s for gongen.<ref name="sos3"/> {{nihongo||権現信仰|Gongen shinkō}} is the term for belief in the existence of gongen.<ref name="sos3"/>
The gongen concept is the cornerstone of the [[honji suijaku]] theory, according to which Buddhist deities choose to appear to the Japanese as native kami in order to save them, which is based on the [[Mahayana|Mahayana Buddhist]] notion of [[upaya]], "expedient means".

The title "gongen" started being attached to the names of kami and shrines were built within the premises of large Buddhist temples to enshrine their tutelary kami.<ref name="TY87">Tamura (2000:87)</ref> During the Japanese Middle Ages, shrines started being called with the name gongen to underline their ties to Buddhism.<ref name="sos3">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=790 Gongen shinkō], accessed on October 5, 2008</ref> For example, in Eastern Japan there are still many [[Mount Haku]] shrines where the shrine itself is called either gongen or jinja.<ref name="sos3"/> Because it represents the application of Buddhist terminology to native kami, the use of the term was legally abolished in the [[Meiji Restoration]] with the {{nihongo|[[Shinbutsu bunri|Shinto and Buddhism Separation Order]]|神仏判然令|Shin-butsu Hanzenrei}} and shrines began to be called ''jinja''.<ref name="sos3"/>

The gongen concept is the cornerstone of the [[honji suijaku]] theory, according to which Buddhist deities choose to appear to the Japanese as native kami in order to save them, which is based on the [[Mahayana|Mahayana Buddhist]] notion of [[upaya]], "expedient means".
== History == [[File:NikkoToriiTablet5127.jpg|left|thumb|Tablet on [[torii]] at [[Nikkō Tōshō-gū]] reads "Tōshō Daigongen" (calligraphy by [[Emperor Go-Mizunoo]])]]It is sometimes assumed that the word ''gongen'' derives from [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]]'s posthumous name (Tōshō Daigongen). However, the term was created and started being used in the middle of the [[Heian period]] in an effort to harmonize Buddhism and [[ethnic religion|indigenous religious practice]] in what is called shinbutsu-shūgō or "[[syncretism]] of kami and buddhas".<ref name="TY87">Tamura (2000:87)</ref> At that time, the assumption that Japanese ''kami'' and buddhas were essentially the same evolved into a theory called {{nihongo|''honji suijaku''|本地垂迹}}, which held that native kami were manifestations or avatars of buddhas, [[bodhisattva]]s and other Buddhist deities.<ref name="TY87"/> The theory gradually spread around the country and the concept of gongen, a dual entity composed of a buddha and a kami, evolved.
Under the influence of [[Tendai|Tendai Buddhism]] and [[Shugendō]], the gongen concept was adapted to religious beliefs tied to [[Mount Iwaki]], a volcano, so that female kami Kuniyasutamahime became associated with [[Avalokiteśvara]] ''ekadaśamukha'' (Jūichimen Kannon Bosatsu, "Eleven-Faced [[Guanyin]]"), [[Ōkuninushi]] with [[Bhaisajyaguru]] (Yakushi Nyōrai) and [[Kuninotokotachi]] with [[Amitābha]] (Amida Nyōrai).<ref>Breen, Teeuwen (2000:194)</ref>
The title "gongen" started being attached to the names of kami and shrines were built within the premises of large Buddhist temples to enshrine their tutelary kami.<ref name="TY87">Tamura (2000:87)</ref> During the Japanese Middle Ages, shrines started being called with the name gongen to underline their ties to Buddhism.<ref name="sos3">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=790 Gongen shinkō], accessed on October 5, 2008</ref> For example, in Eastern Japan there are still many [[Mount Haku]] shrines where the shrine itself is called either gongen or jinja.<ref name="sos3"/> Because it represents the application of Buddhist terminology to native kami, the use of the term was legally abolished in the [[Meiji Restoration]] with the {{nihongo|[[Shinbutsu bunri|Shinto and Buddhism Separation Order]]|神仏判然令|Shin-butsu Hanzenrei}} and shrines began to be called ''jinja''.<ref name="sos3"/>
=== Gongen of Japan === * {{nihongo|Izuna Gongen|飯網の権現}}, also called "Izuna Myōjin" and enshrined in Izuna Shrine in [[Nagano, Nagano|Nagano]], is similar to a [[tengu]] and represents the kami of [[Mount Iizuna]].<ref>Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=193 Izuna Gongen], accessed on October 6, 2008</ref> * {{nihongo|Izusan Gongen|伊豆山権現}} or {{nihongo|Hashiri-yu Gongen|走湯権現}} is the spirit of a hot spring on Izusan, a hill in [[Shizuoka Prefecture]], enshrined in the [[Izusan Jinja]]<ref>Jaanus, ''Izusan Gongen'', accessed on October 6, 2008.</ref> * {{nihongo|Kumano Gongen|熊野権現}}, also known as {{nihongo|Three Mountains of Kumano|熊野三山}}.<ref name="kumano">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1076 Kumano Shinkō], accessed on October 6, 2008</ref><ref>[http://www.sekaiisan-wakayama.jp/english/s_sanzan.htm Kumano Sanzan] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080609225919/http://www.sekaiisan-wakayama.jp/english/s_sanzan.htm |date=2008-06-09 }}, World Heritage Registration Association, accessed on October 13, 2008</ref> The kami enshrined in the three Kumano Sanzan Grand Shrines and worshipped in [[Kumano shrine]]s are the three Kumano mountains: Hongū, Shingū, and Nachi.<ref name="kumano"/> * {{nihongo|Seiryū Gongen|清滝権現}} was enshrined in [[Jingo-ji]] in Takao as the tutelary kami of [[Shingon Buddhism]] by [[Kūkai]].<ref>Jaanus, ''Seiryū Gongen'', accessed on October 6, 2008</ref> * {{nihongo|Tōshō Daigongen|東照大権現}} is one of the most famous examples of gongen, representing [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]] posthumously enshrined in so-called [[Tōshō-gū]] shrines present all over Japan. The original one is [[Nikkō Tōshō-gū]] in [[Nikkō, Tochigi]]. * {{nihongo|Zaō Gongen|蔵王権現}} or {{nihongo|Kongō Zaō Bosatsu|金剛蔵王菩薩}} is a deity worshiped in [[Shugendō]].<ref>Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=201 Zaō Gongen], accessed on October 6, 2008</ref> * {{nihongo|Sannō Gongen|山王権現}} or {{nihongo|Hie (alternatively ''Hiyoshi'') Sannō Daigongen|日吉山王大権現}} is a guardian deity worshiped in [[Tendai]] spread from [[Mount Hiei]]. It is treated as a Buddhist title of Shinto [[Oyamakui no Kami]].
== ''Gongen-zukuri'' == [[File:Gongen Zukuri.png|thumb|Plan of a gongen-zukuri shrine]] {{main|Ishi-no-ma-zukuri}} {{nihongo|''Gongen-zukuri''|権現造}} is the name of a complex [[Shinto shrine]] structure in which the ''[[haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'', or worship hall, and the [[honden]], or main sanctuary, are interconnected under the same roof in the shape of an H.<ref name="sos2">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_g.html#gongen-zukuri ''Gongen-zukuri''] accessed on October 5, 2008</ref><ref>For details about these terms, see the article [[Shinto shrine]].</ref> One of the oldest examples of ''gongen-zukuri'' is Kitano Tenmangū in [[Kyoto]].<ref name="sos2"/> The name comes from Nikkō Tōshō-gū in Nikkō because it enshrines the Tōshō Daigongen and adopts this structure.<ref name="Jaa">Jaanus, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/g/gongenzukuri.htm Gongen-zukuri], accessed on October 5, 2008</ref>
== See also == * The [[Glossary of Shinto]] for an explanation of terms concerning Japanese Shinto, Shinto art, and Shinto shrine architecture

{{Italic title|reason=[[:Category:Japanese words and phrases]]}} [[File:Gate-nikko-japan.jpg|thumb|250px|[[Nikkō Tōshō-gū]] enshrines [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]] under the posthumous name of Tōshō Daigongen]] {{main|Honji suijaku}} A {{nihongo||権現|'''''gongen'''''}}, literally "incarnation", was believed to be the manifestation of a [[Buddhahood|buddha]] in the form of an indigenous [[kami]], an entity who had come to guide the people to [[salvation]], during the era of [[shinbutsu-shūgō]] in premodern Japan.<ref name="SoS">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_g.html#gongen ''Gongen''] accessed on October 5, 2008</ref><ref name="TY87">Tamura (2000:87)</ref> The words {{nihongo||権化|gonge}} and {{nihongo||化現|kegen}} are [[synonym]]s for gongen.<ref name="sos3"/> {{nihongo||権現信仰|Gongen shinkō}} is the term for belief in the existence of gongen.<ref name="sos3"/>
The gongen concept is the cornerstone of the [[honji suijaku]] theory, according to which Buddhist deities choose to appear to the Japanese as native kami in order to save them, which is based on the [[Mahayana|Mahayana Buddhist]] notion of [[upaya]], "expedient means".

Under the influence of [[Tendai|Tendai Buddhism]] and [[Shugendō]], the gongen concept was adapted to religious beliefs tied to [[Mount Iwaki]], a volcano, so that female kami Kuniyasutamahime became associated with [[Avalokiteśvara]] ''ekadaśamukha'' (Jūichimen Kannon Bosatsu, "Eleven-Faced [[Guanyin]]"), [[Ōkuninushi]] with [[Bhaisajyaguru]] (Yakushi Nyōrai) and [[Kuninotokotachi]] with [[Amitābha]] (Amida Nyōrai).<ref>Breen, Teeuwen (2000:194)</ref>
The title "gongen" started being attached to the names of kami and shrines were built within the premises of large Buddhist temples to enshrine their tutelary kami.<ref name="TY87">Tamura (2000:87)</ref> During the Japanese Middle Ages, shrines started being called with the name gongen to underline their ties to Buddhism.<ref name="sos3">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=790 Gongen shinkō], accessed on October 5, 2008</ref> For example, in Eastern Japan there are still many [[Mount Haku]] shrines where the shrine itself is called either gongen or jinja.<ref name="sos3"/> Because it represents the application of Buddhist terminology to native kami, the use of the term was legally abolished in the [[Meiji Restoration]] with the {{nihongo|[[Shinbutsu bunri|Shinto and Buddhism Separation Order]]|神仏判然令|Shin-butsu Hanzenrei}} and shrines began to be called ''jinja''.<ref name="sos3"/>

{{Italic title|reason=[[:Category:Japanese words and phrases]]}} [[File:Gate-nikko-japan.jpg|thumb|250px|[[Nikkō Tōshō-gū]] enshrines [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]] under the posthumous name of Tōshō Daigongen]] {{main|Honji suijaku}} A {{nihongo||権現|'''''gongen'''''}}, literally "incarnation", was believed to be the manifestation of a [[Buddhahood|buddha]] in the form of an indigenous [[kami]], an entity who had come to guide the people to [[salvation]], during the era of [[shinbutsu-shūgō]] in premodern Japan.<ref name="SoS">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_g.html#gongen ''Gongen''] accessed on October 5, 2008</ref><ref name="TY87">Tamura (2000:87)</ref> The words {{nihongo||権化|gonge}} and {{nihongo||化現|kegen}} are [[synonym]]s for gongen.<ref name="sos3"/> {{nihongo||権現信仰|Gongen shinkō}} is the term for belief in the existence of gongen.<ref name="sos3"/>
The gongen concept is the cornerstone of the [[honji suijaku]] theory, according to which Buddhist deities choose to appear to the Japanese as native kami in order to save them, which is based on the [[Mahayana|Mahayana Buddhist]] notion of [[upaya]], "expedient means".
== History == [[File:NikkoToriiTablet5127.jpg|left|thumb|Tablet on [[torii]] at [[Nikkō Tōshō-gū]] reads "Tōshō Daigongen" (calligraphy by [[Emperor Go-Mizunoo]])]]It is sometimes assumed that the word ''gongen'' derives from [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]]'s posthumous name (Tōshō Daigongen). However, the term was created and started being used in the middle of the [[Heian period]] in an effort to harmonize Buddhism and [[ethnic religion|indigenous religious practice]] in what is called shinbutsu-shūgō or "[[syncretism]] of kami and buddhas".<ref name="TY87">Tamura (2000:87)</ref> At that time, the assumption that Japanese ''kami'' and buddhas were essentially the same evolved into a theory called {{nihongo|''honji suijaku''|本地垂迹}}, which held that native kami were manifestations or avatars of buddhas, [[bodhisattva]]s and other Buddhist deities.<ref name="TY87"/> The theory gradually spread around the country and the concept of gongen, a dual entity composed of a buddha and a kami, evolved.
Under the influence of [[Tendai|Tendai Buddhism]] and [[Shugendō]], the gongen concept was adapted to religious beliefs tied to [[Mount Iwaki]], a volcano, so that female kami Kuniyasutamahime became associated with [[Avalokiteśvara]] ''ekadaśamukha'' (Jūichimen Kannon Bosatsu, "Eleven-Faced [[Guanyin]]"), [[Ōkuninushi]] with [[Bhaisajyaguru]] (Yakushi Nyōrai) and [[Kuninotokotachi]] with [[Amitābha]] (Amida Nyōrai).<ref>Breen, Teeuwen (2000:194)</ref>
The title "gongen" started being attached to the names of kami and shrines were built within the premises of large Buddhist temples to enshrine their tutelary kami.<ref name="TY87">Tamura (2000:87)</ref> During the Japanese Middle Ages, shrines started being called with the name gongen to underline their ties to Buddhism.<ref name="sos3">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=790 Gongen shinkō], accessed on October 5, 2008</ref> For example, in Eastern Japan there are still many [[Mount Haku]] shrines where the shrine itself is called either gongen or jinja.<ref name="sos3"/> Because it represents the application of Buddhist terminology to native kami, the use of the term was legally abolished in the [[Meiji Restoration]] with the {{nihongo|[[Shinbutsu bunri|Shinto and Buddhism Separation Order]]|神仏判然令|Shin-butsu Hanzenrei}} and shrines began to be called ''jinja''.<ref name="sos3"/>
=== Gongen of Japan === * {{nihongo|Izuna Gongen|飯網の権現}}, also called "Izuna Myōjin" and enshrined in Izuna Shrine in [[Nagano, Nagano|Nagano]], is similar to a [[tengu]] and represents the kami of [[Mount Iizuna]].<ref>Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=193 Izuna Gongen], accessed on October 6, 2008</ref> * {{nihongo|Izusan Gongen|伊豆山権現}} or {{nihongo|Hashiri-yu Gongen|走湯権現}} is the spirit of a hot spring on Izusan, a hill in [[Shizuoka Prefecture]], enshrined in the [[Izusan Jinja]]<ref>Jaanus, ''Izusan Gongen'', accessed on October 6, 2008.</ref> * {{nihongo|Kumano Gongen|熊野権現}}, also known as {{nihongo|Three Mountains of Kumano|熊野三山}}.<ref name="kumano">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1076 Kumano Shinkō], accessed on October 6, 2008</ref><ref>[http://www.sekaiisan-wakayama.jp/english/s_sanzan.htm Kumano Sanzan] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080609225919/http://www.sekaiisan-wakayama.jp/english/s_sanzan.htm |date=2008-06-09 }}, World Heritage Registration Association, accessed on October 13, 2008</ref> The kami enshrined in the three Kumano Sanzan Grand Shrines and worshipped in [[Kumano shrine]]s are the three Kumano mountains: Hongū, Shingū, and Nachi.<ref name="kumano"/> * {{nihongo|Seiryū Gongen|清滝権現}} was enshrined in [[Jingo-ji]] in Takao as the tutelary kami of [[Shingon Buddhism]] by [[Kūkai]].<ref>Jaanus, ''Seiryū Gongen'', accessed on October 6, 2008</ref> * {{nihongo|Tōshō Daigongen|東照大権現}} is one of the most famous examples of gongen, representing [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]] posthumously enshrined in so-called [[Tōshō-gū]] shrines present all over Japan. The original one is [[Nikkō Tōshō-gū]] in [[Nikkō, Tochigi]]. * {{nihongo|Zaō Gongen|蔵王権現}} or {{nihongo|Kongō Zaō Bosatsu|金剛蔵王菩薩}} is a deity worshiped in [[Shugendō]].<ref>Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=201 Zaō Gongen], accessed on October 6, 2008</ref> * {{nihongo|Sannō Gongen|山王権現}} or {{nihongo|Hie (alternatively ''Hiyoshi'') Sannō Daigongen|日吉山王大権現}} is a guardian deity worshiped in [[Tendai]] spread from [[Mount Hiei]]. It is treated as a Buddhist title of Shinto [[Oyamakui no Kami]].
== ''Gongen-zukuri'' == [[File:Gongen Zukuri.png|thumb|Plan of a gongen-zukuri shrine]] {{main|Ishi-no-ma-zukuri}} {{nihongo|''Gongen-zukuri''|権現造}} is the name of a complex [[Shinto shrine]] structure in which the ''[[haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'', or worship hall, and the [[honden]], or main sanctuary, are interconnected under the same roof in the shape of an H.<ref name="sos2">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_g.html#gongen-zukuri ''Gongen-zukuri''] accessed on October 5, 2008</ref><ref>For details about these terms, see the article [[Shinto shrine]].</ref> One of the oldest examples of ''gongen-zukuri'' is Kitano Tenmangū in [[Kyoto]].<ref name="sos2"/> The name comes from Nikkō Tōshō-gū in Nikkō because it enshrines the Tōshō Daigongen and adopts this structure.<ref name="Jaa">Jaanus, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/g/gongenzukuri.htm Gongen-zukuri], accessed on October 5, 2008</ref>
== See also == * The [[Glossary of Shinto]] for an explanation of terms concerning Japanese Shinto, Shinto art, and Shinto shrine architecture
==Notes==
{{Reflist|2}}
== References == *{{cite book |last=Tamura |first=Yoshiro |title=Japanese Buddhism — A Cultural History |publisher=Kosei Publishing Company |location=Tokyo|year=2000 |edition=First |pages=232 pages |isbn=4-333-01684-3 |language= }} * {{cite book | last = [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] | first = [[Mark Teeuwen]] (editors) | title = Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami | publisher = University of Hawaii Press | date = July 2000 | location = Honolulu | language = | isbn = 978-0-8248-2363-4 | oclc = 43487317 }}

[[Category:Gongen| ]] [[Category:Buddhism in Japan]] [[Category:Shinto terminology]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] [[Category:Shugendō]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia2]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia2]] [[en:Gongen]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia2]] [[Category:Pages from wikipedia]][[en:Gongen]][[category:dd]] [[en:Gongen]] [[en:Gongen]]

{{Italic title|reason=[[:Category:Japanese words and phrases]]}} [[File:Gate-nikko-japan.jpg|thumb|250px|[[Nikkō Tōshō-gū]] enshrines [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]] under the posthumous name of Tōshō Daigongen]] {{main|Honji suijaku}} A {{nihongo||権現|'''''gongen'''''}}, literally "incarnation", was believed to be the manifestation of a [[Buddhahood|buddha]] in the form of an indigenous [[kami]], an entity who had come to guide the people to [[salvation]], during the era of [[shinbutsu-shūgō]] in premodern Japan.<ref name="SoS">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_g.html#gongen ''Gongen''] accessed on October 5, 2008</ref><ref name="TY87">Tamura (2000:87)</ref> The words {{nihongo||権化|gonge}} and {{nihongo||化現|kegen}} are [[synonym]]s for gongen.<ref name="sos3"/> {{nihongo||権現信仰|Gongen shinkō}} is the term for belief in the existence of gongen.<ref name="sos3"/>
The gongen concept is the cornerstone of the [[honji suijaku]] theory, according to which Buddhist deities choose to appear to the Japanese as native kami in order to save them, which is based on the [[Mahayana|Mahayana Buddhist]] notion of [[upaya]], "expedient means".
== History == [[File:NikkoToriiTablet5127.jpg|left|thumb|Tablet on [[torii]] at [[Nikkō Tōshō-gū]] reads "Tōshō Daigongen" (calligraphy by [[Emperor Go-Mizunoo]])]]It is sometimes assumed that the word ''gongen'' derives from [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]]'s posthumous name (Tōshō Daigongen). However, the term was created and started being used in the middle of the [[Heian period]] in an effort to harmonize Buddhism and [[ethnic religion|indigenous religious practice]] in what is called shinbutsu-shūgō or "[[syncretism]] of kami and buddhas".<ref name="TY87">Tamura (2000:87)</ref> At that time, the assumption that Japanese ''kami'' and buddhas were essentially the same evolved into a theory called {{nihongo|''honji suijaku''|本地垂迹}}, which held that native kami were manifestations or avatars of buddhas, [[bodhisattva]]s and other Buddhist deities.<ref name="TY87"/> The theory gradually spread around the country and the concept of gongen, a dual entity composed of a buddha and a kami, evolved.
Under the influence of [[Tendai|Tendai Buddhism]] and [[Shugendō]], the gongen concept was adapted to religious beliefs tied to [[Mount Iwaki]], a volcano, so that female kami Kuniyasutamahime became associated with [[Avalokiteśvara]] ''ekadaśamukha'' (Jūichimen Kannon Bosatsu, "Eleven-Faced [[Guanyin]]"), [[Ōkuninushi]] with [[Bhaisajyaguru]] (Yakushi Nyōrai) and [[Kuninotokotachi]] with [[Amitābha]] (Amida Nyōrai).<ref>Breen, Teeuwen (2000:194)</ref>
The title "gongen" started being attached to the names of kami and shrines were built within the premises of large Buddhist temples to enshrine their tutelary kami.<ref name="TY87">Tamura (2000:87)</ref> During the Japanese Middle Ages, shrines started being called with the name gongen to underline their ties to Buddhism.<ref name="sos3">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=790 Gongen shinkō], accessed on October 5, 2008</ref> For example, in Eastern Japan there are still many [[Mount Haku]] shrines where the shrine itself is called either gongen or jinja.<ref name="sos3"/> Because it represents the application of Buddhist terminology to native kami, the use of the term was legally abolished in the [[Meiji Restoration]] with the {{nihongo|[[Shinbutsu bunri|Shinto and Buddhism Separation Order]]|神仏判然令|Shin-butsu Hanzenrei}} and shrines began to be called ''jinja''.<ref name="sos3"/>
=== Gongen of Japan === * {{nihongo|Izuna Gongen|飯網の権現}}, also called "Izuna Myōjin" and enshrined in Izuna Shrine in [[Nagano, Nagano|Nagano]], is similar to a [[tengu]] and represents the kami of [[Mount Iizuna]].<ref>Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=193 Izuna Gongen], accessed on October 6, 2008</ref> * {{nihongo|Izusan Gongen|伊豆山権現}} or {{nihongo|Hashiri-yu Gongen|走湯権現}} is the spirit of a hot spring on Izusan, a hill in [[Shizuoka Prefecture]], enshrined in the [[Izusan Jinja]]<ref>Jaanus, ''Izusan Gongen'', accessed on October 6, 2008.</ref> * {{nihongo|Kumano Gongen|熊野権現}}, also known as {{nihongo|Three Mountains of Kumano|熊野三山}}.<ref name="kumano">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1076 Kumano Shinkō], accessed on October 6, 2008</ref><ref>[http://www.sekaiisan-wakayama.jp/english/s_sanzan.htm Kumano Sanzan] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080609225919/http://www.sekaiisan-wakayama.jp/english/s_sanzan.htm |date=2008-06-09 }}, World Heritage Registration Association, accessed on October 13, 2008</ref> The kami enshrined in the three Kumano Sanzan Grand Shrines and worshipped in [[Kumano shrine]]s are the three Kumano mountains: Hongū, Shingū, and Nachi.<ref name="kumano"/> * {{nihongo|Seiryū Gongen|清滝権現}} was enshrined in [[Jingo-ji]] in Takao as the tutelary kami of [[Shingon Buddhism]] by [[Kūkai]].<ref>Jaanus, ''Seiryū Gongen'', accessed on October 6, 2008</ref> * {{nihongo|Tōshō Daigongen|東照大権現}} is one of the most famous examples of gongen, representing [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]] posthumously enshrined in so-called [[Tōshō-gū]] shrines present all over Japan. The original one is [[Nikkō Tōshō-gū]] in [[Nikkō, Tochigi]]. * {{nihongo|Zaō Gongen|蔵王権現}} or {{nihongo|Kongō Zaō Bosatsu|金剛蔵王菩薩}} is a deity worshiped in [[Shugendō]].<ref>Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=201 Zaō Gongen], accessed on October 6, 2008</ref> * {{nihongo|Sannō Gongen|山王権現}} or {{nihongo|Hie (alternatively ''Hiyoshi'') Sannō Daigongen|日吉山王大権現}} is a guardian deity worshiped in [[Tendai]] spread from [[Mount Hiei]]. It is treated as a Buddhist title of Shinto [[Oyamakui no Kami]].
== ''Gongen-zukuri'' == [[File:Gongen Zukuri.png|thumb|Plan of a gongen-zukuri shrine]] {{main|Ishi-no-ma-zukuri}} {{nihongo|''Gongen-zukuri''|権現造}} is the name of a complex [[Shinto shrine]] structure in which the ''[[haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'', or worship hall, and the [[honden]], or main sanctuary, are interconnected under the same roof in the shape of an H.<ref name="sos2">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_g.html#gongen-zukuri ''Gongen-zukuri''] accessed on October 5, 2008</ref><ref>For details about these terms, see the article [[Shinto shrine]].</ref> One of the oldest examples of ''gongen-zukuri'' is Kitano Tenmangū in [[Kyoto]].<ref name="sos2"/> The name comes from Nikkō Tōshō-gū in Nikkō because it enshrines the Tōshō Daigongen and adopts this structure.<ref name="Jaa">Jaanus, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/g/gongenzukuri.htm Gongen-zukuri], accessed on October 5, 2008</ref>
== See also == * The [[Glossary of Shinto]] for an explanation of terms concerning Japanese Shinto, Shinto art, and Shinto shrine architecture

The original one is [[Nikkō Tōshō-gū]] in [[Nikkō, Tochigi]]. * {{nihongo|Zaō Gongen|蔵王権現}} or {{nihongo|Kongō Zaō Bosatsu|金剛蔵王菩薩}} is a deity worshiped in [[Shugendō]].<ref>Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=201 Zaō Gongen], accessed on October 6, 2008</ref> * {{nihongo|Sannō Gongen|山王権現}} or {{nihongo|Hie (alternatively ''Hiyoshi'') Sannō Daigongen|日吉山王大権現}} is a guardian deity worshiped in [[Tendai]] spread from [[Mount Hiei]]. It is treated as a Buddhist title of Shinto [[Oyamakui no Kami]].
== ''Gongen-zukuri'' == [[File:Gongen Zukuri.png|thumb|Plan of a gongen-zukuri shrine]] {{main|Ishi-no-ma-zukuri}} {{nihongo|''Gongen-zukuri''|権現造}} is the name of a complex [[Shinto shrine]] structure in which the ''[[haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'', or worship hall, and the [[honden]], or main sanctuary, are interconnected under the same roof in the shape of an H.<ref name="sos2">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_g.html#gongen-zukuri ''Gongen-zukuri''] accessed on October 5, 2008</ref><ref>For details about these terms, see the article [[Shinto shrine]].</ref> One of the oldest examples of ''gongen-zukuri'' is Kitano Tenmangū in [[Kyoto]].<ref name="sos2"/> The name comes from Nikkō Tōshō-gū in Nikkō because it enshrines the Tōshō Daigongen and adopts this structure.<ref name="Jaa">Jaanus, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/g/gongenzukuri.htm Gongen-zukuri], accessed on October 5, 2008</ref>
== See also == * The [[Glossary of Shinto]] for an explanation of terms concerning Japanese Shinto, Shinto art, and Shinto shrine architecture

revision.text:
{{Italic title|reason=[[:Category:Japanese words and phrases]]}} [[File:Gate-nikko-japan.jpg|thumb|250px|[[Nikkō Tōshō-gū]] enshrines [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]] under the posthumous name of Tōshō Daigongen]] {{main|Honji suijaku}} A {{nihongo||権現|'''''gongen'''''}}, literally "incarnation", was believed to be the manifestation of a [[Buddhahood|buddha]] in the form of an indigenous [[kami]], an entity who had come to guide the people to [[salvation]], during the era of [[shinbutsu-shūgō]] in premodern Japan.<ref name="SoS">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_g.html#gongen ''Gongen''] accessed on October 5, 2008</ref><ref name="TY87">Tamura (2000:87)</ref> The words {{nihongo||権化|gonge}} and {{nihongo||化現|kegen}} are [[synonym]]s for gongen.<ref name="sos3"/> {{nihongo||権現信仰|Gongen shinkō}} is the term for belief in the existence of gongen.<ref name="sos3"/>
The gongen concept is the cornerstone of the [[honji suijaku]] theory, according to which Buddhist deities choose to appear to the Japanese as native kami in order to save them, which is based on the [[Mahayana|Mahayana Buddhist]] notion of [[upaya]], "expedient means".
== History == [[File:NikkoToriiTablet5127.jpg|left|thumb|Tablet on [[torii]] at [[Nikkō Tōshō-gū]] reads "Tōshō Daigongen" (calligraphy by [[Emperor Go-Mizunoo]])]]It is sometimes assumed that the word ''gongen'' derives from [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]]'s posthumous name (Tōshō Daigongen). However, the term was created and started being used in the middle of the [[Heian period]] in an effort to harmonize Buddhism and [[ethnic religion|indigenous religious practice]] in what is called shinbutsu-shūgō or "[[syncretism]] of kami and buddhas".<ref name="TY87">Tamura (2000:87)</ref> At that time, the assumption that Japanese ''kami'' and buddhas were essentially the same evolved into a theory called {{nihongo|''honji suijaku''|本地垂迹}}, which held that native kami were manifestations or avatars of buddhas, [[bodhisattva]]s and other Buddhist deities.<ref name="TY87"/> The theory gradually spread around the country and the concept of gongen, a dual entity composed of a buddha and a kami, evolved.
Under the influence of [[Tendai|Tendai Buddhism]] and [[Shugendō]], the gongen concept was adapted to religious beliefs tied to [[Mount Iwaki]], a volcano, so that female kami Kuniyasutamahime became associated with [[Avalokiteśvara]] ''ekadaśamukha'' (Jūichimen Kannon Bosatsu, "Eleven-Faced [[Guanyin]]"), [[Ōkuninushi]] with [[Bhaisajyaguru]] (Yakushi Nyōrai) and [[Kuninotokotachi]] with [[Amitābha]] (Amida Nyōrai).<ref>Breen, Teeuwen (2000:194)</ref>
The title "gongen" started being attached to the names of kami and shrines were built within the premises of large Buddhist temples to enshrine their tutelary kami.<ref name="TY87">Tamura (2000:87)</ref> During the Japanese Middle Ages, shrines started being called with the name gongen to underline their ties to Buddhism.<ref name="sos3">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=790 Gongen shinkō], accessed on October 5, 2008</ref> For example, in Eastern Japan there are still many [[Mount Haku]] shrines where the shrine itself is called either gongen or jinja.<ref name="sos3"/> Because it represents the application of Buddhist terminology to native kami, the use of the term was legally abolished in the [[Meiji Restoration]] with the {{nihongo|[[Shinbutsu bunri|Shinto and Buddhism Separation Order]]|神仏判然令|Shin-butsu Hanzenrei}} and shrines began to be called ''jinja''.<ref name="sos3"/>
=== Gongen of Japan === * {{nihongo|Izuna Gongen|飯網の権現}}, also called "Izuna Myōjin" and enshrined in Izuna Shrine in [[Nagano, Nagano|Nagano]], is similar to a [[tengu]] and represents the kami of [[Mount Iizuna]].<ref>Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=193 Izuna Gongen], accessed on October 6, 2008</ref> * {{nihongo|Izusan Gongen|伊豆山権現}} or {{nihongo|Hashiri-yu Gongen|走湯権現}} is the spirit of a hot spring on Izusan, a hill in [[Shizuoka Prefecture]], enshrined in the [[Izusan Jinja]]<ref>Jaanus, ''Izusan Gongen'', accessed on October 6, 2008.</ref> * {{nihongo|Kumano Gongen|熊野権現}}, also known as {{nihongo|Three Mountains of Kumano|熊野三山}}.<ref name="kumano">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1076 Kumano Shinkō], accessed on October 6, 2008</ref><ref>[http://www.sekaiisan-wakayama.jp/english/s_sanzan.htm Kumano Sanzan] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080609225919/http://www.sekaiisan-wakayama.jp/english/s_sanzan.htm |date=2008-06-09 }}, World Heritage Registration Association, accessed on October 13, 2008</ref> The kami enshrined in the three Kumano Sanzan Grand Shrines and worshipped in [[Kumano shrine]]s are the three Kumano mountains: Hongū, Shingū, and Nachi.<ref name="kumano"/> * {{nihongo|Seiryū Gongen|清滝権現}} was enshrined in [[Jingo-ji]] in Takao as the tutelary kami of [[Shingon Buddhism]] by [[Kūkai]].<ref>Jaanus, ''Seiryū Gongen'', accessed on October 6, 2008</ref> * {{nihongo|Tōshō Daigongen|東照大権現}} is one of the most famous examples of gongen, representing [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]] posthumously enshrined in so-called [[Tōshō-gū]] shrines present all over Japan. The original one is [[Nikkō Tōshō-gū]] in [[Nikkō, Tochigi]]. * {{nihongo|Zaō Gongen|蔵王権現}} or {{nihongo|Kongō Zaō Bosatsu|金剛蔵王菩薩}} is a deity worshiped in [[Shugendō]].<ref>Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=201 Zaō Gongen], accessed on October 6, 2008</ref> * {{nihongo|Sannō Gongen|山王権現}} or {{nihongo|Hie (alternatively ''Hiyoshi'') Sannō Daigongen|日吉山王大権現}} is a guardian deity worshiped in [[Tendai]] spread from [[Mount Hiei]]. It is treated as a Buddhist title of Shinto [[Oyamakui no Kami]].
== ''Gongen-zukuri'' == [[File:Gongen Zukuri.png|thumb|Plan of a gongen-zukuri shrine]] {{main|Ishi-no-ma-zukuri}} {{nihongo|''Gongen-zukuri''|権現造}} is the name of a complex [[Shinto shrine]] structure in which the ''[[haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'', or worship hall, and the [[honden]], or main sanctuary, are interconnected under the same roof in the shape of an H.<ref name="sos2">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_g.html#gongen-zukuri ''Gongen-zukuri''] accessed on October 5, 2008</ref><ref>For details about these terms, see the article [[Shinto shrine]].</ref> One of the oldest examples of ''gongen-zukuri'' is Kitano Tenmangū in [[Kyoto]].<ref name="sos2"/> The name comes from Nikkō Tōshō-gū in Nikkō because it enshrines the Tōshō Daigongen and adopts this structure.<ref name="Jaa">Jaanus, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/g/gongenzukuri.htm Gongen-zukuri], accessed on October 5, 2008</ref>
== See also == * The [[Glossary of Shinto]] for an explanation of terms concerning Japanese Shinto, Shinto art, and Shinto shrine architecture

The title "gongen" started being attached to the names of kami and shrines were built within the premises of large Buddhist temples to enshrine their tutelary kami.<ref name="TY87">Tamura (2000:87)</ref> During the Japanese Middle Ages, shrines started being called with the name gongen to underline their ties to Buddhism.<ref name="sos3">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=790 Gongen shinkō], accessed on October 5, 2008</ref> For example, in Eastern Japan there are still many [[Mount Haku]] shrines where the shrine itself is called either gongen or jinja.<ref name="sos3"/> Because it represents the application of Buddhist terminology to native kami, the use of the term was legally abolished in the [[Meiji Restoration]] with the {{nihongo|[[Shinbutsu bunri|Shinto and Buddhism Separation Order]]|神仏判然令|Shin-butsu Hanzenrei}} and shrines began to be called ''jinja''.<ref name="sos3"/>
=== Gongen of Japan === * {{nihongo|Izuna Gongen|飯網の権現}}, also called "Izuna Myōjin" and enshrined in Izuna Shrine in [[Nagano, Nagano|Nagano]], is similar to a [[tengu]] and represents the kami of [[Mount Iizuna]].<ref>Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=193 Izuna Gongen], accessed on October 6, 2008</ref> * {{nihongo|Izusan Gongen|伊豆山権現}} or {{nihongo|Hashiri-yu Gongen|走湯権現}} is the spirit of a hot spring on Izusan, a hill in [[Shizuoka Prefecture]], enshrined in the [[Izusan Jinja]]<ref>Jaanus, ''Izusan Gongen'', accessed on October 6, 2008.</ref> * {{nihongo|Kumano Gongen|熊野権現}}, also known as {{nihongo|Three Mountains of Kumano|熊野三山}}.<ref name="kumano">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1076 Kumano Shinkō], accessed on October 6, 2008</ref><ref>[http://www.sekaiisan-wakayama.jp/english/s_sanzan.htm Kumano Sanzan] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080609225919/http://www.sekaiisan-wakayama.jp/english/s_sanzan.htm |date=2008-06-09 }}, World Heritage Registration Association, accessed on October 13, 2008</ref> The kami enshrined in the three Kumano Sanzan Grand Shrines and worshipped in [[Kumano shrine]]s are the three Kumano mountains: Hongū, Shingū, and Nachi.<ref name="kumano"/> * {{nihongo|Seiryū Gongen|清滝権現}} was enshrined in [[Jingo-ji]] in Takao as the tutelary kami of [[Shingon Buddhism]] by [[Kūkai]].<ref>Jaanus, ''Seiryū Gongen'', accessed on October 6, 2008</ref> * {{nihongo|Tōshō Daigongen|東照大権現}} is one of the most famous examples of gongen, representing [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]] posthumously enshrined in so-called [[Tōshō-gū]] shrines present all over Japan. It is treated as a Buddhist title of Shinto [[Oyamakui no Kami]].

{{Italic title|reason=[[:Category:Japanese words and phrases]]}} [[File:Gate-nikko-japan.jpg|thumb|250px|[[Nikkō Tōshō-gū]] enshrines [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]] under the posthumous name of Tōshō Daigongen]] {{main|Honji suijaku}} A {{nihongo||権現|'''''gongen'''''}}, literally "incarnation", was believed to be the manifestation of a [[Buddhahood|buddha]] in the form of an indigenous [[kami]], an entity who had come to guide the people to [[salvation]], during the era of [[shinbutsu-shūgō]] in premodern Japan.<ref name="SoS">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_g.html#gongen ''Gongen''] accessed on October 5, 2008</ref><ref name="TY87">Tamura (2000:87)</ref> The words {{nihongo||権化|gonge}} and {{nihongo||化現|kegen}} are [[synonym]]s for gongen.<ref name="sos3"/> {{nihongo||権現信仰|Gongen shinkō}} is the term for belief in the existence of gongen.<ref name="sos3"/>

However, the term was created and started being used in the middle of the [[Heian period]] in an effort to harmonize Buddhism and [[ethnic religion|indigenous religious practice]] in what is called shinbutsu-shūgō or "[[syncretism]] of kami and buddhas".<ref name="TY87">Tamura (2000:87)</ref> At that time, the assumption that Japanese ''kami'' and buddhas were essentially the same evolved into a theory called {{nihongo|''honji suijaku''|本地垂迹}}, which held that native kami were manifestations or avatars of buddhas, [[bodhisattva]]s and other Buddhist deities.<ref name="TY87"/> The theory gradually spread around the country and the concept of gongen, a dual entity composed of a buddha and a kami, evolved.
Under the influence of [[Tendai|Tendai Buddhism]] and [[Shugendō]], the gongen concept was adapted to religious beliefs tied to [[Mount Iwaki]], a volcano, so that female kami Kuniyasutamahime became associated with [[Avalokiteśvara]] ''ekadaśamukha'' (Jūichimen Kannon Bosatsu, "Eleven-Faced [[Guanyin]]"), [[Ōkuninushi]] with [[Bhaisajyaguru]] (Yakushi Nyōrai) and [[Kuninotokotachi]] with [[Amitābha]] (Amida Nyōrai).<ref>Breen, Teeuwen (2000:194)</ref>
The title "gongen" started being attached to the names of kami and shrines were built within the premises of large Buddhist temples to enshrine their tutelary kami.<ref name="TY87">Tamura (2000:87)</ref> During the Japanese Middle Ages, shrines started being called with the name gongen to underline their ties to Buddhism.<ref name="sos3">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=790 Gongen shinkō], accessed on October 5, 2008</ref> For example, in Eastern Japan there are still many [[Mount Haku]] shrines where the shrine itself is called either gongen or jinja.<ref name="sos3"/> Because it represents the application of Buddhist terminology to native kami, the use of the term was legally abolished in the [[Meiji Restoration]] with the {{nihongo|[[Shinbutsu bunri|Shinto and Buddhism Separation Order]]|神仏判然令|Shin-butsu Hanzenrei}} and shrines began to be called ''jinja''.<ref name="sos3"/>
=== Gongen of Japan === * {{nihongo|Izuna Gongen|飯網の権現}}, also called "Izuna Myōjin" and enshrined in Izuna Shrine in [[Nagano, Nagano|Nagano]], is similar to a [[tengu]] and represents the kami of [[Mount Iizuna]].<ref>Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=193 Izuna Gongen], accessed on October 6, 2008</ref> * {{nihongo|Izusan Gongen|伊豆山権現}} or {{nihongo|Hashiri-yu Gongen|走湯権現}} is the spirit of a hot spring on Izusan, a hill in [[Shizuoka Prefecture]], enshrined in the [[Izusan Jinja]]<ref>Jaanus, ''Izusan Gongen'', accessed on October 6, 2008.</ref> * {{nihongo|Kumano Gongen|熊野権現}}, also known as {{nihongo|Three Mountains of Kumano|熊野三山}}.<ref name="kumano">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1076 Kumano Shinkō], accessed on October 6, 2008</ref><ref>[http://www.sekaiisan-wakayama.jp/english/s_sanzan.htm Kumano Sanzan] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080609225919/http://www.sekaiisan-wakayama.jp/english/s_sanzan.htm |date=2008-06-09 }}, World Heritage Registration Association, accessed on October 13, 2008</ref> The kami enshrined in the three Kumano Sanzan Grand Shrines and worshipped in [[Kumano shrine]]s are the three Kumano mountains: Hongū, Shingū, and Nachi.<ref name="kumano"/> * {{nihongo|Seiryū Gongen|清滝権現}} was enshrined in [[Jingo-ji]] in Takao as the tutelary kami of [[Shingon Buddhism]] by [[Kūkai]].<ref>Jaanus, ''Seiryū Gongen'', accessed on October 6, 2008</ref> * {{nihongo|Tōshō Daigongen|東照大権現}} is one of the most famous examples of gongen, representing [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]] posthumously enshrined in so-called [[Tōshō-gū]] shrines present all over Japan. The original one is [[Nikkō Tōshō-gū]] in [[Nikkō, Tochigi]]. * {{nihongo|Zaō Gongen|蔵王権現}} or {{nihongo|Kongō Zaō Bosatsu|金剛蔵王菩薩}} is a deity worshiped in [[Shugendō]].<ref>Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=201 Zaō Gongen], accessed on October 6, 2008</ref> * {{nihongo|Sannō Gongen|山王権現}} or {{nihongo|Hie (alternatively ''Hiyoshi'') Sannō Daigongen|日吉山王大権現}} is a guardian deity worshiped in [[Tendai]] spread from [[Mount Hiei]]. It is treated as a Buddhist title of Shinto [[Oyamakui no Kami]].
== ''Gongen-zukuri'' == [[File:Gongen Zukuri.png|thumb|Plan of a gongen-zukuri shrine]] {{main|Ishi-no-ma-zukuri}} {{nihongo|''Gongen-zukuri''|権現造}} is the name of a complex [[Shinto shrine]] structure in which the ''[[haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'', or worship hall, and the [[honden]], or main sanctuary, are interconnected under the same roof in the shape of an H.<ref name="sos2">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_g.html#gongen-zukuri ''Gongen-zukuri''] accessed on October 5, 2008</ref><ref>For details about these terms, see the article [[Shinto shrine]].</ref> One of the oldest examples of ''gongen-zukuri'' is Kitano Tenmangū in [[Kyoto]].<ref name="sos2"/> The name comes from Nikkō Tōshō-gū in Nikkō because it enshrines the Tōshō Daigongen and adopts this structure.<ref name="Jaa">Jaanus, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/g/gongenzukuri.htm Gongen-zukuri], accessed on October 5, 2008</ref>

{{Italic title|reason=[[:Category:Japanese words and phrases]]}} [[File:Gate-nikko-japan.jpg|thumb|250px|[[Nikkō Tōshō-gū]] enshrines [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]] under the posthumous name of Tōshō Daigongen]] {{main|Honji suijaku}} A {{nihongo||権現|'''''gongen'''''}}, literally "incarnation", was believed to be the manifestation of a [[Buddhahood|buddha]] in the form of an indigenous [[kami]], an entity who had come to guide the people to [[salvation]], during the era of [[shinbutsu-shūgō]] in premodern Japan.<ref name="SoS">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_g.html#gongen ''Gongen''] accessed on October 5, 2008</ref><ref name="TY87">Tamura (2000:87)</ref> The words {{nihongo||権化|gonge}} and {{nihongo||化現|kegen}} are [[synonym]]s for gongen.<ref name="sos3"/> {{nihongo||権現信仰|Gongen shinkō}} is the term for belief in the existence of gongen.<ref name="sos3"/>
The gongen concept is the cornerstone of the [[honji suijaku]] theory, according to which Buddhist deities choose to appear to the Japanese as native kami in order to save them, which is based on the [[Mahayana|Mahayana Buddhist]] notion of [[upaya]], "expedient means".
== History == [[File:NikkoToriiTablet5127.jpg|left|thumb|Tablet on [[torii]] at [[Nikkō Tōshō-gū]] reads "Tōshō Daigongen" (calligraphy by [[Emperor Go-Mizunoo]])]]It is sometimes assumed that the word ''gongen'' derives from [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]]'s posthumous name (Tōshō Daigongen). However, the term was created and started being used in the middle of the [[Heian period]] in an effort to harmonize Buddhism and [[ethnic religion|indigenous religious practice]] in what is called shinbutsu-shūgō or "[[syncretism]] of kami and buddhas".<ref name="TY87">Tamura (2000:87)</ref> At that time, the assumption that Japanese ''kami'' and buddhas were essentially the same evolved into a theory called {{nihongo|''honji suijaku''|本地垂迹}}, which held that native kami were manifestations or avatars of buddhas, [[bodhisattva]]s and other Buddhist deities.<ref name="TY87"/> The theory gradually spread around the country and the concept of gongen, a dual entity composed of a buddha and a kami, evolved.
Under the influence of [[Tendai|Tendai Buddhism]] and [[Shugendō]], the gongen concept was adapted to religious beliefs tied to [[Mount Iwaki]], a volcano, so that female kami Kuniyasutamahime became associated with [[Avalokiteśvara]] ''ekadaśamukha'' (Jūichimen Kannon Bosatsu, "Eleven-Faced [[Guanyin]]"), [[Ōkuninushi]] with [[Bhaisajyaguru]] (Yakushi Nyōrai) and [[Kuninotokotachi]] with [[Amitābha]] (Amida Nyōrai).<ref>Breen, Teeuwen (2000:194)</ref>
The title "gongen" started being attached to the names of kami and shrines were built within the premises of large Buddhist temples to enshrine their tutelary kami.<ref name="TY87">Tamura (2000:87)</ref> During the Japanese Middle Ages, shrines started being called with the name gongen to underline their ties to Buddhism.<ref name="sos3">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=790 Gongen shinkō], accessed on October 5, 2008</ref> For example, in Eastern Japan there are still many [[Mount Haku]] shrines where the shrine itself is called either gongen or jinja.<ref name="sos3"/> Because it represents the application of Buddhist terminology to native kami, the use of the term was legally abolished in the [[Meiji Restoration]] with the {{nihongo|[[Shinbutsu bunri|Shinto and Buddhism Separation Order]]|神仏判然令|Shin-butsu Hanzenrei}} and shrines began to be called ''jinja''.<ref name="sos3"/>
=== Gongen of Japan === * {{nihongo|Izuna Gongen|飯網の権現}}, also called "Izuna Myōjin" and enshrined in Izuna Shrine in [[Nagano, Nagano|Nagano]], is similar to a [[tengu]] and represents the kami of [[Mount Iizuna]].<ref>Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=193 Izuna Gongen], accessed on October 6, 2008</ref> * {{nihongo|Izusan Gongen|伊豆山権現}} or {{nihongo|Hashiri-yu Gongen|走湯権現}} is the spirit of a hot spring on Izusan, a hill in [[Shizuoka Prefecture]], enshrined in the [[Izusan Jinja]]<ref>Jaanus, ''Izusan Gongen'', accessed on October 6, 2008.</ref> * {{nihongo|Kumano Gongen|熊野権現}}, also known as {{nihongo|Three Mountains of Kumano|熊野三山}}.<ref name="kumano">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1076 Kumano Shinkō], accessed on October 6, 2008</ref><ref>[http://www.sekaiisan-wakayama.jp/english/s_sanzan.htm Kumano Sanzan] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080609225919/http://www.sekaiisan-wakayama.jp/english/s_sanzan.htm |date=2008-06-09 }}, World Heritage Registration Association, accessed on October 13, 2008</ref> The kami enshrined in the three Kumano Sanzan Grand Shrines and worshipped in [[Kumano shrine]]s are the three Kumano mountains: Hongū, Shingū, and Nachi.<ref name="kumano"/> * {{nihongo|Seiryū Gongen|清滝権現}} was enshrined in [[Jingo-ji]] in Takao as the tutelary kami of [[Shingon Buddhism]] by [[Kūkai]].<ref>Jaanus, ''Seiryū Gongen'', accessed on October 6, 2008</ref> * {{nihongo|Tōshō Daigongen|東照大権現}} is one of the most famous examples of gongen, representing [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]] posthumously enshrined in so-called [[Tōshō-gū]] shrines present all over Japan. The original one is [[Nikkō Tōshō-gū]] in [[Nikkō, Tochigi]]. * {{nihongo|Zaō Gongen|蔵王権現}} or {{nihongo|Kongō Zaō Bosatsu|金剛蔵王菩薩}} is a deity worshiped in [[Shugendō]].<ref>Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=201 Zaō Gongen], accessed on October 6, 2008</ref> * {{nihongo|Sannō Gongen|山王権現}} or {{nihongo|Hie (alternatively ''Hiyoshi'') Sannō Daigongen|日吉山王大権現}} is a guardian deity worshiped in [[Tendai]] spread from [[Mount Hiei]]. It is treated as a Buddhist title of Shinto [[Oyamakui no Kami]].
== ''Gongen-zukuri'' == [[File:Gongen Zukuri.png|thumb|Plan of a gongen-zukuri shrine]] {{main|Ishi-no-ma-zukuri}} {{nihongo|''Gongen-zukuri''|権現造}} is the name of a complex [[Shinto shrine]] structure in which the ''[[haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'', or worship hall, and the [[honden]], or main sanctuary, are interconnected under the same roof in the shape of an H.<ref name="sos2">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_g.html#gongen-zukuri ''Gongen-zukuri''] accessed on October 5, 2008</ref><ref>For details about these terms, see the article [[Shinto shrine]].</ref> One of the oldest examples of ''gongen-zukuri'' is Kitano Tenmangū in [[Kyoto]].<ref name="sos2"/> The name comes from Nikkō Tōshō-gū in Nikkō because it enshrines the Tōshō Daigongen and adopts this structure.<ref name="Jaa">Jaanus, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/g/gongenzukuri.htm Gongen-zukuri], accessed on October 5, 2008</ref>
