revision.comment: Text replacement - "八王子権現}}" to "八王子権現|1=Hachiōji Gongen}}"
revision.text:
{{出典の明記|date=2017年8月28日 (月) 05:23 (UTC)}} [[File:Gate-nikko-japan.jpg|thumb|right|300 px|[[日光東照宮]]。[[徳川家康]]を神格化した東照大権現（本地は薬師如来）を祀る。]]
'''権現'''（ごんげん）は、日本の神の[[神号]]の一つ。[[日本]]の[[神 (神道)|神々]]を[[仏教]]の[[仏]]や[[菩薩]]が「仮の姿」で現れたものとする[[本地垂迹]]思想による神号である。'''権'''という文字は「[[権官|権大納言]]」などと同じく「臨時の」「仮の」という意味で、仏が「仮に」神の形を取って「'''現'''れた」ことを示す。
[[インド]]の[[バラモン教]]や[[ヒンドゥー教]]の神々は「天」という神号で[[護法善神]]として[[大乗仏教]]の[[天部]]に取り入れられたが、日本の神々が日本仏教に取り入れられた際には'''本地垂迹'''思想に基づき'''権現'''という神号が多く用いられた<ref group="注釈">「権現」以外の例として（実質的には権現であるが）「八幡大菩薩」や「高良大菩薩」など菩薩号を用いることもある。</ref>。権現には[[山王神道]]（[[天台宗]]）・[[両部神道]]（[[真言宗]]）に基づくものや、[[自然崇拝]]（[[山岳信仰]]）と[[修験道]]が融合したもの等があり、[[民間信仰]]においては地域の名士や不慮の死を遂げた人物などが死後に権現として祀られる例も見られる。なお、「[[明神]]号」と「権現号」が区別されずに使用される例も少なくはない。（[[愛宕権現]]＝愛宕明神、[[白山権現]]＝白山明神など）
== 神号の事例 == [[春日大社]]は[[タケミカヅチ|武甕槌命]]（一殿）・[[経津主神]]（二殿）・[[天児屋命]]（三殿）・[[比売神]]（四殿）の四柱の祭神を祀るが、[[中世]]には[[興福寺]]が[[神宮寺]]として支配的な影響力を持ったため、[[神仏習合]]に基づいて[[不空羂索観音]]（一殿）・[[薬師如来]]（二殿）・[[地蔵菩薩]]（三殿）・[[十一面観音]]（四殿）をそれぞれの本地仏とし、社にちなんで「[[春日権現]]」（もしくは[[春日神|春日明神]]）と総称した。また、複数の祭神を祀っている社では「六所権現」などと呼ぶこともあり、この場合は複数の神の総称である。神号との組み合わせにより「[[日光二荒山神社|日光三所権現]]」、「[[熊野権現|熊野十二所権現]]」などのように称される。全国的に最も多く見られるのは「[[白山権現]]」、「[[立山権現]]」、「[[羽黒権現]]」などのように、「山名（もしくは地名）＋権現号」という組み合わせである。その他、少数ではあるが「[[摩利支天]]権現」、「[[薬師如来|薬師]]権現」、「[[釈迦]]権現」のように本地仏の名前がそのまま用いられる例や、「天妃媽祖権現」（[[媽祖]]）、「徐福権現」（[[徐福]]）、「将門権現」（[[平将門]]）などのように神名や人名を用いた例、「[[猫又]]権現」、「大蛇権現」などのように動物由来の例も見られる。以上のことは明神号にも多く共通する。
== 神仏分離・廃仏毀釈 == [[明治維新]]の[[神仏分離令]]（神仏判然令）で権現社・権現宮・権現堂の多くが廃され、「権現」の神号や修験道が一時禁止されたため、権現を祀る神社の多くは本地仏を廃して祭神（垂迹神）のみを祀るようになった。祭神が不詳な権現には[[復古神道]]・[[国学]]の学説に基づき、記紀神話の神を比定した。
== Major Gongen == {{seealso|Japanese Buddhist Deities#Suijaku (Manifestation) Kami|ja|日本の仏尊#垂迹神}}
* {{ill|Atago Gongen|ja|愛宕権現}} ([[愛宕権現]]) * {{ill|Akiba Gongen|ja|秋葉権現}} ([[秋葉権現]]) * {{ill|Ishizuchi Daigongen|ja|石鎚大権現}} ([[石鎚大権現]]) * {{ill|Iizuna Gongen|ja|飯縄権現}} ([[飯縄権現]]) * {{ill|Ochi Daigongen|ja|越知大権現}} ([[越知大権現]]) – Hakusan Shugendō ([[白山修験]]) * {{ill|Kumano Gongen|ja|熊野権現}} ([[熊野権現]]) * {{ill|Konpira Gongen|ja|金毘羅権現}} ([[金毘羅権現]]) * {{ill|Sanki Daigongen|ja|三鬼大権現}} ([[三鬼大権現]]) * {{ill|Zaō Gongen|ja|蔵王権現}} ([[蔵王権現]]) * {{ill|Suzuka Gongen|ja|片山神社 (亀山市)|lt=Suzuka Gongen}} ([[片山神社 (亀山市)|鈴鹿権現]]) – {{ill|Shamanism|ja|巫覡}} ([[巫覡]]) * {{ill|Seiryū Gongen|ja|清瀧権現}} ([[清瀧権現]]) * {{ill|Tateyama Gongen|ja|立山権現}} ([[立山権現]]) – Tateyama Shugendō ([[立山修験]]) * {{ill|Chimyō Gongen|ja|智明権現}} ([[智明権現]]) * {{ill|Nezu Gongen|ja|根津神社#歴史|lt=Nezu Gongen}} ([[根津神社#歴史|根津権現]]) * {{ill|Hakusan Gongen|ja|白山権現}} ([[白山権現]]) – Hakusan Shugendō ([[白山修験]]) * {{ill|Haguro Gongen|ja|羽黒権現}} ([[羽黒権現]]) – Haguro Shugendō ([[羽黒修験]]) * {{ill|Hakone Gongen|ja|箱根権現}} ([[箱根権現]]) * {{ill|Hachiōji Gongen|ja|八王子権現|1=Hachiōji Gongen}} ([[八王子権現]]) * {{ill|Hirō Gongen|ja|飛瀧権現}} ([[飛瀧権現]]) * {{ill|Sannō Gongen|ja|山王権現|lt=Hiyoshi Sannō Gongen}} ([[山王権現|日吉山王権現]]) – {{ill|Sannō Shintō|ja|山王神道}} ([[山王神道]]) * {{ill|Futarasan Gongen|ja|二荒権現}} ([[二荒権現]]) – Nikkō Shugendō ([[日光修験]]) * {{ill|Yuga Daigongen|ja|瑜伽大権現}} ([[瑜伽大権現]]) * {{ill|Hikosan Gongen|ja|英彦山|lt=Hikosan Gongen}} ([[英彦山|英彦山権現]]) * {{ill|Shiramine Daigongen|ja|白峯大権現}} ([[白峯大権現]])
{{seealso|日本の仏尊#垂迹神}} * [[愛宕権現]] * [[秋葉権現]] * 石鎚大権現 * [[飯縄権現]] * [[越知大権現]] - 白山修験 * [[熊野権現]] * [[金毘羅権現]] * [[三鬼大権現]] * [[蔵王権現]] * [[片山神社 (亀山市)|鈴鹿権現]] - [[巫覡]] * [[清瀧権現]] * [[立山権現]] - [[立山修験]] * [[智明権現]] * [[根津神社#歴史|根津権現]] * [[白山権現]] - 白山修験 * [[羽黒権現]] - 羽黒修験 * [[箱根権現]] * [[八王子権現]] * 飛瀧権現 * [[山王権現|日吉山王権現]] - [[山王神道]] * 二荒権現 - [[日光修験]] * [[瑜伽大権現]] * [[英彦山|英彦山権現]] * 白峯大権現
== 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} === 注釈 === {{Notelist}}
== 関連項目 == *[[日本の仏尊]] *[[徳川家康]] - 没後に薬師如来を本地とする「東照大権現」として神格化された。 *[[権現造]] *[[化身]]
== 外部リンク == *{{Commonscat-inline}}
{{Buddhism2}} {{神道 横}} {{buddhism-stub}} {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:こんけん}} [[Category:Gongen]][[Category:Gongen]][[Category:神仏習合]] [[Category:Shinto-Buddhist syncretism]] [[Category:Gongen]][[Category:修験道]] [[Category:Shugendo]] [[Category:untranslated pages]][[ja:権現]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[Category:Mass editing to refresh the first new Wikipedia redirects]]
{{Italic title|reason=[[:Category:Japanese words and phrases]]}} [[File:Gate-nikko-japan.jpg|thumb|250px|[[Nikkō Tōshō-gū]] enshrines [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]] under the posthumous name of Tōshō Daigongen]] {{main|Honji suijaku}} A {{nihongo||権現|'''''gongen'''''}}, literally "incarnation", was believed to be the manifestation of a [[Buddhahood|buddha]] in the form of an indigenous [[kami]], an entity who had come to guide the people to [[salvation]], during the era of [[shinbutsu-shūgō]] in premodern Japan.<ref name="SoS">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_g.html#gongen ''Gongen''] accessed on October 5, 2008</ref><ref name="TY87">Tamura (2000:87)</ref> The words {{nihongo||権化|gonge}} and {{nihongo||化現|kegen}} are [[synonym]]s for gongen.<ref name="sos3"/> {{nihongo||権現信仰|Gongen shinkō}} is the term for belief in the existence of gongen.<ref name="sos3"/>
The gongen concept is the cornerstone of the [[honji suijaku]] theory, according to which Buddhist deities choose to appear to the Japanese as native kami in order to save them, which is based on the [[Mahayana|Mahayana Buddhist]] notion of [[upaya]], "expedient means".
== History == [[File:NikkoToriiTablet5127.jpg|left|thumb|Tablet on [[torii]] at [[Nikkō Tōshō-gū]] reads "Tōshō Daigongen" (calligraphy by [[Emperor Go-Mizunoo]])]]It is sometimes assumed that the word ''gongen'' derives from [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]]'s posthumous name (Tōshō Daigongen). However, the term was created and started being used in the middle of the [[Heian period]] in an effort to harmonize Buddhism and [[ethnic religion|indigenous religious practice]] in what is called shinbutsu-shūgō or "[[syncretism]] of kami and buddhas".<ref name="TY87">Tamura (2000:87)</ref> At that time, the assumption that Japanese ''kami'' and buddhas were essentially the same evolved into a theory called {{nihongo|''honji suijaku''|本地垂迹}}, which held that native kami were manifestations or avatars of buddhas, [[bodhisattva]]s and other Buddhist deities.<ref name="TY87"/> The theory gradually spread around the country and the concept of gongen, a dual entity composed of a buddha and a kami, evolved.
Under the influence of [[Tendai|Tendai Buddhism]] and [[Shugendō]], the gongen concept was adapted to religious beliefs tied to [[Mount Iwaki]], a volcano, so that female kami Kuniyasutamahime became associated with [[Avalokiteśvara]] ''ekadaśamukha'' (Jūichimen Kannon Bosatsu, "Eleven-Faced [[Guanyin]]"), [[Ōkuninushi]] with [[Bhaisajyaguru]] (Yakushi Nyōrai) and [[Kuninotokotachi]] with [[Amitābha]] (Amida Nyōrai).<ref>Breen, Teeuwen (2000:194)</ref>
The title "gongen" started being attached to the names of kami and shrines were built within the premises of large Buddhist temples to enshrine their tutelary kami.<ref name="TY87">Tamura (2000:87)</ref> During the Japanese Middle Ages, shrines started being called with the name gongen to underline their ties to Buddhism.<ref name="sos3">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=790 Gongen shinkō], accessed on October 5, 2008</ref> For example, in Eastern Japan there are still many [[Mount Haku]] shrines where the shrine itself is called either gongen or jinja.<ref name="sos3"/> Because it represents the application of Buddhist terminology to native kami, the use of the term was legally abolished in the [[Meiji Restoration]] with the {{nihongo|[[Shinbutsu bunri|Shinto and Buddhism Separation Order]]|神仏判然令|Shin-butsu Hanzenrei}} and shrines began to be called ''jinja''.<ref name="sos3"/>
=== Gongen of Japan === * {{nihongo|Izuna Gongen|飯網の権現}}, also called "Izuna Myōjin" and enshrined in Izuna Shrine in [[Nagano, Nagano|Nagano]], is similar to a [[tengu]] and represents the kami of [[Mount Iizuna]].<ref>Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=193 Izuna Gongen], accessed on October 6, 2008</ref> * {{nihongo|Izusan Gongen|伊豆山権現}} or {{nihongo|Hashiri-yu Gongen|走湯権現}} is the spirit of a hot spring on Izusan, a hill in [[Shizuoka Prefecture]], enshrined in the [[Izusan Jinja]]<ref>Jaanus, ''Izusan Gongen'', accessed on October 6, 2008.</ref> * {{nihongo|Kumano Gongen|熊野権現}}, also known as {{nihongo|Three Mountains of Kumano|熊野三山}}.<ref name="kumano">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1076 Kumano Shinkō], accessed on October 6, 2008</ref><ref>[http://www.sekaiisan-wakayama.jp/english/s_sanzan.htm Kumano Sanzan] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080609225919/http://www.sekaiisan-wakayama.jp/english/s_sanzan.htm |date=2008-06-09 }}, World Heritage Registration Association, accessed on October 13, 2008</ref> The kami enshrined in the three Kumano Sanzan Grand Shrines and worshipped in [[Kumano shrine]]s are the three Kumano mountains: Hongū, Shingū, and Nachi.<ref name="kumano"/> * {{nihongo|Seiryū Gongen|清滝権現}} was enshrined in [[Jingo-ji]] in Takao as the tutelary kami of [[Shingon Buddhism]] by [[Kūkai]].<ref>Jaanus, ''Seiryū Gongen'', accessed on October 6, 2008</ref> * {{nihongo|Tōshō Daigongen|東照大権現}} is one of the most famous examples of gongen, representing [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]] posthumously enshrined in so-called [[Tōshō-gū]] shrines present all over Japan. The original one is [[Nikkō Tōshō-gū]] in [[Nikkō, Tochigi]]. * {{nihongo|Zaō Gongen|蔵王権現}} or {{nihongo|Kongō Zaō Bosatsu|金剛蔵王菩薩}} is a deity worshiped in [[Shugendō]].<ref>Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=201 Zaō Gongen], accessed on October 6, 2008</ref> * {{nihongo|Sannō Gongen|山王権現}} or {{nihongo|Hie (alternatively ''Hiyoshi'') Sannō Daigongen|日吉山王大権現}} is a guardian deity worshiped in [[Tendai]] spread from [[Mount Hiei]]. It is treated as a Buddhist title of Shinto [[Oyamakui no Kami]].
== ''Gongen-zukuri'' == [[File:Gongen Zukuri.png|thumb|Plan of a gongen-zukuri shrine]] {{main|Ishi-no-ma-zukuri}} {{nihongo|''Gongen-zukuri''|権現造}} is the name of a complex [[Shinto shrine]] structure in which the ''[[haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'', or worship hall, and the [[honden]], or main sanctuary, are interconnected under the same roof in the shape of an H.<ref name="sos2">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_g.html#gongen-zukuri ''Gongen-zukuri''] accessed on October 5, 2008</ref><ref>For details about these terms, see the article [[Shinto shrine]].</ref> One of the oldest examples of ''gongen-zukuri'' is Kitano Tenmangū in [[Kyoto]].<ref name="sos2"/> The name comes from Nikkō Tōshō-gū in Nikkō because it enshrines the Tōshō Daigongen and adopts this structure.<ref name="Jaa">Jaanus, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/g/gongenzukuri.htm Gongen-zukuri], accessed on October 5, 2008</ref>
== See also == * The [[Glossary of Shinto]] for an explanation of terms concerning Japanese Shinto, Shinto art, and Shinto shrine architecture
==Notes==
{{Reflist|2}}

revision.text:
{{出典の明記|date=2017年8月28日 (月) 05:23 (UTC)}} [[File:Gate-nikko-japan.jpg|thumb|right|300 px|[[日光東照宮]]。[[徳川家康]]を神格化した東照大権現（本地は薬師如来）を祀る。]]
'''権現'''（ごんげん）は、日本の神の[[神号]]の一つ。[[日本]]の[[神 (神道)|神々]]を[[仏教]]の[[仏]]や[[菩薩]]が「仮の姿」で現れたものとする[[本地垂迹]]思想による神号である。'''権'''という文字は「[[権官|権大納言]]」などと同じく「臨時の」「仮の」という意味で、仏が「仮に」神の形を取って「'''現'''れた」ことを示す。
[[インド]]の[[バラモン教]]や[[ヒンドゥー教]]の神々は「天」という神号で[[護法善神]]として[[大乗仏教]]の[[天部]]に取り入れられたが、日本の神々が日本仏教に取り入れられた際には'''本地垂迹'''思想に基づき'''権現'''という神号が多く用いられた<ref group="注釈">「権現」以外の例として（実質的には権現であるが）「八幡大菩薩」や「高良大菩薩」など菩薩号を用いることもある。</ref>。権現には[[山王神道]]（[[天台宗]]）・[[両部神道]]（[[真言宗]]）に基づくものや、[[自然崇拝]]（[[山岳信仰]]）と[[修験道]]が融合したもの等があり、[[民間信仰]]においては地域の名士や不慮の死を遂げた人物などが死後に権現として祀られる例も見られる。なお、「[[明神]]号」と「権現号」が区別されずに使用される例も少なくはない。（[[愛宕権現]]＝愛宕明神、[[白山権現]]＝白山明神など）
== 神号の事例 == [[春日大社]]は[[タケミカヅチ|武甕槌命]]（一殿）・[[経津主神]]（二殿）・[[天児屋命]]（三殿）・[[比売神]]（四殿）の四柱の祭神を祀るが、[[中世]]には[[興福寺]]が[[神宮寺]]として支配的な影響力を持ったため、[[神仏習合]]に基づいて[[不空羂索観音]]（一殿）・[[薬師如来]]（二殿）・[[地蔵菩薩]]（三殿）・[[十一面観音]]（四殿）をそれぞれの本地仏とし、社にちなんで「[[春日権現]]」（もしくは[[春日神|春日明神]]）と総称した。また、複数の祭神を祀っている社では「六所権現」などと呼ぶこともあり、この場合は複数の神の総称である。神号との組み合わせにより「[[日光二荒山神社|日光三所権現]]」、「[[熊野権現|熊野十二所権現]]」などのように称される。全国的に最も多く見られるのは「[[白山権現]]」、「[[立山権現]]」、「[[羽黒権現]]」などのように、「山名（もしくは地名）＋権現号」という組み合わせである。その他、少数ではあるが「[[摩利支天]]権現」、「[[薬師如来|薬師]]権現」、「[[釈迦]]権現」のように本地仏の名前がそのまま用いられる例や、「天妃媽祖権現」（[[媽祖]]）、「徐福権現」（[[徐福]]）、「将門権現」（[[平将門]]）などのように神名や人名を用いた例、「[[猫又]]権現」、「大蛇権現」などのように動物由来の例も見られる。以上のことは明神号にも多く共通する。
== 神仏分離・廃仏毀釈 == [[明治維新]]の[[神仏分離令]]（神仏判然令）で権現社・権現宮・権現堂の多くが廃され、「権現」の神号や修験道が一時禁止されたため、権現を祀る神社の多くは本地仏を廃して祭神（垂迹神）のみを祀るようになった。祭神が不詳な権現には[[復古神道]]・[[国学]]の学説に基づき、記紀神話の神を比定した。
== Major Gongen == {{seealso|Japanese Buddhist Deities#Suijaku (Manifestation) Kami|ja|日本の仏尊#垂迹神}}
* {{ill|Atago Gongen|ja|愛宕権現}} ([[愛宕権現]]) * {{ill|Akiba Gongen|ja|秋葉権現}} ([[秋葉権現]]) * {{ill|Ishizuchi Daigongen|ja|石鎚大権現}} ([[石鎚大権現]]) * {{ill|Iizuna Gongen|ja|飯縄権現}} ([[飯縄権現]]) * {{ill|Ochi Daigongen|ja|越知大権現}} ([[越知大権現]]) – Hakusan Shugendō ([[白山修験]]) * {{ill|Kumano Gongen|ja|熊野権現}} ([[熊野権現]]) * {{ill|Konpira Gongen|ja|金毘羅権現}} ([[金毘羅権現]]) * {{ill|Sanki Daigongen|ja|三鬼大権現}} ([[三鬼大権現]]) * {{ill|Zaō Gongen|ja|蔵王権現}} ([[蔵王権現]]) * {{ill|Suzuka Gongen|ja|片山神社 (亀山市)|lt=Suzuka Gongen}} ([[片山神社 (亀山市)|鈴鹿権現]]) – {{ill|Shamanism|ja|巫覡}} ([[巫覡]]) * {{ill|Seiryū Gongen|ja|清瀧権現}} ([[清瀧権現]]) * {{ill|Tateyama Gongen|ja|立山権現}} ([[立山権現]]) – Tateyama Shugendō ([[立山修験]]) * {{ill|Chimyō Gongen|ja|智明権現}} ([[智明権現]]) * {{ill|Nezu Gongen|ja|根津神社#歴史|lt=Nezu Gongen}} ([[根津神社#歴史|根津権現]]) * {{ill|Hakusan Gongen|ja|白山権現}} ([[白山権現]]) – Hakusan Shugendō ([[白山修験]]) * {{ill|Haguro Gongen|ja|羽黒権現}} ([[羽黒権現]]) – Haguro Shugendō ([[羽黒修験]]) * {{ill|Hakone Gongen|ja|箱根権現}} ([[箱根権現]]) * {{ill|Hachiōji Gongen|ja|八王子権現|1=Hachiōji Gongen}} ([[八王子権現]]) * {{ill|Hirō Gongen|ja|飛瀧権現}} ([[飛瀧権現]]) * {{ill|Sannō Gongen|ja|山王権現|lt=Hiyoshi Sannō Gongen}} ([[山王権現|日吉山王権現]]) – {{ill|Sannō Shintō|ja|山王神道}} ([[山王神道]]) * {{ill|Futarasan Gongen|ja|二荒権現}} ([[二荒権現]]) – Nikkō Shugendō ([[日光修験]]) * {{ill|Yuga Daigongen|ja|瑜伽大権現}} ([[瑜伽大権現]]) * {{ill|Hikosan Gongen|ja|英彦山|lt=Hikosan Gongen}} ([[英彦山|英彦山権現]]) * {{ill|Shiramine Daigongen|ja|白峯大権現}} ([[白峯大権現]])
{{seealso|日本の仏尊#垂迹神}} * [[愛宕権現]] * [[秋葉権現]] * 石鎚大権現 * [[飯縄権現]] * [[越知大権現]] - 白山修験 * [[熊野権現]] * [[金毘羅権現]] * [[三鬼大権現]] * [[蔵王権現]] * [[片山神社 (亀山市)|鈴鹿権現]] - [[巫覡]] * [[清瀧権現]] * [[立山権現]] - [[立山修験]] * [[智明権現]] * [[根津神社#歴史|根津権現]] * [[白山権現]] - 白山修験 * [[羽黒権現]] - 羽黒修験 * [[箱根権現]] * [[八王子権現]] * 飛瀧権現 * [[山王権現|日吉山王権現]] - [[山王神道]] * 二荒権現 - [[日光修験]] * [[瑜伽大権現]] * [[英彦山|英彦山権現]] * 白峯大権現
== 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} === 注釈 === {{Notelist}}
== See Also == *[[日本の仏尊]] *[[徳川家康]] - 没後に薬師如来を本地とする「東照大権現」として神格化された。 *[[権現造]] *[[化身]]
== 外部リンク == *{{Commonscat-inline}}
{{Buddhism2}} {{神道 横}} {{buddhism-stub}} {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:こんけん}} [[Category:Gongen]][[Category:Gongen]][[Category:神仏習合]] [[Category:Shinto-Buddhist syncretism]] [[Category:Gongen]][[Category:修験道]] [[Category:Shugendo]] [[Category:untranslated pages]][[ja:権現]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[Category:Mass editing to refresh the first new Wikipedia redirects]]
{{Italic title|reason=[[:Category:Japanese words and phrases]]}} [[File:Gate-nikko-japan.jpg|thumb|250px|[[Nikkō Tōshō-gū]] enshrines [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]] under the posthumous name of Tōshō Daigongen]] {{main|Honji suijaku}} A {{nihongo||権現|'''''gongen'''''}}, literally "incarnation", was believed to be the manifestation of a [[Buddhahood|buddha]] in the form of an indigenous [[kami]], an entity who had come to guide the people to [[salvation]], during the era of [[shinbutsu-shūgō]] in premodern Japan.<ref name="SoS">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_g.html#gongen ''Gongen''] accessed on October 5, 2008</ref><ref name="TY87">Tamura (2000:87)</ref> The words {{nihongo||権化|gonge}} and {{nihongo||化現|kegen}} are [[synonym]]s for gongen.<ref name="sos3"/> {{nihongo||権現信仰|Gongen shinkō}} is the term for belief in the existence of gongen.<ref name="sos3"/>
The gongen concept is the cornerstone of the [[honji suijaku]] theory, according to which Buddhist deities choose to appear to the Japanese as native kami in order to save them, which is based on the [[Mahayana|Mahayana Buddhist]] notion of [[upaya]], "expedient means".
== History == [[File:NikkoToriiTablet5127.jpg|left|thumb|Tablet on [[torii]] at [[Nikkō Tōshō-gū]] reads "Tōshō Daigongen" (calligraphy by [[Emperor Go-Mizunoo]])]]It is sometimes assumed that the word ''gongen'' derives from [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]]'s posthumous name (Tōshō Daigongen). However, the term was created and started being used in the middle of the [[Heian period]] in an effort to harmonize Buddhism and [[ethnic religion|indigenous religious practice]] in what is called shinbutsu-shūgō or "[[syncretism]] of kami and buddhas".<ref name="TY87">Tamura (2000:87)</ref> At that time, the assumption that Japanese ''kami'' and buddhas were essentially the same evolved into a theory called {{nihongo|''honji suijaku''|本地垂迹}}, which held that native kami were manifestations or avatars of buddhas, [[bodhisattva]]s and other Buddhist deities.<ref name="TY87"/> The theory gradually spread around the country and the concept of gongen, a dual entity composed of a buddha and a kami, evolved.
Under the influence of [[Tendai|Tendai Buddhism]] and [[Shugendō]], the gongen concept was adapted to religious beliefs tied to [[Mount Iwaki]], a volcano, so that female kami Kuniyasutamahime became associated with [[Avalokiteśvara]] ''ekadaśamukha'' (Jūichimen Kannon Bosatsu, "Eleven-Faced [[Guanyin]]"), [[Ōkuninushi]] with [[Bhaisajyaguru]] (Yakushi Nyōrai) and [[Kuninotokotachi]] with [[Amitābha]] (Amida Nyōrai).<ref>Breen, Teeuwen (2000:194)</ref>
The title "gongen" started being attached to the names of kami and shrines were built within the premises of large Buddhist temples to enshrine their tutelary kami.<ref name="TY87">Tamura (2000:87)</ref> During the Japanese Middle Ages, shrines started being called with the name gongen to underline their ties to Buddhism.<ref name="sos3">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=790 Gongen shinkō], accessed on October 5, 2008</ref> For example, in Eastern Japan there are still many [[Mount Haku]] shrines where the shrine itself is called either gongen or jinja.<ref name="sos3"/> Because it represents the application of Buddhist terminology to native kami, the use of the term was legally abolished in the [[Meiji Restoration]] with the {{nihongo|[[Shinbutsu bunri|Shinto and Buddhism Separation Order]]|神仏判然令|Shin-butsu Hanzenrei}} and shrines began to be called ''jinja''.<ref name="sos3"/>
=== Gongen of Japan === * {{nihongo|Izuna Gongen|飯網の権現}}, also called "Izuna Myōjin" and enshrined in Izuna Shrine in [[Nagano, Nagano|Nagano]], is similar to a [[tengu]] and represents the kami of [[Mount Iizuna]].<ref>Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=193 Izuna Gongen], accessed on October 6, 2008</ref> * {{nihongo|Izusan Gongen|伊豆山権現}} or {{nihongo|Hashiri-yu Gongen|走湯権現}} is the spirit of a hot spring on Izusan, a hill in [[Shizuoka Prefecture]], enshrined in the [[Izusan Jinja]]<ref>Jaanus, ''Izusan Gongen'', accessed on October 6, 2008.</ref> * {{nihongo|Kumano Gongen|熊野権現}}, also known as {{nihongo|Three Mountains of Kumano|熊野三山}}.<ref name="kumano">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1076 Kumano Shinkō], accessed on October 6, 2008</ref><ref>[http://www.sekaiisan-wakayama.jp/english/s_sanzan.htm Kumano Sanzan] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080609225919/http://www.sekaiisan-wakayama.jp/english/s_sanzan.htm |date=2008-06-09 }}, World Heritage Registration Association, accessed on October 13, 2008</ref> The kami enshrined in the three Kumano Sanzan Grand Shrines and worshipped in [[Kumano shrine]]s are the three Kumano mountains: Hongū, Shingū, and Nachi.<ref name="kumano"/> * {{nihongo|Seiryū Gongen|清滝権現}} was enshrined in [[Jingo-ji]] in Takao as the tutelary kami of [[Shingon Buddhism]] by [[Kūkai]].<ref>Jaanus, ''Seiryū Gongen'', accessed on October 6, 2008</ref> * {{nihongo|Tōshō Daigongen|東照大権現}} is one of the most famous examples of gongen, representing [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]] posthumously enshrined in so-called [[Tōshō-gū]] shrines present all over Japan. The original one is [[Nikkō Tōshō-gū]] in [[Nikkō, Tochigi]]. * {{nihongo|Zaō Gongen|蔵王権現}} or {{nihongo|Kongō Zaō Bosatsu|金剛蔵王菩薩}} is a deity worshiped in [[Shugendō]].<ref>Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=201 Zaō Gongen], accessed on October 6, 2008</ref> * {{nihongo|Sannō Gongen|山王権現}} or {{nihongo|Hie (alternatively ''Hiyoshi'') Sannō Daigongen|日吉山王大権現}} is a guardian deity worshiped in [[Tendai]] spread from [[Mount Hiei]]. It is treated as a Buddhist title of Shinto [[Oyamakui no Kami]].
== ''Gongen-zukuri'' == [[File:Gongen Zukuri.png|thumb|Plan of a gongen-zukuri shrine]] {{main|Ishi-no-ma-zukuri}} {{nihongo|''Gongen-zukuri''|権現造}} is the name of a complex [[Shinto shrine]] structure in which the ''[[haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'', or worship hall, and the [[honden]], or main sanctuary, are interconnected under the same roof in the shape of an H.<ref name="sos2">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_g.html#gongen-zukuri ''Gongen-zukuri''] accessed on October 5, 2008</ref><ref>For details about these terms, see the article [[Shinto shrine]].</ref> One of the oldest examples of ''gongen-zukuri'' is Kitano Tenmangū in [[Kyoto]].<ref name="sos2"/> The name comes from Nikkō Tōshō-gū in Nikkō because it enshrines the Tōshō Daigongen and adopts this structure.<ref name="Jaa">Jaanus, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/g/gongenzukuri.htm Gongen-zukuri], accessed on October 5, 2008</ref>
== See also == * The [[Glossary of Shinto]] for an explanation of terms concerning Japanese Shinto, Shinto art, and Shinto shrine architecture
==Notes==

{{出典の明記|date=2017年8月28日 (月) 05:23 (UTC)}} [[File:Gate-nikko-japan.jpg|thumb|right|300 px|[[日光東照宮]]。[[徳川家康]]を神格化した東照大権現（本地は薬師如来）を祀る。]]
'''権現'''（ごんげん）は、日本の神の[[神号]]の一つ。[[日本]]の[[神 (神道)|神々]]を[[仏教]]の[[仏]]や[[菩薩]]が「仮の姿」で現れたものとする[[本地垂迹]]思想による神号である。'''権'''という文字は「[[権官|権大納言]]」などと同じく「臨時の」「仮の」という意味で、仏が「仮に」神の形を取って「'''現'''れた」ことを示す。
[[インド]]の[[バラモン教]]や[[ヒンドゥー教]]の神々は「天」という神号で[[護法善神]]として[[大乗仏教]]の[[天部]]に取り入れられたが、日本の神々が日本仏教に取り入れられた際には'''本地垂迹'''思想に基づき'''権現'''という神号が多く用いられた<ref group="注釈">「権現」以外の例として（実質的には権現であるが）「八幡大菩薩」や「高良大菩薩」など菩薩号を用いることもある。</ref>。権現には[[山王神道]]（[[天台宗]]）・[[両部神道]]（[[真言宗]]）に基づくものや、[[自然崇拝]]（[[山岳信仰]]）と[[修験道]]が融合したもの等があり、[[民間信仰]]においては地域の名士や不慮の死を遂げた人物などが死後に権現として祀られる例も見られる。なお、「[[明神]]号」と「権現号」が区別されずに使用される例も少なくはない。（[[愛宕権現]]＝愛宕明神、[[白山権現]]＝白山明神など）
== 神号の事例 == [[春日大社]]は[[タケミカヅチ|武甕槌命]]（一殿）・[[経津主神]]（二殿）・[[天児屋命]]（三殿）・[[比売神]]（四殿）の四柱の祭神を祀るが、[[中世]]には[[興福寺]]が[[神宮寺]]として支配的な影響力を持ったため、[[神仏習合]]に基づいて[[不空羂索観音]]（一殿）・[[薬師如来]]（二殿）・[[地蔵菩薩]]（三殿）・[[十一面観音]]（四殿）をそれぞれの本地仏とし、社にちなんで「[[春日権現]]」（もしくは[[春日神|春日明神]]）と総称した。また、複数の祭神を祀っている社では「六所権現」などと呼ぶこともあり、この場合は複数の神の総称である。神号との組み合わせにより「[[日光二荒山神社|日光三所権現]]」、「[[熊野権現|熊野十二所権現]]」などのように称される。全国的に最も多く見られるのは「[[白山権現]]」、「[[立山権現]]」、「[[羽黒権現]]」などのように、「山名（もしくは地名）＋権現号」という組み合わせである。その他、少数ではあるが「[[摩利支天]]権現」、「[[薬師如来|薬師]]権現」、「[[釈迦]]権現」のように本地仏の名前がそのまま用いられる例や、「天妃媽祖権現」（[[媽祖]]）、「徐福権現」（[[徐福]]）、「将門権現」（[[平将門]]）などのように神名や人名を用いた例、「[[猫又]]権現」、「大蛇権現」などのように動物由来の例も見られる。以上のことは明神号にも多く共通する。
== 神仏分離・廃仏毀釈 == [[明治維新]]の[[神仏分離令]]（神仏判然令）で権現社・権現宮・権現堂の多くが廃され、「権現」の神号や修験道が一時禁止されたため、権現を祀る神社の多くは本地仏を廃して祭神（垂迹神）のみを祀るようになった。祭神が不詳な権現には[[復古神道]]・[[国学]]の学説に基づき、記紀神話の神を比定した。
== Major Gongen == {{seealso|Japanese Buddhist Deities#Suijaku (Manifestation) Kami|ja|日本の仏尊#垂迹神}}

== 外部リンク == *{{Commonscat-inline}}
{{Buddhism2}} {{神道 横}} {{buddhism-stub}} {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:こんけん}} [[Category:Gongen]][[Category:Gongen]][[Category:神仏習合]] [[Category:Shinto-Buddhist syncretism]] [[Category:Gongen]][[Category:修験道]] [[Category:Shugendo]] [[Category:untranslated pages]][[ja:権現]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[Category:Mass editing to refresh the first new Wikipedia redirects]]
{{Italic title|reason=[[:Category:Japanese words and phrases]]}} [[File:Gate-nikko-japan.jpg|thumb|250px|[[Nikkō Tōshō-gū]] enshrines [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]] under the posthumous name of Tōshō Daigongen]] {{main|Honji suijaku}} A {{nihongo||権現|'''''gongen'''''}}, literally "incarnation", was believed to be the manifestation of a [[Buddhahood|buddha]] in the form of an indigenous [[kami]], an entity who had come to guide the people to [[salvation]], during the era of [[shinbutsu-shūgō]] in premodern Japan.<ref name="SoS">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_g.html#gongen ''Gongen''] accessed on October 5, 2008</ref><ref name="TY87">Tamura (2000:87)</ref> The words {{nihongo||権化|gonge}} and {{nihongo||化現|kegen}} are [[synonym]]s for gongen.<ref name="sos3"/> {{nihongo||権現信仰|Gongen shinkō}} is the term for belief in the existence of gongen.<ref name="sos3"/>
The gongen concept is the cornerstone of the [[honji suijaku]] theory, according to which Buddhist deities choose to appear to the Japanese as native kami in order to save them, which is based on the [[Mahayana|Mahayana Buddhist]] notion of [[upaya]], "expedient means".
== History == [[File:NikkoToriiTablet5127.jpg|left|thumb|Tablet on [[torii]] at [[Nikkō Tōshō-gū]] reads "Tōshō Daigongen" (calligraphy by [[Emperor Go-Mizunoo]])]]It is sometimes assumed that the word ''gongen'' derives from [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]]'s posthumous name (Tōshō Daigongen). However, the term was created and started being used in the middle of the [[Heian period]] in an effort to harmonize Buddhism and [[ethnic religion|indigenous religious practice]] in what is called shinbutsu-shūgō or "[[syncretism]] of kami and buddhas".<ref name="TY87">Tamura (2000:87)</ref> At that time, the assumption that Japanese ''kami'' and buddhas were essentially the same evolved into a theory called {{nihongo|''honji suijaku''|本地垂迹}}, which held that native kami were manifestations or avatars of buddhas, [[bodhisattva]]s and other Buddhist deities.<ref name="TY87"/> The theory gradually spread around the country and the concept of gongen, a dual entity composed of a buddha and a kami, evolved.
Under the influence of [[Tendai|Tendai Buddhism]] and [[Shugendō]], the gongen concept was adapted to religious beliefs tied to [[Mount Iwaki]], a volcano, so that female kami Kuniyasutamahime became associated with [[Avalokiteśvara]] ''ekadaśamukha'' (Jūichimen Kannon Bosatsu, "Eleven-Faced [[Guanyin]]"), [[Ōkuninushi]] with [[Bhaisajyaguru]] (Yakushi Nyōrai) and [[Kuninotokotachi]] with [[Amitābha]] (Amida Nyōrai).<ref>Breen, Teeuwen (2000:194)</ref>
The title "gongen" started being attached to the names of kami and shrines were built within the premises of large Buddhist temples to enshrine their tutelary kami.<ref name="TY87">Tamura (2000:87)</ref> During the Japanese Middle Ages, shrines started being called with the name gongen to underline their ties to Buddhism.<ref name="sos3">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=790 Gongen shinkō], accessed on October 5, 2008</ref> For example, in Eastern Japan there are still many [[Mount Haku]] shrines where the shrine itself is called either gongen or jinja.<ref name="sos3"/> Because it represents the application of Buddhist terminology to native kami, the use of the term was legally abolished in the [[Meiji Restoration]] with the {{nihongo|[[Shinbutsu bunri|Shinto and Buddhism Separation Order]]|神仏判然令|Shin-butsu Hanzenrei}} and shrines began to be called ''jinja''.<ref name="sos3"/>
=== Gongen of Japan === * {{nihongo|Izuna Gongen|飯網の権現}}, also called "Izuna Myōjin" and enshrined in Izuna Shrine in [[Nagano, Nagano|Nagano]], is similar to a [[tengu]] and represents the kami of [[Mount Iizuna]].<ref>Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=193 Izuna Gongen], accessed on October 6, 2008</ref> * {{nihongo|Izusan Gongen|伊豆山権現}} or {{nihongo|Hashiri-yu Gongen|走湯権現}} is the spirit of a hot spring on Izusan, a hill in [[Shizuoka Prefecture]], enshrined in the [[Izusan Jinja]]<ref>Jaanus, ''Izusan Gongen'', accessed on October 6, 2008.</ref> * {{nihongo|Kumano Gongen|熊野権現}}, also known as {{nihongo|Three Mountains of Kumano|熊野三山}}.<ref name="kumano">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1076 Kumano Shinkō], accessed on October 6, 2008</ref><ref>[http://www.sekaiisan-wakayama.jp/english/s_sanzan.htm Kumano Sanzan] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080609225919/http://www.sekaiisan-wakayama.jp/english/s_sanzan.htm |date=2008-06-09 }}, World Heritage Registration Association, accessed on October 13, 2008</ref> The kami enshrined in the three Kumano Sanzan Grand Shrines and worshipped in [[Kumano shrine]]s are the three Kumano mountains: Hongū, Shingū, and Nachi.<ref name="kumano"/> * {{nihongo|Seiryū Gongen|清滝権現}} was enshrined in [[Jingo-ji]] in Takao as the tutelary kami of [[Shingon Buddhism]] by [[Kūkai]].<ref>Jaanus, ''Seiryū Gongen'', accessed on October 6, 2008</ref> * {{nihongo|Tōshō Daigongen|東照大権現}} is one of the most famous examples of gongen, representing [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]] posthumously enshrined in so-called [[Tōshō-gū]] shrines present all over Japan. The original one is [[Nikkō Tōshō-gū]] in [[Nikkō, Tochigi]]. * {{nihongo|Zaō Gongen|蔵王権現}} or {{nihongo|Kongō Zaō Bosatsu|金剛蔵王菩薩}} is a deity worshiped in [[Shugendō]].<ref>Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=201 Zaō Gongen], accessed on October 6, 2008</ref> * {{nihongo|Sannō Gongen|山王権現}} or {{nihongo|Hie (alternatively ''Hiyoshi'') Sannō Daigongen|日吉山王大権現}} is a guardian deity worshiped in [[Tendai]] spread from [[Mount Hiei]]. It is treated as a Buddhist title of Shinto [[Oyamakui no Kami]].
== ''Gongen-zukuri'' == [[File:Gongen Zukuri.png|thumb|Plan of a gongen-zukuri shrine]] {{main|Ishi-no-ma-zukuri}} {{nihongo|''Gongen-zukuri''|権現造}} is the name of a complex [[Shinto shrine]] structure in which the ''[[haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'', or worship hall, and the [[honden]], or main sanctuary, are interconnected under the same roof in the shape of an H.<ref name="sos2">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_g.html#gongen-zukuri ''Gongen-zukuri''] accessed on October 5, 2008</ref><ref>For details about these terms, see the article [[Shinto shrine]].</ref> One of the oldest examples of ''gongen-zukuri'' is Kitano Tenmangū in [[Kyoto]].<ref name="sos2"/> The name comes from Nikkō Tōshō-gū in Nikkō because it enshrines the Tōshō Daigongen and adopts this structure.<ref name="Jaa">Jaanus, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/g/gongenzukuri.htm Gongen-zukuri], accessed on October 5, 2008</ref>
== See also == * The [[Glossary of Shinto]] for an explanation of terms concerning Japanese Shinto, Shinto art, and Shinto shrine architecture

'''権現'''（ごんげん）は、日本の神の[[神号]]の一つ。[[日本]]の[[神 (神道)|神々]]を[[仏教]]の[[仏]]や[[菩薩]]が「仮の姿」で現れたものとする[[本地垂迹]]思想による神号である。'''権'''という文字は「[[権官|権大納言]]」などと同じく「臨時の」「仮の」という意味で、仏が「仮に」神の形を取って「'''現'''れた」ことを示す。
[[インド]]の[[バラモン教]]や[[ヒンドゥー教]]の神々は「天」という神号で[[護法善神]]として[[大乗仏教]]の[[天部]]に取り入れられたが、日本の神々が日本仏教に取り入れられた際には'''本地垂迹'''思想に基づき'''権現'''という神号が多く用いられた<ref group="注釈">「権現」以外の例として（実質的には権現であるが）「八幡大菩薩」や「高良大菩薩」など菩薩号を用いることもある。</ref>。権現には[[山王神道]]（[[天台宗]]）・[[両部神道]]（[[真言宗]]）に基づくものや、[[自然崇拝]]（[[山岳信仰]]）と[[修験道]]が融合したもの等があり、[[民間信仰]]においては地域の名士や不慮の死を遂げた人物などが死後に権現として祀られる例も見られる。なお、「[[明神]]号」と「権現号」が区別されずに使用される例も少なくはない。（[[愛宕権現]]＝愛宕明神、[[白山権現]]＝白山明神など）
== 神号の事例 == [[春日大社]]は[[タケミカヅチ|武甕槌命]]（一殿）・[[経津主神]]（二殿）・[[天児屋命]]（三殿）・[[比売神]]（四殿）の四柱の祭神を祀るが、[[中世]]には[[興福寺]]が[[神宮寺]]として支配的な影響力を持ったため、[[神仏習合]]に基づいて[[不空羂索観音]]（一殿）・[[薬師如来]]（二殿）・[[地蔵菩薩]]（三殿）・[[十一面観音]]（四殿）をそれぞれの本地仏とし、社にちなんで「[[春日権現]]」（もしくは[[春日神|春日明神]]）と総称した。また、複数の祭神を祀っている社では「六所権現」などと呼ぶこともあり、この場合は複数の神の総称である。神号との組み合わせにより「[[日光二荒山神社|日光三所権現]]」、「[[熊野権現|熊野十二所権現]]」などのように称される。全国的に最も多く見られるのは「[[白山権現]]」、「[[立山権現]]」、「[[羽黒権現]]」などのように、「山名（もしくは地名）＋権現号」という組み合わせである。その他、少数ではあるが「[[摩利支天]]権現」、「[[薬師如来|薬師]]権現」、「[[釈迦]]権現」のように本地仏の名前がそのまま用いられる例や、「天妃媽祖権現」（[[媽祖]]）、「徐福権現」（[[徐福]]）、「将門権現」（[[平将門]]）などのように神名や人名を用いた例、「[[猫又]]権現」、「大蛇権現」などのように動物由来の例も見られる。以上のことは明神号にも多く共通する。
== 神仏分離・廃仏毀釈 == [[明治維新]]の[[神仏分離令]]（神仏判然令）で権現社・権現宮・権現堂の多くが廃され、「権現」の神号や修験道が一時禁止されたため、権現を祀る神社の多くは本地仏を廃して祭神（垂迹神）のみを祀るようになった。祭神が不詳な権現には[[復古神道]]・[[国学]]の学説に基づき、記紀神話の神を比定した。
== Major Gongen == {{seealso|Japanese Buddhist Deities#Suijaku (Manifestation) Kami|ja|日本の仏尊#垂迹神}}
* {{ill|Atago Gongen|ja|愛宕権現}} ([[愛宕権現]]) * {{ill|Akiba Gongen|ja|秋葉権現}} ([[秋葉権現]]) * {{ill|Ishizuchi Daigongen|ja|石鎚大権現}} ([[石鎚大権現]]) * {{ill|Iizuna Gongen|ja|飯縄権現}} ([[飯縄権現]]) * {{ill|Ochi Daigongen|ja|越知大権現}} ([[越知大権現]]) – Hakusan Shugendō ([[白山修験]]) * {{ill|Kumano Gongen|ja|熊野権現}} ([[熊野権現]]) * {{ill|Konpira Gongen|ja|金毘羅権現}} ([[金毘羅権現]]) * {{ill|Sanki Daigongen|ja|三鬼大権現}} ([[三鬼大権現]]) * {{ill|Zaō Gongen|ja|蔵王権現}} ([[蔵王権現]]) * {{ill|Suzuka Gongen|ja|片山神社 (亀山市)|lt=Suzuka Gongen}} ([[片山神社 (亀山市)|鈴鹿権現]]) – {{ill|Shamanism|ja|巫覡}} ([[巫覡]]) * {{ill|Seiryū Gongen|ja|清瀧権現}} ([[清瀧権現]]) * {{ill|Tateyama Gongen|ja|立山権現}} ([[立山権現]]) – Tateyama Shugendō ([[立山修験]]) * {{ill|Chimyō Gongen|ja|智明権現}} ([[智明権現]]) * {{ill|Nezu Gongen|ja|根津神社#歴史|lt=Nezu Gongen}} ([[根津神社#歴史|根津権現]]) * {{ill|Hakusan Gongen|ja|白山権現}} ([[白山権現]]) – Hakusan Shugendō ([[白山修験]]) * {{ill|Haguro Gongen|ja|羽黒権現}} ([[羽黒権現]]) – Haguro Shugendō ([[羽黒修験]]) * {{ill|Hakone Gongen|ja|箱根権現}} ([[箱根権現]]) * {{ill|Hachiōji Gongen|ja|八王子権現|1=Hachiōji Gongen}} ([[八王子権現]]) * {{ill|Hirō Gongen|ja|飛瀧権現}} ([[飛瀧権現]]) * {{ill|Sannō Gongen|ja|山王権現|lt=Hiyoshi Sannō Gongen}} ([[山王権現|日吉山王権現]]) – {{ill|Sannō Shintō|ja|山王神道}} ([[山王神道]]) * {{ill|Futarasan Gongen|ja|二荒権現}} ([[二荒権現]]) – Nikkō Shugendō ([[日光修験]]) * {{ill|Yuga Daigongen|ja|瑜伽大権現}} ([[瑜伽大権現]]) * {{ill|Hikosan Gongen|ja|英彦山|lt=Hikosan Gongen}} ([[英彦山|英彦山権現]]) * {{ill|Shiramine Daigongen|ja|白峯大権現}} ([[白峯大権現]])

== See Also == *[[日本の仏尊]] *[[徳川家康]] - 没後に薬師如来を本地とする「東照大権現」として神格化された。 *[[権現造]] *[[化身]]
== 外部リンク == *{{Commonscat-inline}}
{{Buddhism2}} {{神道 横}} {{buddhism-stub}} {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:こんけん}} [[Category:Gongen]][[Category:Gongen]][[Category:神仏習合]] [[Category:Shinto-Buddhist syncretism]] [[Category:Gongen]][[Category:修験道]] [[Category:Shugendo]] [[Category:untranslated pages]][[ja:権現]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[Category:Mass editing to refresh the first new Wikipedia redirects]]
{{Italic title|reason=[[:Category:Japanese words and phrases]]}} [[File:Gate-nikko-japan.jpg|thumb|250px|[[Nikkō Tōshō-gū]] enshrines [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]] under the posthumous name of Tōshō Daigongen]] {{main|Honji suijaku}} A {{nihongo||権現|'''''gongen'''''}}, literally "incarnation", was believed to be the manifestation of a [[Buddhahood|buddha]] in the form of an indigenous [[kami]], an entity who had come to guide the people to [[salvation]], during the era of [[shinbutsu-shūgō]] in premodern Japan.<ref name="SoS">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_g.html#gongen ''Gongen''] accessed on October 5, 2008</ref><ref name="TY87">Tamura (2000:87)</ref> The words {{nihongo||権化|gonge}} and {{nihongo||化現|kegen}} are [[synonym]]s for gongen.<ref name="sos3"/> {{nihongo||権現信仰|Gongen shinkō}} is the term for belief in the existence of gongen.<ref name="sos3"/>
The gongen concept is the cornerstone of the [[honji suijaku]] theory, according to which Buddhist deities choose to appear to the Japanese as native kami in order to save them, which is based on the [[Mahayana|Mahayana Buddhist]] notion of [[upaya]], "expedient means".
== History == [[File:NikkoToriiTablet5127.jpg|left|thumb|Tablet on [[torii]] at [[Nikkō Tōshō-gū]] reads "Tōshō Daigongen" (calligraphy by [[Emperor Go-Mizunoo]])]]It is sometimes assumed that the word ''gongen'' derives from [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]]'s posthumous name (Tōshō Daigongen). However, the term was created and started being used in the middle of the [[Heian period]] in an effort to harmonize Buddhism and [[ethnic religion|indigenous religious practice]] in what is called shinbutsu-shūgō or "[[syncretism]] of kami and buddhas".<ref name="TY87">Tamura (2000:87)</ref> At that time, the assumption that Japanese ''kami'' and buddhas were essentially the same evolved into a theory called {{nihongo|''honji suijaku''|本地垂迹}}, which held that native kami were manifestations or avatars of buddhas, [[bodhisattva]]s and other Buddhist deities.<ref name="TY87"/> The theory gradually spread around the country and the concept of gongen, a dual entity composed of a buddha and a kami, evolved.
Under the influence of [[Tendai|Tendai Buddhism]] and [[Shugendō]], the gongen concept was adapted to religious beliefs tied to [[Mount Iwaki]], a volcano, so that female kami Kuniyasutamahime became associated with [[Avalokiteśvara]] ''ekadaśamukha'' (Jūichimen Kannon Bosatsu, "Eleven-Faced [[Guanyin]]"), [[Ōkuninushi]] with [[Bhaisajyaguru]] (Yakushi Nyōrai) and [[Kuninotokotachi]] with [[Amitābha]] (Amida Nyōrai).<ref>Breen, Teeuwen (2000:194)</ref>
The title "gongen" started being attached to the names of kami and shrines were built within the premises of large Buddhist temples to enshrine their tutelary kami.<ref name="TY87">Tamura (2000:87)</ref> During the Japanese Middle Ages, shrines started being called with the name gongen to underline their ties to Buddhism.<ref name="sos3">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=790 Gongen shinkō], accessed on October 5, 2008</ref> For example, in Eastern Japan there are still many [[Mount Haku]] shrines where the shrine itself is called either gongen or jinja.<ref name="sos3"/> Because it represents the application of Buddhist terminology to native kami, the use of the term was legally abolished in the [[Meiji Restoration]] with the {{nihongo|[[Shinbutsu bunri|Shinto and Buddhism Separation Order]]|神仏判然令|Shin-butsu Hanzenrei}} and shrines began to be called ''jinja''.<ref name="sos3"/>
=== Gongen of Japan === * {{nihongo|Izuna Gongen|飯網の権現}}, also called "Izuna Myōjin" and enshrined in Izuna Shrine in [[Nagano, Nagano|Nagano]], is similar to a [[tengu]] and represents the kami of [[Mount Iizuna]].<ref>Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=193 Izuna Gongen], accessed on October 6, 2008</ref> * {{nihongo|Izusan Gongen|伊豆山権現}} or {{nihongo|Hashiri-yu Gongen|走湯権現}} is the spirit of a hot spring on Izusan, a hill in [[Shizuoka Prefecture]], enshrined in the [[Izusan Jinja]]<ref>Jaanus, ''Izusan Gongen'', accessed on October 6, 2008.</ref> * {{nihongo|Kumano Gongen|熊野権現}}, also known as {{nihongo|Three Mountains of Kumano|熊野三山}}.<ref name="kumano">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1076 Kumano Shinkō], accessed on October 6, 2008</ref><ref>[http://www.sekaiisan-wakayama.jp/english/s_sanzan.htm Kumano Sanzan] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080609225919/http://www.sekaiisan-wakayama.jp/english/s_sanzan.htm |date=2008-06-09 }}, World Heritage Registration Association, accessed on October 13, 2008</ref> The kami enshrined in the three Kumano Sanzan Grand Shrines and worshipped in [[Kumano shrine]]s are the three Kumano mountains: Hongū, Shingū, and Nachi.<ref name="kumano"/> * {{nihongo|Seiryū Gongen|清滝権現}} was enshrined in [[Jingo-ji]] in Takao as the tutelary kami of [[Shingon Buddhism]] by [[Kūkai]].<ref>Jaanus, ''Seiryū Gongen'', accessed on October 6, 2008</ref> * {{nihongo|Tōshō Daigongen|東照大権現}} is one of the most famous examples of gongen, representing [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]] posthumously enshrined in so-called [[Tōshō-gū]] shrines present all over Japan. The original one is [[Nikkō Tōshō-gū]] in [[Nikkō, Tochigi]]. * {{nihongo|Zaō Gongen|蔵王権現}} or {{nihongo|Kongō Zaō Bosatsu|金剛蔵王菩薩}} is a deity worshiped in [[Shugendō]].<ref>Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=201 Zaō Gongen], accessed on October 6, 2008</ref> * {{nihongo|Sannō Gongen|山王権現}} or {{nihongo|Hie (alternatively ''Hiyoshi'') Sannō Daigongen|日吉山王大権現}} is a guardian deity worshiped in [[Tendai]] spread from [[Mount Hiei]]. It is treated as a Buddhist title of Shinto [[Oyamakui no Kami]].
== ''Gongen-zukuri'' == [[File:Gongen Zukuri.png|thumb|Plan of a gongen-zukuri shrine]] {{main|Ishi-no-ma-zukuri}} {{nihongo|''Gongen-zukuri''|権現造}} is the name of a complex [[Shinto shrine]] structure in which the ''[[haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'', or worship hall, and the [[honden]], or main sanctuary, are interconnected under the same roof in the shape of an H.<ref name="sos2">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_g.html#gongen-zukuri ''Gongen-zukuri''] accessed on October 5, 2008</ref><ref>For details about these terms, see the article [[Shinto shrine]].</ref> One of the oldest examples of ''gongen-zukuri'' is Kitano Tenmangū in [[Kyoto]].<ref name="sos2"/> The name comes from Nikkō Tōshō-gū in Nikkō because it enshrines the Tōshō Daigongen and adopts this structure.<ref name="Jaa">Jaanus, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/g/gongenzukuri.htm Gongen-zukuri], accessed on October 5, 2008</ref>
== See also == * The [[Glossary of Shinto]] for an explanation of terms concerning Japanese Shinto, Shinto art, and Shinto shrine architecture

{{出典の明記|date=2017年8月28日 (月) 05:23 (UTC)}} [[File:Gate-nikko-japan.jpg|thumb|right|300 px|[[日光東照宮]]。[[徳川家康]]を神格化した東照大権現（本地は薬師如来）を祀る。]]
'''権現'''（ごんげん）は、日本の神の[[神号]]の一つ。[[日本]]の[[神 (神道)|神々]]を[[仏教]]の[[仏]]や[[菩薩]]が「仮の姿」で現れたものとする[[本地垂迹]]思想による神号である。'''権'''という文字は「[[権官|権大納言]]」などと同じく「臨時の」「仮の」という意味で、仏が「仮に」神の形を取って「'''現'''れた」ことを示す。
[[インド]]の[[バラモン教]]や[[ヒンドゥー教]]の神々は「天」という神号で[[護法善神]]として[[大乗仏教]]の[[天部]]に取り入れられたが、日本の神々が日本仏教に取り入れられた際には'''本地垂迹'''思想に基づき'''権現'''という神号が多く用いられた<ref group="注釈">「権現」以外の例として（実質的には権現であるが）「八幡大菩薩」や「高良大菩薩」など菩薩号を用いることもある。</ref>。権現には[[山王神道]]（[[天台宗]]）・[[両部神道]]（[[真言宗]]）に基づくものや、[[自然崇拝]]（[[山岳信仰]]）と[[修験道]]が融合したもの等があり、[[民間信仰]]においては地域の名士や不慮の死を遂げた人物などが死後に権現として祀られる例も見られる。なお、「[[明神]]号」と「権現号」が区別されずに使用される例も少なくはない。（[[愛宕権現]]＝愛宕明神、[[白山権現]]＝白山明神など）
== 神号の事例 == [[春日大社]]は[[タケミカヅチ|武甕槌命]]（一殿）・[[経津主神]]（二殿）・[[天児屋命]]（三殿）・[[比売神]]（四殿）の四柱の祭神を祀るが、[[中世]]には[[興福寺]]が[[神宮寺]]として支配的な影響力を持ったため、[[神仏習合]]に基づいて[[不空羂索観音]]（一殿）・[[薬師如来]]（二殿）・[[地蔵菩薩]]（三殿）・[[十一面観音]]（四殿）をそれぞれの本地仏とし、社にちなんで「[[春日権現]]」（もしくは[[春日神|春日明神]]）と総称した。また、複数の祭神を祀っている社では「六所権現」などと呼ぶこともあり、この場合は複数の神の総称である。神号との組み合わせにより「[[日光二荒山神社|日光三所権現]]」、「[[熊野権現|熊野十二所権現]]」などのように称される。全国的に最も多く見られるのは「[[白山権現]]」、「[[立山権現]]」、「[[羽黒権現]]」などのように、「山名（もしくは地名）＋権現号」という組み合わせである。その他、少数ではあるが「[[摩利支天]]権現」、「[[薬師如来|薬師]]権現」、「[[釈迦]]権現」のように本地仏の名前がそのまま用いられる例や、「天妃媽祖権現」（[[媽祖]]）、「徐福権現」（[[徐福]]）、「将門権現」（[[平将門]]）などのように神名や人名を用いた例、「[[猫又]]権現」、「大蛇権現」などのように動物由来の例も見られる。以上のことは明神号にも多く共通する。
== 神仏分離・廃仏毀釈 == [[明治維新]]の[[神仏分離令]]（神仏判然令）で権現社・権現宮・権現堂の多くが廃され、「権現」の神号や修験道が一時禁止されたため、権現を祀る神社の多くは本地仏を廃して祭神（垂迹神）のみを祀るようになった。祭神が不詳な権現には[[復古神道]]・[[国学]]の学説に基づき、記紀神話の神を比定した。
== Major Gongen == {{seealso|Japanese Buddhist Deities#Suijaku (Manifestation) Kami|ja|日本の仏尊#垂迹神}}

== 外部リンク == *{{Commonscat-inline}}
{{Buddhism2}} {{神道 横}} {{buddhism-stub}} {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:こんけん}} [[Category:Gongen]][[Category:Gongen]][[Category:神仏習合]] [[Category:Shinto-Buddhist syncretism]] [[Category:Gongen]][[Category:修験道]] [[Category:Shugendo]] [[Category:untranslated pages]][[ja:権現]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[Category:Mass editing to refresh the first new Wikipedia redirects]]
{{Italic title|reason=[[:Category:Japanese words and phrases]]}} [[File:Gate-nikko-japan.jpg|thumb|250px|[[Nikkō Tōshō-gū]] enshrines [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]] under the posthumous name of Tōshō Daigongen]] {{main|Honji suijaku}} A {{nihongo||権現|'''''gongen'''''}}, literally "incarnation", was believed to be the manifestation of a [[Buddhahood|buddha]] in the form of an indigenous [[kami]], an entity who had come to guide the people to [[salvation]], during the era of [[shinbutsu-shūgō]] in premodern Japan.<ref name="SoS">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_g.html#gongen ''Gongen''] accessed on October 5, 2008</ref><ref name="TY87">Tamura (2000:87)</ref> The words {{nihongo||権化|gonge}} and {{nihongo||化現|kegen}} are [[synonym]]s for gongen.<ref name="sos3"/> {{nihongo||権現信仰|Gongen shinkō}} is the term for belief in the existence of gongen.<ref name="sos3"/>
The gongen concept is the cornerstone of the [[honji suijaku]] theory, according to which Buddhist deities choose to appear to the Japanese as native kami in order to save them, which is based on the [[Mahayana|Mahayana Buddhist]] notion of [[upaya]], "expedient means".
== History == [[File:NikkoToriiTablet5127.jpg|left|thumb|Tablet on [[torii]] at [[Nikkō Tōshō-gū]] reads "Tōshō Daigongen" (calligraphy by [[Emperor Go-Mizunoo]])]]It is sometimes assumed that the word ''gongen'' derives from [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]]'s posthumous name (Tōshō Daigongen). However, the term was created and started being used in the middle of the [[Heian period]] in an effort to harmonize Buddhism and [[ethnic religion|indigenous religious practice]] in what is called shinbutsu-shūgō or "[[syncretism]] of kami and buddhas".<ref name="TY87">Tamura (2000:87)</ref> At that time, the assumption that Japanese ''kami'' and buddhas were essentially the same evolved into a theory called {{nihongo|''honji suijaku''|本地垂迹}}, which held that native kami were manifestations or avatars of buddhas, [[bodhisattva]]s and other Buddhist deities.<ref name="TY87"/> The theory gradually spread around the country and the concept of gongen, a dual entity composed of a buddha and a kami, evolved.
Under the influence of [[Tendai|Tendai Buddhism]] and [[Shugendō]], the gongen concept was adapted to religious beliefs tied to [[Mount Iwaki]], a volcano, so that female kami Kuniyasutamahime became associated with [[Avalokiteśvara]] ''ekadaśamukha'' (Jūichimen Kannon Bosatsu, "Eleven-Faced [[Guanyin]]"), [[Ōkuninushi]] with [[Bhaisajyaguru]] (Yakushi Nyōrai) and [[Kuninotokotachi]] with [[Amitābha]] (Amida Nyōrai).<ref>Breen, Teeuwen (2000:194)</ref>
The title "gongen" started being attached to the names of kami and shrines were built within the premises of large Buddhist temples to enshrine their tutelary kami.<ref name="TY87">Tamura (2000:87)</ref> During the Japanese Middle Ages, shrines started being called with the name gongen to underline their ties to Buddhism.<ref name="sos3">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=790 Gongen shinkō], accessed on October 5, 2008</ref> For example, in Eastern Japan there are still many [[Mount Haku]] shrines where the shrine itself is called either gongen or jinja.<ref name="sos3"/> Because it represents the application of Buddhist terminology to native kami, the use of the term was legally abolished in the [[Meiji Restoration]] with the {{nihongo|[[Shinbutsu bunri|Shinto and Buddhism Separation Order]]|神仏判然令|Shin-butsu Hanzenrei}} and shrines began to be called ''jinja''.<ref name="sos3"/>
=== Gongen of Japan === * {{nihongo|Izuna Gongen|飯網の権現}}, also called "Izuna Myōjin" and enshrined in Izuna Shrine in [[Nagano, Nagano|Nagano]], is similar to a [[tengu]] and represents the kami of [[Mount Iizuna]].<ref>Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=193 Izuna Gongen], accessed on October 6, 2008</ref> * {{nihongo|Izusan Gongen|伊豆山権現}} or {{nihongo|Hashiri-yu Gongen|走湯権現}} is the spirit of a hot spring on Izusan, a hill in [[Shizuoka Prefecture]], enshrined in the [[Izusan Jinja]]<ref>Jaanus, ''Izusan Gongen'', accessed on October 6, 2008.</ref> * {{nihongo|Kumano Gongen|熊野権現}}, also known as {{nihongo|Three Mountains of Kumano|熊野三山}}.<ref name="kumano">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1076 Kumano Shinkō], accessed on October 6, 2008</ref><ref>[http://www.sekaiisan-wakayama.jp/english/s_sanzan.htm Kumano Sanzan] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080609225919/http://www.sekaiisan-wakayama.jp/english/s_sanzan.htm |date=2008-06-09 }}, World Heritage Registration Association, accessed on October 13, 2008</ref> The kami enshrined in the three Kumano Sanzan Grand Shrines and worshipped in [[Kumano shrine]]s are the three Kumano mountains: Hongū, Shingū, and Nachi.<ref name="kumano"/> * {{nihongo|Seiryū Gongen|清滝権現}} was enshrined in [[Jingo-ji]] in Takao as the tutelary kami of [[Shingon Buddhism]] by [[Kūkai]].<ref>Jaanus, ''Seiryū Gongen'', accessed on October 6, 2008</ref> * {{nihongo|Tōshō Daigongen|東照大権現}} is one of the most famous examples of gongen, representing [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]] posthumously enshrined in so-called [[Tōshō-gū]] shrines present all over Japan. The original one is [[Nikkō Tōshō-gū]] in [[Nikkō, Tochigi]]. * {{nihongo|Zaō Gongen|蔵王権現}} or {{nihongo|Kongō Zaō Bosatsu|金剛蔵王菩薩}} is a deity worshiped in [[Shugendō]].<ref>Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=201 Zaō Gongen], accessed on October 6, 2008</ref> * {{nihongo|Sannō Gongen|山王権現}} or {{nihongo|Hie (alternatively ''Hiyoshi'') Sannō Daigongen|日吉山王大権現}} is a guardian deity worshiped in [[Tendai]] spread from [[Mount Hiei]]. It is treated as a Buddhist title of Shinto [[Oyamakui no Kami]].
== ''Gongen-zukuri'' == [[File:Gongen Zukuri.png|thumb|Plan of a gongen-zukuri shrine]] {{main|Ishi-no-ma-zukuri}} {{nihongo|''Gongen-zukuri''|権現造}} is the name of a complex [[Shinto shrine]] structure in which the ''[[haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'', or worship hall, and the [[honden]], or main sanctuary, are interconnected under the same roof in the shape of an H.<ref name="sos2">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_g.html#gongen-zukuri ''Gongen-zukuri''] accessed on October 5, 2008</ref><ref>For details about these terms, see the article [[Shinto shrine]].</ref> One of the oldest examples of ''gongen-zukuri'' is Kitano Tenmangū in [[Kyoto]].<ref name="sos2"/> The name comes from Nikkō Tōshō-gū in Nikkō because it enshrines the Tōshō Daigongen and adopts this structure.<ref name="Jaa">Jaanus, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/g/gongenzukuri.htm Gongen-zukuri], accessed on October 5, 2008</ref>
== See also == * The [[Glossary of Shinto]] for an explanation of terms concerning Japanese Shinto, Shinto art, and Shinto shrine architecture

{{出典の明記|date=2017年8月28日 (月) 05:23 (UTC)}} [[File:Gate-nikko-japan.jpg|thumb|right|300 px|[[日光東照宮]]。[[徳川家康]]を神格化した東照大権現（本地は薬師如来）を祀る。]]
'''権現'''（ごんげん）は、日本の神の[[神号]]の一つ。[[日本]]の[[神 (神道)|神々]]を[[仏教]]の[[仏]]や[[菩薩]]が「仮の姿」で現れたものとする[[本地垂迹]]思想による神号である。'''権'''という文字は「[[権官|権大納言]]」などと同じく「臨時の」「仮の」という意味で、仏が「仮に」神の形を取って「'''現'''れた」ことを示す。
[[インド]]の[[バラモン教]]や[[ヒンドゥー教]]の神々は「天」という神号で[[護法善神]]として[[大乗仏教]]の[[天部]]に取り入れられたが、日本の神々が日本仏教に取り入れられた際には'''本地垂迹'''思想に基づき'''権現'''という神号が多く用いられた<ref group="注釈">「権現」以外の例として（実質的には権現であるが）「八幡大菩薩」や「高良大菩薩」など菩薩号を用いることもある。</ref>。権現には[[山王神道]]（[[天台宗]]）・[[両部神道]]（[[真言宗]]）に基づくものや、[[自然崇拝]]（[[山岳信仰]]）と[[修験道]]が融合したもの等があり、[[民間信仰]]においては地域の名士や不慮の死を遂げた人物などが死後に権現として祀られる例も見られる。なお、「[[明神]]号」と「権現号」が区別されずに使用される例も少なくはない。（[[愛宕権現]]＝愛宕明神、[[白山権現]]＝白山明神など）
== 神号の事例 == [[春日大社]]は[[タケミカヅチ|武甕槌命]]（一殿）・[[経津主神]]（二殿）・[[天児屋命]]（三殿）・[[比売神]]（四殿）の四柱の祭神を祀るが、[[中世]]には[[興福寺]]が[[神宮寺]]として支配的な影響力を持ったため、[[神仏習合]]に基づいて[[不空羂索観音]]（一殿）・[[薬師如来]]（二殿）・[[地蔵菩薩]]（三殿）・[[十一面観音]]（四殿）をそれぞれの本地仏とし、社にちなんで「[[春日権現]]」（もしくは[[春日神|春日明神]]）と総称した。また、複数の祭神を祀っている社では「六所権現」などと呼ぶこともあり、この場合は複数の神の総称である。神号との組み合わせにより「[[日光二荒山神社|日光三所権現]]」、「[[熊野権現|熊野十二所権現]]」などのように称される。全国的に最も多く見られるのは「[[白山権現]]」、「[[立山権現]]」、「[[羽黒権現]]」などのように、「山名（もしくは地名）＋権現号」という組み合わせである。その他、少数ではあるが「[[摩利支天]]権現」、「[[薬師如来|薬師]]権現」、「[[釈迦]]権現」のように本地仏の名前がそのまま用いられる例や、「天妃媽祖権現」（[[媽祖]]）、「徐福権現」（[[徐福]]）、「将門権現」（[[平将門]]）などのように神名や人名を用いた例、「[[猫又]]権現」、「大蛇権現」などのように動物由来の例も見られる。以上のことは明神号にも多く共通する。
== 神仏分離・廃仏毀釈 == [[明治維新]]の[[神仏分離令]]（神仏判然令）で権現社・権現宮・権現堂の多くが廃され、「権現」の神号や修験道が一時禁止されたため、権現を祀る神社の多くは本地仏を廃して祭神（垂迹神）のみを祀るようになった。祭神が不詳な権現には[[復古神道]]・[[国学]]の学説に基づき、記紀神話の神を比定した。
== Major Gongen == {{seealso|Japanese Buddhist Deities#Suijaku (Manifestation) Kami|ja|日本の仏尊#垂迹神}}
* {{ill|Atago Gongen|ja|愛宕権現}} ([[愛宕権現]]) * {{ill|Akiba Gongen|ja|秋葉権現}} ([[秋葉権現]]) * {{ill|Ishizuchi Daigongen|ja|石鎚大権現}} ([[石鎚大権現]]) * {{ill|Iizuna Gongen|ja|飯縄権現}} ([[飯縄権現]]) * {{ill|Ochi Daigongen|ja|越知大権現}} ([[越知大権現]]) – Hakusan Shugendō ([[白山修験]]) * {{ill|Kumano Gongen|ja|熊野権現}} ([[熊野権現]]) * {{ill|Konpira Gongen|ja|金毘羅権現}} ([[金毘羅権現]]) * {{ill|Sanki Daigongen|ja|三鬼大権現}} ([[三鬼大権現]]) * {{ill|Zaō Gongen|ja|蔵王権現}} ([[蔵王権現]]) * {{ill|Suzuka Gongen|ja|片山神社 (亀山市)|lt=Suzuka Gongen}} ([[片山神社 (亀山市)|鈴鹿権現]]) – {{ill|Shamanism|ja|巫覡}} ([[巫覡]]) * {{ill|Seiryū Gongen|ja|清瀧権現}} ([[清瀧権現]]) * {{ill|Tateyama Gongen|ja|立山権現}} ([[立山権現]]) – Tateyama Shugendō ([[立山修験]]) * {{ill|Chimyō Gongen|ja|智明権現}} ([[智明権現]]) * {{ill|Nezu Gongen|ja|根津神社#歴史|lt=Nezu Gongen}} ([[根津神社#歴史|根津権現]]) * {{ill|Hakusan Gongen|ja|白山権現}} ([[白山権現]]) – Hakusan Shugendō ([[白山修験]]) * {{ill|Haguro Gongen|ja|羽黒権現}} ([[羽黒権現]]) – Haguro Shugendō ([[羽黒修験]]) * {{ill|Hakone Gongen|ja|箱根権現}} ([[箱根権現]]) * {{ill|Hachiōji Gongen|ja|八王子権現|1=Hachiōji Gongen}} ([[八王子権現]]) * {{ill|Hirō Gongen|ja|飛瀧権現}} ([[飛瀧権現]]) * {{ill|Sannō Gongen|ja|山王権現|lt=Hiyoshi Sannō Gongen}} ([[山王権現|日吉山王権現]]) – {{ill|Sannō Shintō|ja|山王神道}} ([[山王神道]]) * {{ill|Futarasan Gongen|ja|二荒権現}} ([[二荒権現]]) – Nikkō Shugendō ([[日光修験]]) * {{ill|Yuga Daigongen|ja|瑜伽大権現}} ([[瑜伽大権現]]) * {{ill|Hikosan Gongen|ja|英彦山|lt=Hikosan Gongen}} ([[英彦山|英彦山権現]]) * {{ill|Shiramine Daigongen|ja|白峯大権現}} ([[白峯大権現]])

== 外部リンク == *{{Commonscat-inline}}
{{Buddhism2}} {{神道 横}} {{buddhism-stub}} {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:こんけん}} [[Category:Gongen]][[Category:Gongen]][[Category:神仏習合]] [[Category:Shinto-Buddhist syncretism]] [[Category:Gongen]][[Category:修験道]] [[Category:Shugendo]] [[Category:untranslated pages]][[ja:権現]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[Category:Mass editing to refresh the first new Wikipedia redirects]]
{{Italic title|reason=[[:Category:Japanese words and phrases]]}} [[File:Gate-nikko-japan.jpg|thumb|250px|[[Nikkō Tōshō-gū]] enshrines [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]] under the posthumous name of Tōshō Daigongen]] {{main|Honji suijaku}} A {{nihongo||権現|'''''gongen'''''}}, literally "incarnation", was believed to be the manifestation of a [[Buddhahood|buddha]] in the form of an indigenous [[kami]], an entity who had come to guide the people to [[salvation]], during the era of [[shinbutsu-shūgō]] in premodern Japan.<ref name="SoS">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_g.html#gongen ''Gongen''] accessed on October 5, 2008</ref><ref name="TY87">Tamura (2000:87)</ref> The words {{nihongo||権化|gonge}} and {{nihongo||化現|kegen}} are [[synonym]]s for gongen.<ref name="sos3"/> {{nihongo||権現信仰|Gongen shinkō}} is the term for belief in the existence of gongen.<ref name="sos3"/>
The gongen concept is the cornerstone of the [[honji suijaku]] theory, according to which Buddhist deities choose to appear to the Japanese as native kami in order to save them, which is based on the [[Mahayana|Mahayana Buddhist]] notion of [[upaya]], "expedient means".
== History == [[File:NikkoToriiTablet5127.jpg|left|thumb|Tablet on [[torii]] at [[Nikkō Tōshō-gū]] reads "Tōshō Daigongen" (calligraphy by [[Emperor Go-Mizunoo]])]]It is sometimes assumed that the word ''gongen'' derives from [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]]'s posthumous name (Tōshō Daigongen). However, the term was created and started being used in the middle of the [[Heian period]] in an effort to harmonize Buddhism and [[ethnic religion|indigenous religious practice]] in what is called shinbutsu-shūgō or "[[syncretism]] of kami and buddhas".<ref name="TY87">Tamura (2000:87)</ref> At that time, the assumption that Japanese ''kami'' and buddhas were essentially the same evolved into a theory called {{nihongo|''honji suijaku''|本地垂迹}}, which held that native kami were manifestations or avatars of buddhas, [[bodhisattva]]s and other Buddhist deities.<ref name="TY87"/> The theory gradually spread around the country and the concept of gongen, a dual entity composed of a buddha and a kami, evolved.
Under the influence of [[Tendai|Tendai Buddhism]] and [[Shugendō]], the gongen concept was adapted to religious beliefs tied to [[Mount Iwaki]], a volcano, so that female kami Kuniyasutamahime became associated with [[Avalokiteśvara]] ''ekadaśamukha'' (Jūichimen Kannon Bosatsu, "Eleven-Faced [[Guanyin]]"), [[Ōkuninushi]] with [[Bhaisajyaguru]] (Yakushi Nyōrai) and [[Kuninotokotachi]] with [[Amitābha]] (Amida Nyōrai).<ref>Breen, Teeuwen (2000:194)</ref>
The title "gongen" started being attached to the names of kami and shrines were built within the premises of large Buddhist temples to enshrine their tutelary kami.<ref name="TY87">Tamura (2000:87)</ref> During the Japanese Middle Ages, shrines started being called with the name gongen to underline their ties to Buddhism.<ref name="sos3">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=790 Gongen shinkō], accessed on October 5, 2008</ref> For example, in Eastern Japan there are still many [[Mount Haku]] shrines where the shrine itself is called either gongen or jinja.<ref name="sos3"/> Because it represents the application of Buddhist terminology to native kami, the use of the term was legally abolished in the [[Meiji Restoration]] with the {{nihongo|[[Shinbutsu bunri|Shinto and Buddhism Separation Order]]|神仏判然令|Shin-butsu Hanzenrei}} and shrines began to be called ''jinja''.<ref name="sos3"/>
=== Gongen of Japan === * {{nihongo|Izuna Gongen|飯網の権現}}, also called "Izuna Myōjin" and enshrined in Izuna Shrine in [[Nagano, Nagano|Nagano]], is similar to a [[tengu]] and represents the kami of [[Mount Iizuna]].<ref>Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=193 Izuna Gongen], accessed on October 6, 2008</ref> * {{nihongo|Izusan Gongen|伊豆山権現}} or {{nihongo|Hashiri-yu Gongen|走湯権現}} is the spirit of a hot spring on Izusan, a hill in [[Shizuoka Prefecture]], enshrined in the [[Izusan Jinja]]<ref>Jaanus, ''Izusan Gongen'', accessed on October 6, 2008.</ref> * {{nihongo|Kumano Gongen|熊野権現}}, also known as {{nihongo|Three Mountains of Kumano|熊野三山}}.<ref name="kumano">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1076 Kumano Shinkō], accessed on October 6, 2008</ref><ref>[http://www.sekaiisan-wakayama.jp/english/s_sanzan.htm Kumano Sanzan] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080609225919/http://www.sekaiisan-wakayama.jp/english/s_sanzan.htm |date=2008-06-09 }}, World Heritage Registration Association, accessed on October 13, 2008</ref> The kami enshrined in the three Kumano Sanzan Grand Shrines and worshipped in [[Kumano shrine]]s are the three Kumano mountains: Hongū, Shingū, and Nachi.<ref name="kumano"/> * {{nihongo|Seiryū Gongen|清滝権現}} was enshrined in [[Jingo-ji]] in Takao as the tutelary kami of [[Shingon Buddhism]] by [[Kūkai]].<ref>Jaanus, ''Seiryū Gongen'', accessed on October 6, 2008</ref> * {{nihongo|Tōshō Daigongen|東照大権現}} is one of the most famous examples of gongen, representing [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]] posthumously enshrined in so-called [[Tōshō-gū]] shrines present all over Japan. The original one is [[Nikkō Tōshō-gū]] in [[Nikkō, Tochigi]]. * {{nihongo|Zaō Gongen|蔵王権現}} or {{nihongo|Kongō Zaō Bosatsu|金剛蔵王菩薩}} is a deity worshiped in [[Shugendō]].<ref>Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=201 Zaō Gongen], accessed on October 6, 2008</ref> * {{nihongo|Sannō Gongen|山王権現}} or {{nihongo|Hie (alternatively ''Hiyoshi'') Sannō Daigongen|日吉山王大権現}} is a guardian deity worshiped in [[Tendai]] spread from [[Mount Hiei]]. It is treated as a Buddhist title of Shinto [[Oyamakui no Kami]].
== ''Gongen-zukuri'' == [[File:Gongen Zukuri.png|thumb|Plan of a gongen-zukuri shrine]] {{main|Ishi-no-ma-zukuri}} {{nihongo|''Gongen-zukuri''|権現造}} is the name of a complex [[Shinto shrine]] structure in which the ''[[haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'', or worship hall, and the [[honden]], or main sanctuary, are interconnected under the same roof in the shape of an H.<ref name="sos2">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_g.html#gongen-zukuri ''Gongen-zukuri''] accessed on October 5, 2008</ref><ref>For details about these terms, see the article [[Shinto shrine]].</ref> One of the oldest examples of ''gongen-zukuri'' is Kitano Tenmangū in [[Kyoto]].<ref name="sos2"/> The name comes from Nikkō Tōshō-gū in Nikkō because it enshrines the Tōshō Daigongen and adopts this structure.<ref name="Jaa">Jaanus, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/g/gongenzukuri.htm Gongen-zukuri], accessed on October 5, 2008</ref>
== See also == * The [[Glossary of Shinto]] for an explanation of terms concerning Japanese Shinto, Shinto art, and Shinto shrine architecture

revision.text:
{{出典の明記|date=2017年8月28日 (月) 05:23 (UTC)}} [[File:Gate-nikko-japan.jpg|thumb|right|300 px|[[日光東照宮]]。[[徳川家康]]を神格化した東照大権現（本地は薬師如来）を祀る。]]
'''権現'''（ごんげん）は、日本の神の[[神号]]の一つ。[[日本]]の[[神 (神道)|神々]]を[[仏教]]の[[仏]]や[[菩薩]]が「仮の姿」で現れたものとする[[本地垂迹]]思想による神号である。'''権'''という文字は「[[権官|権大納言]]」などと同じく「臨時の」「仮の」という意味で、仏が「仮に」神の形を取って「'''現'''れた」ことを示す。
[[インド]]の[[バラモン教]]や[[ヒンドゥー教]]の神々は「天」という神号で[[護法善神]]として[[大乗仏教]]の[[天部]]に取り入れられたが、日本の神々が日本仏教に取り入れられた際には'''本地垂迹'''思想に基づき'''権現'''という神号が多く用いられた<ref group="注釈">「権現」以外の例として（実質的には権現であるが）「八幡大菩薩」や「高良大菩薩」など菩薩号を用いることもある。</ref>。権現には[[山王神道]]（[[天台宗]]）・[[両部神道]]（[[真言宗]]）に基づくものや、[[自然崇拝]]（[[山岳信仰]]）と[[修験道]]が融合したもの等があり、[[民間信仰]]においては地域の名士や不慮の死を遂げた人物などが死後に権現として祀られる例も見られる。なお、「[[明神]]号」と「権現号」が区別されずに使用される例も少なくはない。（[[愛宕権現]]＝愛宕明神、[[白山権現]]＝白山明神など）
== 神号の事例 == [[春日大社]]は[[タケミカヅチ|武甕槌命]]（一殿）・[[経津主神]]（二殿）・[[天児屋命]]（三殿）・[[比売神]]（四殿）の四柱の祭神を祀るが、[[中世]]には[[興福寺]]が[[神宮寺]]として支配的な影響力を持ったため、[[神仏習合]]に基づいて[[不空羂索観音]]（一殿）・[[薬師如来]]（二殿）・[[地蔵菩薩]]（三殿）・[[十一面観音]]（四殿）をそれぞれの本地仏とし、社にちなんで「[[春日権現]]」（もしくは[[春日神|春日明神]]）と総称した。また、複数の祭神を祀っている社では「六所権現」などと呼ぶこともあり、この場合は複数の神の総称である。神号との組み合わせにより「[[日光二荒山神社|日光三所権現]]」、「[[熊野権現|熊野十二所権現]]」などのように称される。全国的に最も多く見られるのは「[[白山権現]]」、「[[立山権現]]」、「[[羽黒権現]]」などのように、「山名（もしくは地名）＋権現号」という組み合わせである。その他、少数ではあるが「[[摩利支天]]権現」、「[[薬師如来|薬師]]権現」、「[[釈迦]]権現」のように本地仏の名前がそのまま用いられる例や、「天妃媽祖権現」（[[媽祖]]）、「徐福権現」（[[徐福]]）、「将門権現」（[[平将門]]）などのように神名や人名を用いた例、「[[猫又]]権現」、「大蛇権現」などのように動物由来の例も見られる。以上のことは明神号にも多く共通する。
== 神仏分離・廃仏毀釈 == [[明治維新]]の[[神仏分離令]]（神仏判然令）で権現社・権現宮・権現堂の多くが廃され、「権現」の神号や修験道が一時禁止されたため、権現を祀る神社の多くは本地仏を廃して祭神（垂迹神）のみを祀るようになった。祭神が不詳な権現には[[復古神道]]・[[国学]]の学説に基づき、記紀神話の神を比定した。
== Major Gongen == {{seealso|Japanese Buddhist Deities#Suijaku (Manifestation) Kami|ja|日本の仏尊#垂迹神}}

== 外部リンク == *{{Commonscat-inline}}
{{Buddhism2}} {{神道 横}} {{buddhism-stub}} {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:こんけん}} [[Category:Gongen]][[Category:Gongen]][[Category:神仏習合]] [[Category:Shinto-Buddhist syncretism]] [[Category:Gongen]][[Category:修験道]] [[Category:Shugendo]] [[Category:untranslated pages]][[ja:権現]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[Category:Mass editing to refresh the first new Wikipedia redirects]]
{{Italic title|reason=[[:Category:Japanese words and phrases]]}} [[File:Gate-nikko-japan.jpg|thumb|250px|[[Nikkō Tōshō-gū]] enshrines [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]] under the posthumous name of Tōshō Daigongen]] {{main|Honji suijaku}} A {{nihongo||権現|'''''gongen'''''}}, literally "incarnation", was believed to be the manifestation of a [[Buddhahood|buddha]] in the form of an indigenous [[kami]], an entity who had come to guide the people to [[salvation]], during the era of [[shinbutsu-shūgō]] in premodern Japan.<ref name="SoS">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_g.html#gongen ''Gongen''] accessed on October 5, 2008</ref><ref name="TY87">Tamura (2000:87)</ref> The words {{nihongo||権化|gonge}} and {{nihongo||化現|kegen}} are [[synonym]]s for gongen.<ref name="sos3"/> {{nihongo||権現信仰|Gongen shinkō}} is the term for belief in the existence of gongen.<ref name="sos3"/>
The gongen concept is the cornerstone of the [[honji suijaku]] theory, according to which Buddhist deities choose to appear to the Japanese as native kami in order to save them, which is based on the [[Mahayana|Mahayana Buddhist]] notion of [[upaya]], "expedient means".
== History == [[File:NikkoToriiTablet5127.jpg|left|thumb|Tablet on [[torii]] at [[Nikkō Tōshō-gū]] reads "Tōshō Daigongen" (calligraphy by [[Emperor Go-Mizunoo]])]]It is sometimes assumed that the word ''gongen'' derives from [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]]'s posthumous name (Tōshō Daigongen). However, the term was created and started being used in the middle of the [[Heian period]] in an effort to harmonize Buddhism and [[ethnic religion|indigenous religious practice]] in what is called shinbutsu-shūgō or "[[syncretism]] of kami and buddhas".<ref name="TY87">Tamura (2000:87)</ref> At that time, the assumption that Japanese ''kami'' and buddhas were essentially the same evolved into a theory called {{nihongo|''honji suijaku''|本地垂迹}}, which held that native kami were manifestations or avatars of buddhas, [[bodhisattva]]s and other Buddhist deities.<ref name="TY87"/> The theory gradually spread around the country and the concept of gongen, a dual entity composed of a buddha and a kami, evolved.
Under the influence of [[Tendai|Tendai Buddhism]] and [[Shugendō]], the gongen concept was adapted to religious beliefs tied to [[Mount Iwaki]], a volcano, so that female kami Kuniyasutamahime became associated with [[Avalokiteśvara]] ''ekadaśamukha'' (Jūichimen Kannon Bosatsu, "Eleven-Faced [[Guanyin]]"), [[Ōkuninushi]] with [[Bhaisajyaguru]] (Yakushi Nyōrai) and [[Kuninotokotachi]] with [[Amitābha]] (Amida Nyōrai).<ref>Breen, Teeuwen (2000:194)</ref>
The title "gongen" started being attached to the names of kami and shrines were built within the premises of large Buddhist temples to enshrine their tutelary kami.<ref name="TY87">Tamura (2000:87)</ref> During the Japanese Middle Ages, shrines started being called with the name gongen to underline their ties to Buddhism.<ref name="sos3">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=790 Gongen shinkō], accessed on October 5, 2008</ref> For example, in Eastern Japan there are still many [[Mount Haku]] shrines where the shrine itself is called either gongen or jinja.<ref name="sos3"/> Because it represents the application of Buddhist terminology to native kami, the use of the term was legally abolished in the [[Meiji Restoration]] with the {{nihongo|[[Shinbutsu bunri|Shinto and Buddhism Separation Order]]|神仏判然令|Shin-butsu Hanzenrei}} and shrines began to be called ''jinja''.<ref name="sos3"/>
=== Gongen of Japan === * {{nihongo|Izuna Gongen|飯網の権現}}, also called "Izuna Myōjin" and enshrined in Izuna Shrine in [[Nagano, Nagano|Nagano]], is similar to a [[tengu]] and represents the kami of [[Mount Iizuna]].<ref>Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=193 Izuna Gongen], accessed on October 6, 2008</ref> * {{nihongo|Izusan Gongen|伊豆山権現}} or {{nihongo|Hashiri-yu Gongen|走湯権現}} is the spirit of a hot spring on Izusan, a hill in [[Shizuoka Prefecture]], enshrined in the [[Izusan Jinja]]<ref>Jaanus, ''Izusan Gongen'', accessed on October 6, 2008.</ref> * {{nihongo|Kumano Gongen|熊野権現}}, also known as {{nihongo|Three Mountains of Kumano|熊野三山}}.<ref name="kumano">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1076 Kumano Shinkō], accessed on October 6, 2008</ref><ref>[http://www.sekaiisan-wakayama.jp/english/s_sanzan.htm Kumano Sanzan] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080609225919/http://www.sekaiisan-wakayama.jp/english/s_sanzan.htm |date=2008-06-09 }}, World Heritage Registration Association, accessed on October 13, 2008</ref> The kami enshrined in the three Kumano Sanzan Grand Shrines and worshipped in [[Kumano shrine]]s are the three Kumano mountains: Hongū, Shingū, and Nachi.<ref name="kumano"/> * {{nihongo|Seiryū Gongen|清滝権現}} was enshrined in [[Jingo-ji]] in Takao as the tutelary kami of [[Shingon Buddhism]] by [[Kūkai]].<ref>Jaanus, ''Seiryū Gongen'', accessed on October 6, 2008</ref> * {{nihongo|Tōshō Daigongen|東照大権現}} is one of the most famous examples of gongen, representing [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]] posthumously enshrined in so-called [[Tōshō-gū]] shrines present all over Japan. The original one is [[Nikkō Tōshō-gū]] in [[Nikkō, Tochigi]]. * {{nihongo|Zaō Gongen|蔵王権現}} or {{nihongo|Kongō Zaō Bosatsu|金剛蔵王菩薩}} is a deity worshiped in [[Shugendō]].<ref>Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=201 Zaō Gongen], accessed on October 6, 2008</ref> * {{nihongo|Sannō Gongen|山王権現}} or {{nihongo|Hie (alternatively ''Hiyoshi'') Sannō Daigongen|日吉山王大権現}} is a guardian deity worshiped in [[Tendai]] spread from [[Mount Hiei]]. It is treated as a Buddhist title of Shinto [[Oyamakui no Kami]].
== ''Gongen-zukuri'' == [[File:Gongen Zukuri.png|thumb|Plan of a gongen-zukuri shrine]] {{main|Ishi-no-ma-zukuri}} {{nihongo|''Gongen-zukuri''|権現造}} is the name of a complex [[Shinto shrine]] structure in which the ''[[haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'', or worship hall, and the [[honden]], or main sanctuary, are interconnected under the same roof in the shape of an H.<ref name="sos2">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_g.html#gongen-zukuri ''Gongen-zukuri''] accessed on October 5, 2008</ref><ref>For details about these terms, see the article [[Shinto shrine]].</ref> One of the oldest examples of ''gongen-zukuri'' is Kitano Tenmangū in [[Kyoto]].<ref name="sos2"/> The name comes from Nikkō Tōshō-gū in Nikkō because it enshrines the Tōshō Daigongen and adopts this structure.<ref name="Jaa">Jaanus, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/g/gongenzukuri.htm Gongen-zukuri], accessed on October 5, 2008</ref>

{{出典の明記|date=2017年8月28日 (月) 05:23 (UTC)}} [[File:Gate-nikko-japan.jpg|thumb|right|300 px|[[日光東照宮]]。[[徳川家康]]を神格化した東照大権現（本地は薬師如来）を祀る。]]
'''権現'''（ごんげん）は、日本の神の[[神号]]の一つ。[[日本]]の[[神 (神道)|神々]]を[[仏教]]の[[仏]]や[[菩薩]]が「仮の姿」で現れたものとする[[本地垂迹]]思想による神号である。'''権'''という文字は「[[権官|権大納言]]」などと同じく「臨時の」「仮の」という意味で、仏が「仮に」神の形を取って「'''現'''れた」ことを示す。
[[インド]]の[[バラモン教]]や[[ヒンドゥー教]]の神々は「天」という神号で[[護法善神]]として[[大乗仏教]]の[[天部]]に取り入れられたが、日本の神々が日本仏教に取り入れられた際には'''本地垂迹'''思想に基づき'''権現'''という神号が多く用いられた<ref group="注釈">「権現」以外の例として（実質的には権現であるが）「八幡大菩薩」や「高良大菩薩」など菩薩号を用いることもある。</ref>。権現には[[山王神道]]（[[天台宗]]）・[[両部神道]]（[[真言宗]]）に基づくものや、[[自然崇拝]]（[[山岳信仰]]）と[[修験道]]が融合したもの等があり、[[民間信仰]]においては地域の名士や不慮の死を遂げた人物などが死後に権現として祀られる例も見られる。なお、「[[明神]]号」と「権現号」が区別されずに使用される例も少なくはない。（[[愛宕権現]]＝愛宕明神、[[白山権現]]＝白山明神など）
== 神号の事例 == [[春日大社]]は[[タケミカヅチ|武甕槌命]]（一殿）・[[経津主神]]（二殿）・[[天児屋命]]（三殿）・[[比売神]]（四殿）の四柱の祭神を祀るが、[[中世]]には[[興福寺]]が[[神宮寺]]として支配的な影響力を持ったため、[[神仏習合]]に基づいて[[不空羂索観音]]（一殿）・[[薬師如来]]（二殿）・[[地蔵菩薩]]（三殿）・[[十一面観音]]（四殿）をそれぞれの本地仏とし、社にちなんで「[[春日権現]]」（もしくは[[春日神|春日明神]]）と総称した。また、複数の祭神を祀っている社では「六所権現」などと呼ぶこともあり、この場合は複数の神の総称である。神号との組み合わせにより「[[日光二荒山神社|日光三所権現]]」、「[[熊野権現|熊野十二所権現]]」などのように称される。全国的に最も多く見られるのは「[[白山権現]]」、「[[立山権現]]」、「[[羽黒権現]]」などのように、「山名（もしくは地名）＋権現号」という組み合わせである。その他、少数ではあるが「[[摩利支天]]権現」、「[[薬師如来|薬師]]権現」、「[[釈迦]]権現」のように本地仏の名前がそのまま用いられる例や、「天妃媽祖権現」（[[媽祖]]）、「徐福権現」（[[徐福]]）、「将門権現」（[[平将門]]）などのように神名や人名を用いた例、「[[猫又]]権現」、「大蛇権現」などのように動物由来の例も見られる。以上のことは明神号にも多く共通する。
== 神仏分離・廃仏毀釈 == [[明治維新]]の[[神仏分離令]]（神仏判然令）で権現社・権現宮・権現堂の多くが廃され、「権現」の神号や修験道が一時禁止されたため、権現を祀る神社の多くは本地仏を廃して祭神（垂迹神）のみを祀るようになった。祭神が不詳な権現には[[復古神道]]・[[国学]]の学説に基づき、記紀神話の神を比定した。

{{Buddhism2}} {{神道 横}} {{buddhism-stub}} {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:こんけん}} [[Category:Gongen]][[Category:Gongen]][[Category:Gongen]] [[Category:Gongen]] [[Category:Gongen]][[Category:Gongen]] [[Category:Gongen]] [[Category:Gongen]][[Category:Gongen]] [[Category:Gongen]] [[Category:Gongen]]
{{Italic title|reason=[[:Category:Japanese words and phrases]]}} [[File:Gate-nikko-japan.jpg|thumb|250px|[[Nikkō Tōshō-gū]] enshrines [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]] under the posthumous name of Tōshō Daigongen]] {{main|Honji suijaku}} A {{nihongo||権現|'''''gongen'''''}}, literally "incarnation", was believed to be the manifestation of a [[Buddhahood|buddha]] in the form of an indigenous [[kami]], an entity who had come to guide the people to [[salvation]], during the era of [[shinbutsu-shūgō]] in premodern Japan.<ref name="SoS">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_g.html#gongen ''Gongen''] accessed on October 5, 2008</ref><ref name="TY87">Tamura (2000:87)</ref> The words {{nihongo||権化|gonge}} and {{nihongo||化現|kegen}} are [[synonym]]s for gongen.<ref name="sos3"/> {{nihongo||権現信仰|Gongen shinkō}} is the term for belief in the existence of gongen.<ref name="sos3"/>
The gongen concept is the cornerstone of the [[honji suijaku]] theory, according to which Buddhist deities choose to appear to the Japanese as native kami in order to save them, which is based on the [[Mahayana|Mahayana Buddhist]] notion of [[upaya]], "expedient means".
== History == [[File:NikkoToriiTablet5127.jpg|left|thumb|Tablet on [[torii]] at [[Nikkō Tōshō-gū]] reads "Tōshō Daigongen" (calligraphy by [[Emperor Go-Mizunoo]])]]It is sometimes assumed that the word ''gongen'' derives from [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]]'s posthumous name (Tōshō Daigongen). However, the term was created and started being used in the middle of the [[Heian period]] in an effort to harmonize Buddhism and [[ethnic religion|indigenous religious practice]] in what is called shinbutsu-shūgō or "[[syncretism]] of kami and buddhas".<ref name="TY87">Tamura (2000:87)</ref> At that time, the assumption that Japanese ''kami'' and buddhas were essentially the same evolved into a theory called {{nihongo|''honji suijaku''|本地垂迹}}, which held that native kami were manifestations or avatars of buddhas, [[bodhisattva]]s and other Buddhist deities.<ref name="TY87"/> The theory gradually spread around the country and the concept of gongen, a dual entity composed of a buddha and a kami, evolved.
Under the influence of [[Tendai|Tendai Buddhism]] and [[Shugendō]], the gongen concept was adapted to religious beliefs tied to [[Mount Iwaki]], a volcano, so that female kami Kuniyasutamahime became associated with [[Avalokiteśvara]] ''ekadaśamukha'' (Jūichimen Kannon Bosatsu, "Eleven-Faced [[Guanyin]]"), [[Ōkuninushi]] with [[Bhaisajyaguru]] (Yakushi Nyōrai) and [[Kuninotokotachi]] with [[Amitābha]] (Amida Nyōrai).<ref>Breen, Teeuwen (2000:194)</ref>
The title "gongen" started being attached to the names of kami and shrines were built within the premises of large Buddhist temples to enshrine their tutelary kami.<ref name="TY87">Tamura (2000:87)</ref> During the Japanese Middle Ages, shrines started being called with the name gongen to underline their ties to Buddhism.<ref name="sos3">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=790 Gongen shinkō], accessed on October 5, 2008</ref> For example, in Eastern Japan there are still many [[Mount Haku]] shrines where the shrine itself is called either gongen or jinja.<ref name="sos3"/> Because it represents the application of Buddhist terminology to native kami, the use of the term was legally abolished in the [[Meiji Restoration]] with the {{nihongo|[[Shinbutsu bunri|Shinto and Buddhism Separation Order]]|神仏判然令|Shin-butsu Hanzenrei}} and shrines began to be called ''jinja''.<ref name="sos3"/>
=== Gongen of Japan === * {{nihongo|Izuna Gongen|飯網の権現}}, also called "Izuna Myōjin" and enshrined in Izuna Shrine in [[Nagano, Nagano|Nagano]], is similar to a [[tengu]] and represents the kami of [[Mount Iizuna]].<ref>Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=193 Izuna Gongen], accessed on October 6, 2008</ref> * {{nihongo|Izusan Gongen|伊豆山権現}} or {{nihongo|Hashiri-yu Gongen|走湯権現}} is the spirit of a hot spring on Izusan, a hill in [[Shizuoka Prefecture]], enshrined in the [[Izusan Jinja]]<ref>Jaanus, ''Izusan Gongen'', accessed on October 6, 2008.</ref> * {{nihongo|Kumano Gongen|熊野権現}}, also known as {{nihongo|Three Mountains of Kumano|熊野三山}}.<ref name="kumano">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1076 Kumano Shinkō], accessed on October 6, 2008</ref><ref>[http://www.sekaiisan-wakayama.jp/english/s_sanzan.htm Kumano Sanzan] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080609225919/http://www.sekaiisan-wakayama.jp/english/s_sanzan.htm |date=2008-06-09 }}, World Heritage Registration Association, accessed on October 13, 2008</ref> The kami enshrined in the three Kumano Sanzan Grand Shrines and worshipped in [[Kumano shrine]]s are the three Kumano mountains: Hongū, Shingū, and Nachi.<ref name="kumano"/> * {{nihongo|Seiryū Gongen|清滝権現}} was enshrined in [[Jingo-ji]] in Takao as the tutelary kami of [[Shingon Buddhism]] by [[Kūkai]].<ref>Jaanus, ''Seiryū Gongen'', accessed on October 6, 2008</ref> * {{nihongo|Tōshō Daigongen|東照大権現}} is one of the most famous examples of gongen, representing [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]] posthumously enshrined in so-called [[Tōshō-gū]] shrines present all over Japan. The original one is [[Nikkō Tōshō-gū]] in [[Nikkō, Tochigi]]. * {{nihongo|Zaō Gongen|蔵王権現}} or {{nihongo|Kongō Zaō Bosatsu|金剛蔵王菩薩}} is a deity worshiped in [[Shugendō]].<ref>Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=201 Zaō Gongen], accessed on October 6, 2008</ref> * {{nihongo|Sannō Gongen|山王権現}} or {{nihongo|Hie (alternatively ''Hiyoshi'') Sannō Daigongen|日吉山王大権現}} is a guardian deity worshiped in [[Tendai]] spread from [[Mount Hiei]]. It is treated as a Buddhist title of Shinto [[Oyamakui no Kami]].
== ''Gongen-zukuri'' == [[File:Gongen Zukuri.png|thumb|Plan of a gongen-zukuri shrine]] {{main|Ishi-no-ma-zukuri}} {{nihongo|''Gongen-zukuri''|権現造}} is the name of a complex [[Shinto shrine]] structure in which the ''[[haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'', or worship hall, and the [[honden]], or main sanctuary, are interconnected under the same roof in the shape of an H.<ref name="sos2">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_g.html#gongen-zukuri ''Gongen-zukuri''] accessed on October 5, 2008</ref><ref>For details about these terms, see the article [[Shinto shrine]].</ref> One of the oldest examples of ''gongen-zukuri'' is Kitano Tenmangū in [[Kyoto]].<ref name="sos2"/> The name comes from Nikkō Tōshō-gū in Nikkō because it enshrines the Tōshō Daigongen and adopts this structure.<ref name="Jaa">Jaanus, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/g/gongenzukuri.htm Gongen-zukuri], accessed on October 5, 2008</ref>

{{出典の明記|date=2017年8月28日 (月) 05:23 (UTC)}} [[File:Gate-nikko-japan.jpg|thumb|right|300 px|[[日光東照宮]]。[[徳川家康]]を神格化した東照大権現（本地は薬師如来）を祀る。]]
'''権現'''（ごんげん）は、日本の神の[[神号]]の一つ。[[日本]]の[[神 (神道)|神々]]を[[仏教]]の[[仏]]や[[菩薩]]が「仮の姿」で現れたものとする[[本地垂迹]]思想による神号である。'''権'''という文字は「[[権官|権大納言]]」などと同じく「臨時の」「仮の」という意味で、仏が「仮に」神の形を取って「'''現'''れた」ことを示す。
[[インド]]の[[バラモン教]]や[[ヒンドゥー教]]の神々は「天」という神号で[[護法善神]]として[[大乗仏教]]の[[天部]]に取り入れられたが、日本の神々が日本仏教に取り入れられた際には'''本地垂迹'''思想に基づき'''権現'''という神号が多く用いられた<ref group="注釈">「権現」以外の例として（実質的には権現であるが）「八幡大菩薩」や「高良大菩薩」など菩薩号を用いることもある。</ref>。権現には[[山王神道]]（[[天台宗]]）・[[両部神道]]（[[真言宗]]）に基づくものや、[[自然崇拝]]（[[山岳信仰]]）と[[修験道]]が融合したもの等があり、[[民間信仰]]においては地域の名士や不慮の死を遂げた人物などが死後に権現として祀られる例も見られる。なお、「[[明神]]号」と「権現号」が区別されずに使用される例も少なくはない。（[[愛宕権現]]＝愛宕明神、[[白山権現]]＝白山明神など）
== 神号の事例 == [[春日大社]]は[[タケミカヅチ|武甕槌命]]（一殿）・[[経津主神]]（二殿）・[[天児屋命]]（三殿）・[[比売神]]（四殿）の四柱の祭神を祀るが、[[中世]]には[[興福寺]]が[[神宮寺]]として支配的な影響力を持ったため、[[神仏習合]]に基づいて[[不空羂索観音]]（一殿）・[[薬師如来]]（二殿）・[[地蔵菩薩]]（三殿）・[[十一面観音]]（四殿）をそれぞれの本地仏とし、社にちなんで「[[春日権現]]」（もしくは[[春日神|春日明神]]）と総称した。また、複数の祭神を祀っている社では「六所権現」などと呼ぶこともあり、この場合は複数の神の総称である。神号との組み合わせにより「[[日光二荒山神社|日光三所権現]]」、「[[熊野権現|熊野十二所権現]]」などのように称される。全国的に最も多く見られるのは「[[白山権現]]」、「[[立山権現]]」、「[[羽黒権現]]」などのように、「山名（もしくは地名）＋権現号」という組み合わせである。その他、少数ではあるが「[[摩利支天]]権現」、「[[薬師如来|薬師]]権現」、「[[釈迦]]権現」のように本地仏の名前がそのまま用いられる例や、「天妃媽祖権現」（[[媽祖]]）、「徐福権現」（[[徐福]]）、「将門権現」（[[平将門]]）などのように神名や人名を用いた例、「[[猫又]]権現」、「大蛇権現」などのように動物由来の例も見られる。以上のことは明神号にも多く共通する。
== 神仏分離・廃仏毀釈 == [[明治維新]]の[[神仏分離令]]（神仏判然令）で権現社・権現宮・権現堂の多くが廃され、「権現」の神号や修験道が一時禁止されたため、権現を祀る神社の多くは本地仏を廃して祭神（垂迹神）のみを祀るようになった。祭神が不詳な権現には[[復古神道]]・[[国学]]の学説に基づき、記紀神話の神を比定した。
